abstract: This article presents an integrated differential single-pole double-throw (SPDT) switch implemented in IHP SG13G3 technology, which offers heterojunction bipolar transistors with f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">t</inf>/f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> of 470/650 GHz. The chosen topology uses parallel shunt transistors in reverse saturation configuration for minimizing the insertion loss. The scattering parameters are measured in the WR3.4 band between 220 and 320 GHz. A minimal insertion loss of 3.2 dB at 285 GHz along with 1 dB bandwidth of 100 GHz are reached. The isolation from the input to the unselected output is better than 14 dB within the entire bandwidth. The switch occupies a chip core area of 165µm × 170µm and the chip consumes 4.8 mW. To the best knowledge of the authors, the circuit achieves the lowest insertion loss and the highest 1dB bandwidth among the switches operating above 200 GHz in silicon technologies, while being the only switch supporting differential signalling.

title: Incremental Rough-PCA Integrated Framework for Adaptive Malware Detection and Classification
authors: Sasmita Pradhan, Subhasis Dash, Arup Abhinna Acharya
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Malware's rapid evolution poses major challenges to cybersecurity and the reliability of computing infrastructures. This study presents a scalable and adaptive framework to enhance malware detection and classification accuracy. The proposed model integrates Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) approaches for binary and multiclass classification, leveraging three primary categories of data sources derived from memory dumps, system monitoring patterns and PE malwares. A hybrid approach of Rough Set Theory (RST), combined with Principal Component Analysis (PCA), is employed for feature selection and dimensionality reduction, yielding compact and discriminative feature representations. An incremental learning mechanism enables continuous model adaptation to new malware samples without complete retraining. Experimental evaluation on four types of experimental datasets demonstrates that the proposed hybrid approach achieves impressive reduction in feature dimensionality while preserving essential information. The DL-based model attained a detection accuracy of 99.60 %, and the ML-based model achieved 99.89 %. Additionally, the framework identified new malware variants and classified 19 attack categories with an average accuracy of 90.72 %. The integration of incremental learning with RST-based feature selection establishes a novel, efficient and continually adaptive approach for real-world malware classification.

title: An Automatic Knowledge Extraction Method for Penetration Testing Based on Transfer Learning
authors: Jie Liu, Bingjun He, Bingwen Wang, Haohan Ma
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: With the continuous evolution of cyberattack techniques, traditional penetration testing methods face challenges in efficiency and accuracy. To improve the automation level and knowledge extraction accuracy of penetration testing, this paper proposes an automated knowledge extraction method for penetration testing cases based on transfer learning. Transfer learning transfers knowledge from models trained in the source domain (such as CVE, MITRE ATT&CK) to the target domain (penetration testing blogs and reports, etc.), thus compensating for the lack of labeled data in the target domain. This improves the accuracy of the model in entity recognition and relation extraction tasks, even when structured samples are scarce in the target domain. In the experimental phase, the proposed transfer learning model is compared with baseline models (rule-based model and shallow machine learning model). The identified penetration testing-related entities and relations are used to complete the knowledge graph, thus achieving attack path coverage experiments. Experimental results show that the proposed transfer learning-based method has significant advantages in knowledge extraction tasks and can effectively improve the attack path coverage of the knowledge graph.

title: FCMF-UNetFormer: A Full-Connected Multi-Scale Fused UNetFormer for Urban Scene Parsing
authors: Zhuo Liu, Yanxi Jia, Chuang Zhang, Jingchun Cheng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: With the rapid growth of high-resolution remote sensing imagery, fine-grained urban scene parsing faces increasing challenges such as diverse object categories, large scale variations, and complex boundaries. Towards this issue, traditional CNNs are limited by local receptive fields; while Transformers which are, strong in global modeling struggle with hierarchical feature fusion. In this paper, we propose the FCMF-UNetFormer for urban scene parsing, a fully connected multi-scale fusion network which solves the semantic gap between shallow and deep features of transformer. In specific, we introduce a fully connected skip structure to enable progressive multi-scale interaction, a MultiSkipFusion module for continuous deep–shallow semantic integration, and a Proportional Skip Fusion (PSF) module that adaptively aligns and fuses features through learnable proportional weights. Experiments on the Vaihingen and LoveDA datasets demonstrate that the FCMF-UNetFormer achieves notable improvements over its baseline (i.e. UNetFormer) and outperforms recent state-of-the-art models in mIoU, F1-score, boundary quality, and overall accuracy, showing strong robustness and generalization.

title: vPID: An Enhanced PID Controller with Variation Model Filter for Robust Motion System Control
authors: Abdul Rahman Abdul Majid
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: This paper presents variation-based proportional–integral–derivative (vPID) control strategy for robust motion system regulation. The proposed approach enhances conventional PID control by integrating a Variation Model Filter (VMF) that estimates and compensates for bias terms arising from unmatched disturbances, slow-varying uncertainties, and unmodeled nonlinearities. Unlike modelbased methods such as Disturbance Observer-Based Control (DOBC) or semi-model based such as Active Disturbance Rejection Control (ADRC), vPID is fully model-free, easy to implement, and requires minimal tuning. The VMF operates directly on measurable system variations to generate a bias estimate, enabling improved disturbance rejection without increasing system complexity. The method is validated through high-fidelity simulations on two representative motion control problems: (i) speed regulation of a brushless DC (BLDC) motor under load disturbances, and (ii) trajectory tracking of a 4-degree-of-freedom (DOF) robotic manipulator under dynamic perturbations. Results demonstrate that vPID achieves faster settling, lower steady-state error, and significantly reduced tracking error compared to conventional PID and disturbance/uncertainty estimation and attenuation (DUEA)based controllers. These findings indicate that vPID offers a practical, cost-effective, and robust alternative for motion control applications in robotics, automation, and precision mechatronics.

title: Analyzing Gender Disparities in STEM Leadership: An Analytical Study of Engineering Institutions in Delhi
authors: Rippandeep Kaur, Ranjeeta Saini, Vandana Aggarwal, Guneet Kaur Cheema, Raj Kumar, Priya Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: This study investigates gender gap in leadership at STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics) institutions of higher learning in Delhi. The study uses the data provided by 127 Head of Departments (HoDs) of 20 engineering colleges and 1293 faculty in the country and examines the variation in work-life balance, level of research outputs, placement activities, University Social Responsibility (USR) and the potential to generate corporate and government funding. Results indicate high variation between genders on a leadership level with males tending to be more productive, work-life balanced and most successful in placement and funding whereas females are found to be involved in more socially related USR actions. The present study highlights the absence of structural reforms and policy interventions to encourage gender equity in the STEM leadership positions.

title: Bridging the Patch Gap: Analyzing Ransomware Exploitation Tactics Over Extended Vulnerability Dwell Times
authors: Olalekan Kosile, Oladimeji Oyegunle
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: This study analyzes the exploitation of unpatched vulnerabilities by ransomware groups CL0P, RansomHub, and Magniber during extended dwell times between public disclosure and remediation from 2020 to 2025, targeting three high-severity vulnerabilities: CVE-2024-0012 (Authentication Bypass in PAN-OS), CVE-2024-9474 (Privilege Escalation in PAN-OS), and CVE-2024-0076 (Remote Code Execution in Cisco Webex). It systematically delineates adversary behavior, overlooked mitigations, and organizational maturity through the integration of the MITRE ATT&CK, D3FEND, and CCMM frameworks. The results indicate that exploitation frequently occurs within 3 to 15 days following disclosure. The frequency of attacks increases significantly when patch delays extend from three to six months, especially in enterprises with lower cybersecurity capability maturity levels, where remediation cycles are slower compared to more mature enterprises. Findings demonstrate that identical high-severity vulnerabilities are consistently exploited to obtain privileged access in ransomware operations. The study provides recommendations for enhancing patch management processes, highlighting the need for more proactive and rapid remediation strategies. It illustrates that delays in patching directly facilitate ransomware operations, highlighting the necessity for a rapid response in today’s cyber defense.

title: Miniaturized 47 GHz Antenna Design for Emerging Millimeter-Wave Technologies
authors: Kusum Kumari, Himanshi Verma, Bhawna Bhawna, Vaishali Kikan, Ashwni Kumar
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Microstrip antennas are increasingly used in high-frequency applications such as the millimeter-wave range of the electromagnetic spectrum. In this paper, we discuss the design of a small, symmetrical microstrip antenna for use in the V band at 47 GHz. The antenna has a modified rectangular patch radiating element with center vias and horizontal slots that provide a more uniform distribution of current on the patch surface, resulting in improved impedance matching and stable radiation patterns. The antenna was built on an RT/Duroid 5880 substrate (a low-loss dielectric material) with a thickness of 0.254 mm. The RT/Duroid substrate is ideal for manufacturing antennas to operate at high frequencies. We used HFSS to simulate the performance of the antenna and found the main resonance frequency of the antenna to be 47.00 GHz, with an input impedance of 49.87 ohms and a maximum return loss of –32.18 dB; this demonstrates very good power transfer to the antenna. The range of frequencies over which the input impedance stays within the limits of the specified conditions is referred to as the bandwidth of the antenna (the frequency range in which the input impedance remains consistent). For this antenna, this range is 45.5 to 48.5 GHz.

title: Short-Term Gains vs. Long-Term Success: Reward Strategy Design for Reinforcement Learning in Football
authors: Mohammad Pashaei, Amirhossein Tayebi, Hadi Amiri, Ali Fahim
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Reinforcement learning in complex games like soccer relies heavily on how you define your reward function and environment. In this work, we developed a custom 3v3 soccer environment and implemented two RL-based teams with distinct learning trends: one with a fast convergence but limited long-term adaptation, and another with a slower yet more robust learning trajectory. Simulation shows that despite performing better at the start, the short-term agents fall short of the performance of the long-term agents in the long run, and after passing 50% of the episodes, the win rate of long-term agents rises from 30% in the beginning to 50%.

title: Enhanced Software Fault Prediction Using Machine Learning
authors: Piyush Bagla, Rohit Kumar, Kuldeep Kumar, K P Sharma, Bhumika Punetha, Radhika Malhotra
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Software fault prediction and proneness continue to be a pressing problem in the sphere of software business since this directly affects the quality of software products and reliability. The conventional techniques to determine software weaknesses rely on very basic knowledge or manual searching of modules that contain defects, and this is a time-consuming task and heavy workload. To address these issues, self-recovery models of software faults have risen to use the ML methodology to identify and recover software defects with more precision. This paper satirizes various software fault prediction models that positively use ML techniques to improve functionality of a system and minimize examples of faults. On these four datasets, we thoroughly analyze these models, which are evaluated with a ten-fold cross-validation and evaluated with respect to precision, recall, F1 measure, and accuracy. Findings The performance in terms of classification is at the highest level as the results show accuracy rates of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$98,99 {\%}$</tex> (along with results of respective F1 scores) where Support Vector Machines (SVM), Naive Bayes (NB), Random Forest (RF) and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) algorithms are used. The results of this research highlights that there is a significant gain in the area of software fault prediction, which is also indicative of the effectiveness of ML tools in promoting predictive reliability and accuracy of systems.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for Detecting Phishing Websites Through URL Feature Analysis and API Integration
authors: Shubhangi Tirpude, Devishree Naidu, Amey R. Kakde, Nikunj M. Gindodiya, Pratham A. Agarwal, Vidit M. Agrawal
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Phishing attacks remain a major and persistent challenge in the field of cybersecurity, as traditional blacklistbased detection methods struggle to keep up with the fast-evolving nature of online threats. This paper presents a comprehensive phishing website detection system powered by machine learning, integrating the XGBoost classification algorithm with external API verification and user-centric transparency features. To enhance detection reliability, the system incorporates real-time feature extraction, Google Safe Browsing API integration, trusted domain management, and transparent result explanations that foster user trust. Additionally, the system offers dual accessibility-through both a web interface and RESTful API-demonstrating its adaptability for individual users as well as enterprise-level integration. Experimental results indicate that the proposed model significantly outperforms conventional detection techniques, especially in identifying zero-day phishing attacks, while maintaining a low false-positive rate. The inclusion of adaptive feedback mechanisms further improves system performance over time. The proposed system leverages a diverse set of discriminative URL and web content features, achieving an accuracy of 86 %, a precision of 91 %, and a recall of 80 % on balanced datasets. Overall, this research contributes a robust, scalable, and user-oriented approach to phishing detection, bridging the gap between academic innovation and real-world cybersecurity applications.

title: Real-Time Cross-Project Software Fault Prediction using Transfer Learning, Online Learning and Auto ML
authors: Deepak Sharma, Pankaj Kumar, Praveen Kumar Rai
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Significant financial losses and a decline in confidence in sectors including banking, healthcare, and self-driving cars are the results of serious issues brought on by software defects. RT-CPFP, a novel framework for real-time defect prediction in various applications, is presented in this paper. It integrates automated machine learning, internet learning, and transfer learning. By addressing the problems of limited data and shifting topic areas, TL facilitates knowledge sharing between source and target activities. OL lessens the chance of concept drift by regulating frequent code changes and updating the model in little steps. By automatically choosing features, models, and settings, AutoML simplifies the process and improves overall efficiency. Using the NASA and PROMISE datasets, RT-CPFP yielded an F1-score of 0. With an area under the curve (AUC) of 0, the score is 86. It outperforms the benchmarks by 812% with a score of 91 and a latency of less than 100 milliseconds. Working with continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) systems is made simple by the framework's architecture, which is depicted in a straightforward picture. Among the contributions are a robust and adaptable architecture, extensive testing using real-world data, and practical DevOps deployment strategies. Distribution inconsistencies, codebase modifications, and human adjustments are all handled by RTCPFP. In today's software development, it offers a fresh approach to address issues before they arise. Agile processes may make extensive use of this strategy.

title: Comparative Assessment of U-Net and Pix2Pix for Applying Direct Attenuation Correction in the Image Domain in ${ }^{68}$ Ga-PSMA PET/CT Imaging
authors: Negin Hamidiyan, Hadi Taleshi Ahangari, Pardis Ghafarian, Hossein Arabi, Mohammad Reza Ay
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Accurate quantitative positron emission tomography (PET) imaging needs effective attenuation correction (AC). This remains a challenge in dedicated PET systems that lack concurrent computed tomography (CT). Recent research has investigated deep learning (DL) methods for AC, but direct comparisons between models are still limited. This study systematically compares the performance of two widely used DL architectures, U-Net and Pix2Pix, for direct AC of whole-body <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{68}$</tex> Ga-PSMA PET images using a consistent set of 95 patient data sets. Both models are evaluated under identical conditions. For each data set, CT-based attenuation-corrected PET (PET-CTAC) was used as the reference. Quantitative evaluation included mean error (ME) of mean of standardized uptake value (SUVmean), normalized root mean square error (NRMSE), structural similarity index (SSIM), and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR). Both U-Net and Pix2Pix generate PET images with visual quality similar to PET-CTAC, but Pix2Pix generally showed better quantitative metrics. Specifically, UNet achieved ME, NRMSE, SSIM, and PSNR values of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.037 \pm 0.02,0.006 \pm 0.005,12.88 \pm 2.73$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.98 \pm 0.14$</tex>, respectively, whereas Pix2Pix achieved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.015 \pm 0.015,0.005 \pm 0.004,13.93 \pm$</tex> 2.48, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.99 \pm 0.004$</tex>. Statistical analyses are conducted using paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, selected based on the normality of the data, demonstrated that Pix2Pix produced SUV estimates closer to those of PET-CTAC, with lower bias and variability than U-Net. In conclusion, both DL models enabled direct AC of whole-body <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{68}$</tex> Ga-PSMA PET, but Pix2Pix provided more accurate and reliable AC when the two models were directly compared, indicating Pix2Pix is the stronger candidate for clinical use in dedicated PET systems without CT imaging.

title: Playmaking: A Task-fMRI Study
authors: Mohammad Rezaei, Mahdi Siami, Asghar Zarei, Alireza Talesh Jafadideh
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: We investigated how large-scale brain networks support creative decision-making in soccer. Thirty-five male players completed the Standardized Video Task in Soccer (SVT), a video-based creativity task during fMRI; professional coaches rated the originality/feasibility of each imagined play. We computed topography-based high-order functional connectivity (tHOFC) between 100 cortical regions during the idea-generation (“Think”) periods and compared the High and Low SVT groups across all edges using a max-statistic permutation test. Global connectivity did not differ between groups, but six edges linking a right peripheral-visual node to dorsal and ventral attention hubs were weaker in the High-SVT group. Across individuals, lower visual-attention connectivity correlated with higher creativity scores (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=-0.52$</tex>, 95% CI −0.76 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-0.17, p=0.006$</tex>; significant after FDR correction at q = 0.05). These results indicate that selective decoupling, not global hyperconnectivity, characterizes creative playmaking and aligns with accounts of internally directed cognition. Methodologically, our connectivity analysis with strict permutation testing reveals subtle, task-locked network reconfigurations in sport cognition.

title: SignSpeak: AI-Powered Real-Time Sign Language Interpretation with Deep Learning and Speech Synthesis
authors: P Steffy Sherly, K Kamatchi, B Netaji Naidu, A Vamsikrishna, D Dheeraj Reddy
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Sign language is adopted by millions of people in the Deaf and Hard of Hearing (DHH) community as their exclusive form of communication. There is nonetheless a barrier to communication between them and the hearing community, and it is a significant hindrance. This paper presents Sign2Speech, a real-time sign language interpreter based on deep learning, pose estimation, and Text To Speech. Our approach utilizes computer vision and natural language processing to construct a universal communication device. An efficient pose estimation model is at the heart of our system capable of identifying expressive hand poses, facial expressions, and body language that are input to sign language. Our system uses a light-weight pose estimation architecture in a bid to provide real-time performance on typical computer hardware. The keypoints from the pose are passed to a temporal deep learning model in the form of a Transformer-based one that has already been trained to identify and translate sequences of signs into text. The Transformer model is particularly capable of modeling long-range context and sign language dependencies. The text is then synthesized into natural- sounding speech using a state-of-the-art Text-to-Speech (Text To Speech) engine. We tested and compared on a large diverse sign language video dataset with good accuracy in sign recognition and good speech output. The experiments show that Sign2Speech is superior to state-of-the-art systems in accuracy as well as in real-time behavior. This research is a significant contribution towards the removal of communication barriers of the DHH population and towards a more inclusive society. The proposed system here can be implemented in different environments, such as deaf and dumb schools, and would make the DHH community more independent and aware of information.

title: Target Probability-guided Multi-UAV Cooperative Search and Mission Planning
authors: Shaocong Ma, Xinglin Chen, Yishuai Cai, Yunxin Mao, Minglong Li
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning, Computational Intelligence and Pattern Recognition (MLCIPR)
abstract: Cooperative mission planning for multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) is a complex multi-agent collaborative decision-making problem. Establishing reasonable mathematical models and selecting appropriate solution algorithms are key to addressing this challenge, involving processes such as environment and mission modeling, optimization objective formulation, and multi-agent solution space exploration. However, existing modeling approaches fail to effectively abstract environmental and mission information: they either propose unrealistic assumptions or establish overly complex models that are difficult to solve. This paper develops a mathematical model for multi-UAV region search problems by utilizing QuadTree to perform non-uniform grid discretization of the environment, obtaining a discrete target probability distribution information map while fully considering various practical constraints. The problem is ultimately solved through genetic algorithm optimization. Simulation results demonstrate that our model can effectively simulate real-world scenarios, and within limited search durations, the optimization algorithm can obtain satisfactory planning solutions, with target detection rates improving as search time increases.

title: Predicting Sleep Efficiency and Apnea Index Using ECG-Derived and Sleep Quality Features: A Machine Learning Approach
authors: Mahla Khodaverdi, Raheleh Davoodi
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Sleep quality and obstructive sleep apnea profoundly influence cardiovascular function, cognition, and overall well-being, yet conventional monitoring approaches remain largely invasive or cumbersome, underscoring the imperative for streamlined, non-invasive alternatives. Herein, we present a machine learning framework that synergistically integrates electrocardiogram (ECG)-derived features with sleep quality metrics to forecast sleep efficiency and apnea-hypopnea index (AHI). Drawing upon the ECSMP (A Dataset on Emotion, Cognition, Sleep, and Multi-Modal Physiological Signals) dataset—encompassing recordings from 89 healthy participants— we curated a subset of 33 subjects whose data exhibited complete and unimpaired capture across all ECG-sleep modalities, thereby ensuring analytical fidelity; incomplete records from the remaining participants, attributable to recording artifacts or procedural inconsistencies, were judiciously excluded to uphold data integrity. From these selected recordings, 22 ECG-derived and sleep quality features were extracted and subsequently refined through recursive feature elimination (RFE) to mitigate redundancy and enhance predictive salience. We evaluated three regression model—Ridge Regression, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting—employing subject-based 5-fold crossvalidation to foster generalizability across individuals. For sleep efficiency, Ridge Regression attained a mean <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> of 0.8734, indicating a high degree of explained variance; by comparison, Random Forest registered an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> of 0.2756 for AHI, which underscores the formidable obstacles in modeling sporadic apnea episodes amid constrained empirical resources. Feature importance scrutiny further illuminated wake hours and deep sleep ratio as preeminent correlates for sleep efficiency, complemented by deep sleep ratio and QRS amplitude for AHI. Collectively, this framework lays a promising foundation for noninvasive, individualized sleep monitoring, offering reliable estimates of sleep efficiency and preliminary insights into apnea patterns, albeit within the constraints of a modest sample size.

title: Capacitance to Digital Converter Using MASH Sigma-Delta ADC Architecture for IoT Sensor Applications
authors: Bindu Bodi, Chella Amala, Burra Subbarao, Banee Bandana Das, Jayendra Kumar, Priyanka Singh, Saswat Kumar Ram, Saraju P Mohanty
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: In the field of MEMS, there is a wide use of capacitive sensors for several applications like pressure sensor, accelerometers etc. in internet of things (IoT) applications. Due to this we also need analog to digital converters which are more compact and can provide better gain. Hence, in this research, we are designing a Capacitance to Digital converter using MASH Sigma-Delta Architecture in the ADC part which can provide better SNR than the existing methods. MASH Sigma-Delta Architecture provides excellent Noise-Shaping which helps in increasing the circuit efficiency. A switched capacitor circuit is used for capacitance-to-voltage conversion, and the voltage output is fed to the MASH Sigma Delta ADC. Switched capacitance circuits provide better frequency response, and the ADC part provides better noise rejection and gain. This design is implemented in the Cadence Virtuoso environment using 90nm technology. We designed the building blocks and integrated them to form the whole CDC circuit and the simulation results and performance metrics validate the proposed concept. In this implementation, we calculated the metrics of the circuits by the parameters and achieved an SNR of 77.62 dB with a capacitive resolution of 78.13 aF.

title: Performance Trade-offs of MIMO Communication Systems: Alamouti versus Zero-Forcing
authors: Zhiduan Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: This paper investigates the performance of multiple input multiple output (MIMO) communication systems over Rayleigh fading channels, focusing on the trade-offs between modulation schemes, antenna configurations, and signal processing techniques (Alamouti diversity, Zero-Forcing (ZF) detection). Simulations were conducted to evaluate the bit error rate (BER) performance of three quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) schemes (4-QAM, 16-QAM, and 64-QAM) employing a single-antenna transmission configuration under varying channel conditions, 2 × 1 and 2 × 2 Alamouti MIMO, and 2 × 2, 2 × 4 and 4 × 10 ZF-based MIMO, with E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">b</inf>/N<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> ranging from 0 to 20 dB. Key findings reveal that Alamouti’s orthogonal design delivers significant diversity gain in small MIMO setups: 2 × 2 Alamouti reduced 4QAM’s BER by three orders of magnitude at E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">b</inf>/N<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> = 10 dB compared to single-antenna transmission, outperforming ZF in 2 × 2 configurations (where ZF suffered from severe noise amplification). For ZF detection, scaling receive antennas mitigated noise amplification, 4 × 10 ZF enabled 64QAM to achieve the BER interval of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup> at E<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">b</inf>/N<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> = 20 dB, a stark improvement over 2 × 2 ZF. The results confirm that Alamouti is optimal for small MIMO systems in Rayleigh fading, while ZF becomes with large antenna arrays, providing guidance for balancing spectral efficiency and reliability in wireless communication systems.

title: Power-Area Efficient Hardware Architecture Design of 2D FIR Filter using CSD
authors: Venkata Krishna Odugu, Sireesha Pendem, Harish Babu Gade, Merina Jaladi
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: This paper details the design of a hardware architecture for a Two-Dimensional Finite Impulse Response (2D FIR) filter, optimized for efficiency in power, area, and delay for image processing tasks. The multiplier module, which is typically a component in such filters that is both resource-intensive and complex, has been replaced by a structure that is efficient and does not require a multiplier. In order to perform arithmetic operations, this proposed architecture is solely dependent on adders/subtractors, and shifters. The adoption of Canonical Signed Digit (CSD) encoding for the filter coefficients is the most important aspect of this multiplierless approach. This representation reduces the amount of bits that are non-zero in comparison to a normal binary format. This reduction in the number of non-zero bits directly correlates to a hardware implementation that is sparser and requires fewer operators to add. Because of this, architecture is able to accomplish significant improvements in terms of both space and power efficiency. The design process involved generating filter coefficients with specific characteristics using MATLAB, and the coefficients are converted into CSD by the dedicated hardware in this work, followed by hardware implementation using Verilog HDL. Finally, the design was synthesized for a 45nm ASIC CMOS technology using Cadence tools and also using Xilinx Vivado tools to produce and analyze its real-world area, power, and delay metrics.

title: Optimization Convolutional Neural Networks using Particle Swarm Optimization for Malaria Disease Cell Classification
authors: Izza Anisa, Purwanto, Guruh Fajar Shidik, Catur Supriyanto
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Malaria remains a significant global health challenge, where early and accurate detection is critical for effective treatment and control. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated remarkable success in image classification tasks, including malaria diagnosis using microscopy images. However, optimizing hyperparameters on CNN for the specific task of malaria classification remains a challenging endeavor. This paper presents an innovative approach to optimizing CNNs for malaria classification using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). PSO is employed to automatically evolve the network architecture, including layer configurations, filter sizes, and hyperparameters dropout and learning rate. The experiments utilized the National Library of Medicine (NLM) Malaria dataset, sourced from Kaggle, which contains blood smear images collected from 201 patients. The dataset is classified into two groups: malaria-infected and uninfected, providing a robust foundation for model training and evaluation. To ensure reliable model assessment, the dataset was split into $\mathbf{8 0 \%}$ for training, $\mathbf{1 0 \%}$ for validation, and $\mathbf{1 0 \%}$ for testing. Experimental results reveal that PSO-based CNN optimization significantly outperforms manually-tuned CNNs, offering superior performance and reliability. The proposed method provides a promising solution for malaria detection, achieving an impressive accuracy of $99.40 \%$ enabling more efficient and accurate automated diagnoses from medical images. These findings suggest that integrating PSO with CNNs can make a substantial contribution to the advancement of automated healthcare systems.

title: Performance Evaluation of Advanced Control Strategies for Microgrids Using ANOVA-Based Statistical Analysis
authors: Khaled Belhouchet, Assam Zorig, Abdelhakim Idir, Mohamed Amroune, Taki Eddine Benterbah, Ismail Ghadbane, Noureddine Hamouda
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: This paper evaluates three advanced microgrid control strategies—Droop Control, Model Predictive Control (MPC), and Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)—under varying renewable penetration and grid conditions. A detailed microgrid model with storage and realistic loads is developed, and performance indicators such as frequency deviation, voltage stability, energy cost, and loss of load probability are assessed. Statistical validation using two-way ANOVA confirms significant differences, with MPC achieving the best overall performance, FLC showing strong robustness, and droop control remaining reliable but less efficient. A regression model further predicts energy cost as a function of control strategy, renewable integration, and storage capacity, providing practical guidance for cost-effective and reliable microgrid operation.

title: Motor-Imagery Electroencephalography signal decoding Using ICA and Machine Learning for Prosthetic Control
authors: Jigar Gohil, Reena Trivedi
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: We present a compact motor-imagery (MI) EEG pipeline that pairs Butterworth band-pass filtering with ICA denoising and lightweight feature engineering (statistical + PSD), followed by a Random Forest classifier. Using multi-electrode EEG (F3, F4, FC5, FC6) from 18 participants, the model achieves 92% accuracy with high precision/recall and ROC-AUC = 0.92, outperforming SVM, logistic regression (SGD), and gradient boosting while requiring modest compute—well-suited for embedded neuroprosthetic control. Predictions are mapped to a 3D-printed prosthetic arm, demonstrating end-to-end feasibility. Limitations include offline evaluation in a controlled lab and a modest sample size; cross-subject validation and online adaptation remain open. These results show that ICA-assisted preprocessing + classical ML can approach deep-learning performance at a fraction of the complexity, motivating real-time deployment and multi-class extensions.

title: Identification of Chinese Internet neologism and Implicit Sentiment Analysis Based on COT
authors: Xinyu Zhang, Long Zhang, Qiusheng Zheng, Yanli Liang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: To address the dual challenges of novel term recognition and implicit sentiment comprehension in sentiment analysis of internet neologisms, this study innovatively proposes a framework integrating large language models with chain-of-thought reasoning. Four core modules were designed: a dynamically updated "Internet Neologism" knowledge base, neologism detection, semantic analysis, and semantically enhanced sentiment analysis. The knowledge base provides foundational data support for subsequent analysis; the neologism detection module precisely locates Internet neologism; the semantic analysis module deeply explores the meanings of new words; finally, the semantically enhanced sentiment analysis module combines new word semantics for accurate sentiment judgment. Experimental results fully validate the method's effectiveness: neologism recognition accuracy reaches 84.04%, surpassing the baseline model by 11.64%. For sentiment analysis tasks, accuracy and F1 score improve by 11.5% and 7.45% respectively compared to the baseline model, significantly enhancing the performance of sentiment analysis for online neologisms.

title: Wavelet-Fusion WMD-Net for Detecting Multi-Scale Ships in Complex SAR Scenes
authors: Rui Wang, Zhihui Xin, Yuhu Qiao, Shuanghui Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) with its all-weather and all-day imaging capability, has been widely used in ship detection. However, the SAR imaging principle and the influence of nearshore complex background interference make the existing ship detection methods have limited detection accuracy under multi-scale and dense targets. For this reason, based on You Only Look Once (YOLOv8), this paper proposes Wavelet-fusion Multi-scale Detection Network (WMD-Net), a SAR ship detection algorithm integrated wavelet-fusion features with multi-scale modeling information to enhance ship detection performance. First, WMD-Net introduces Wavelet-Attention-Convolution Mix (WACmix) in the Neck, which integrates wavelet decomposition, local convolution and attention mechanism to deepen the multi-frequency and cross-channel feature interactions. It enhances the perception ability of small targets and complex textures. In addition, using Cross-Scale Feature Fusion Module (CCFM) structure fuses different scale features through fusion operations to enhance adaptability of scale variations. It can effectively integrate detailed features and contextual information of SAR image. The experimental results show that on the SSDD dataset, the F1, AP0.5 and AP value of WMD-Net reach 96.2%, 97.5% and 66%, respectively, which are better than the other compared models, showing excellent detection performance in complex backgrounds.

title: Deep Learning for Drylands: Intelligent Sensing to Transform Agriculture in Arid Regions
authors: Abdul Haris, Iriansyah Bm Sangadji, Hengki Sikumbang, Dinola, Rahma Farah Ningrum, Salsabila Tsabilatul Qolbi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Dryland agriculture plays an important role in food production and livelihoods, but its productivity is often constrained by low rainfall, uneven water distribution, limited soil fertility, and the impacts of climate change. These conditions exacerbate the uncertainty of water supply and increase the socio-economic vulnerability of farmers. To address these challenges, this study proposes the integration of Internet of Things (IoT)-based smart sensors with a Deep Learning model using the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) architecture to predict irrigation requirements in dryland areas in real time. Environmental data including soil moisture, air temperature, and relative humidity were collected every 10 minutes and then processed through cleaning, normalization, and time-series formation stages before being trained using the GRU model. The experimental results demonstrate that the model can predict water requirements with high accuracy, as indicated by an MSE of 0.009914, MAE of 0.083784, and a coefficient of determination ($\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$) close to $\mathbf{1}$. The model predictions closely match the actual data over a 30-day period and can classify water requirements into Needs Water, Normal, and No Water Needed. This system can be integrated with IoT-based automated control for precise pump and valve operation. This approach has the potential to improve water use efficiency, optimize crop productivity, and support sustainable agriculture in drought-prone regions.

title: Derivation of the Benchmark Models of Gyroscope and 3-DoF Robotic Arm via Euler-Lagrange Equation
authors: Yipeng Yang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel method to derive, via Euler-Lagrange equations, the full dynamics of gyroscope and 3-DoF robotic arm, which are two fundamental models of rigid body systems. Unlike traditional approaches that directly use physical quantities in relation to rigid body motion, involved quantities naturally appear in this derivation. This method can be analogously applied to more sophisticated rigid body systems. Numerical simulations are provided and a state feedback control is applied to the robotic arm pertaining the positioning problem, showing that these models can be used to simulate system behavior with any feasible initial conditions.

title: Privacy-Aware Sharing of Raw Spatial Sensor Data for Cooperative Perception
authors: Bangya Liu, Chengpo Yan, Chenghao Jiang, Suman Banerjee, Akarsh Prabhakara
publish: Proceedings of the 27th International Workshop on Mobile Computing Systems and Applications

authors: Anurag Pallaprolu, Winston Hurst, Yasamin Mostofi
publish: Proceedings of the 27th International Workshop on Mobile Computing Systems and Applications
abstract: None

title: Analysis and Optimization of Multi-Active RIS Assisted Cell-Free Massive MIMO
authors: Prince Anokye, Suho Shin, Mustapha Benjillali, Samir Saoudi, Kyoung-Jae Lee
abstract: This paper investigates the sum spectral efficiency (SE) and total energy efficiency (EE) of the active reconfigurable intelligent surface (aRIS)-assisted cell-free (CF) massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) over temporal and spatially correlated channels. Multiple aRISs are deployed between numerous access points (APs) and mobile users to enhance the signals. The active reflective elements (REs) amplify and vary the signal phase. The continuously evolving channel creates a situation, where the channel differs during training and data transmission. We characterize the joint impact of multi-user interference (MUI), pilot contamination (PC), channel aging (CA), and active RIS noise amplification (RNA). The desired signal is enhanced as the reflected signal amplitude and REs per aRIS increase. However, the PC, MUI, CA, and RNA also grow– constraining the SE. Also, the network power consumption increases. An alternating optimization framework based on the weighted minimum mean square error and fractional programming is proposed to optimize the transmit power and reflection coefficients (RCs) with the objective of maximizing the sum SE. It is demonstrated that the number of APs and REs can be reduced by deploying aRISs. The proposed power and RC optimization algorithms considerably improves the sum SE. The trade-off analysis between the total EE and sum SE shows that the envelope of the operating region of the CF mMIMO is expanded by deploying multiple aRISs.

title: Torque Ripple Suppression for High-speed Slotless PMSM Considering Unbalanced Back-EMF and Dead-time Effect
authors: Bo Jiang, Zhong Zheng, Cheng Pi, Jiang Long, Tianyi Zhao
abstract: Affected by manufacturing imperfections and inverter dead time, high-speed slotless permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) often suffer from undesirable torque ripples. To mitigate these ripples, this paper proposes a closed-loop harmonic current suppression strategy. The method extracts the sequence components of the stator current using a harmonic extraction matrix to generate corresponding positive and negative reference voltage vectors. These are then synthesized into multiple voltage vectors for space vector modulation (SVM). It is shown that the torque ripple caused by unbalanced back-electromotive force (Back-EMF) cannot be fully suppressed—only the negative-sequence components can be effectively addressed. For digital implementation, two matrix calculation methods are presented. As only the DC components are controlled throughout the process, simple PI controllers can be employed, significantly reducing parameter tuning effort. Experimental results validate the feasibility of the proposed strategy on a laboratory slotless PMSM with intentional defects, under both steady-state and dynamic operating conditions.

title: Transformer-Driven Multi-Center Manifold Modeling Deep SVDD for Anomaly Detection in IIoT Networks
authors: Weijian Zhong, Dagang Li, Yuntao Zou, Tongjun Guan, Wei Wang
abstract: Anomaly detection in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) systems is crucial for ensuring operational safety and product quality. However, challenges such as heterogeneous sensor data, dynamic network structures, and complex spatio-temporal dependencies hinder existing methods. To address these issues, we propose a novel Transformer-based anomaly detection framework designed for dynamic IIoT networks. Our framework integrates self-supervised graph contrastive learning with multi-center Support Vector Data Description (SVDD) for the first time. Specifically, we construct temporal graph snapshots from normal time windows. Graph Attention Networks (GAT) are used to extract structural features, while a lightweight Transformer with temporal window attention captures long-range dependencies among sensor sequences, producing robust spatio-temporal node representations. To improve the model’s generalization without requiring labeled anomalies, a self-supervised contrastive loss is introduced. The resulting node embeddings are clustered via k-Medoids, and an SVDD sub-model is trained per cluster to define multiple hyperspheres representing normal behavior. During inference, the proximity of test representations to these hyper-spheres enables accurate and interpretable anomaly detection. Extensive experiments on real-world IIoT datasets, including SWaT and WADI, show our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in AUC and F1 score, demonstrating its effectiveness and practical value for IIoT security applications.

title: Fast-Tactical Diffusion for On-Board UAV Spectrum-Level Signal Deception
authors: Lin Xu, Yihan Wu, Zeyu Zhang, Lingyun Feng, Chao Zhu, Fangxin Wang, Jianping An
abstract: Spectrum deception has found broad utility across multiple domains, including electronic warfare, tactical counter-measures, and adversarial sensing suppression. However, generating complex time–frequency signatures relies on sophisticated signal processing pipelines, which poses significant challenges for UAV and other IoT platforms with severely constrained onboard computational resources. Moreover, the limited onboard capability further restricts rapid signal synthesis and adaptation, failing to meet the strict rapid-response requirements of the battlefield. To address this dilemma, we propose the Fast-Tactical Signal Deception Framework (FT-SDF), a specialized generative architecture optimized for real-time signal synthesis. We formulate a novel spectro-temporal diffusion dynamics mechanism that innovatively incorporates additional spectral blurring and reverse process variance, jointly optimizing noise prediction and variance, which is necessary to preserve fine-grained spectral structures and key time–frequency signatures across different modulation schemes. Notably, to ensure strict adherence to communication protocols, we introduce a lightweight spectrum-context encoder that employs a dual-domain embedding strategy for physics-aware conditioning. Furthermore, to enable rapid inference, we develop a variance-aware acceleration mechanism that exploits learned spectral uncertainty to guide a dynamic warm-start schedule, thereby drastically compressing the sampling trajectory. Extensive evaluations on a systematically reconstructed RadioML benchmark demonstrate that FT-SDF outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Specifically, it achieves a 59.3% reduction in sampling iterations (more than 2-fold inference speedup), while maintaining both high statistical fidelity (FID < 15) and industrial-grade precision (EVM ≤ 14%), demonstrating the feasibility of rapid, controllable generative AI in complex electromagnetic environments.

title: A Low-Capacitance Long-Strip Electrode Design for Fast-Switching Vertical SiC PCSS
authors: Jia Huang, Long Hu, Xianwei Wang, Xiangjun Meng, Xinran Yang, Yingxiang Yang, Xianghong Yang
abstract: This letter investigates the effect of parasitic capacitance on the switching speed of vertical vanadium-compensated semi-insulating (VCSI) SiC photoconductive semiconductor switches (vPCSSs) under sub-nanosecond laser excitation, revealing that fall time is strongly influenced by the parasitic capacitance. The simple adoption of parallel-plate scaling suffers from an inherent trade-off between parasitic capacitance reduction and optical absorption efficiency. To address this, a novel opto-electronically co-optimized long-strip electrode design is proposed, with an optimal 3 mm width that minimizes parasitic capacitance while limiting optical power leakage to only 3.6%. Compared with common circular-electrode vPCSSs, the fabricated device achieves a 23% reduction in rise time, 53% reduction in fall time, and 25% increase in peak output voltage.

title: K -means and DDPG Based Access Point Deployment Strategy in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Systems with Hybrid Energy Supply
authors: Yunan Zhang, Yanxiang Jiang, Hao Wu, Boyu Zhang, Fu-Chun Zheng
abstract: The energy consumption of communications networks has been steadily increasing, becoming one of the key factors restricting the development of mobile communications. Cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO) systems offer wide coverage, high spectral efficiency (SE) and excellent energy efficiency (EE), and by integrating renewable energy sources, can greatly enhance their energy-saving capabilities. However, differences in energy harvesting (EH) efficiency and communications conditions across various deployment locations bring significant challenges. In this paper, we investigate the access point (AP) deployment strategy for CF-mMIMO systems with hybrid energy supply. We formulate an optimization problem aimed at maximizing grid EE and propose an AP deployment optimization method based on the deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) algorithm. By considering the large number of potential AP locations, a K-means-based preliminary AP selection method is introduced to significantly reduce the action space dimensionality in the DDPG framework. Simulation results indicate that the proposed K-means-based approach effectively reduces the action space dimensionality and enhances the training efficiency of the DDPG algorithm. Furthermore, the proposed method shows great effectiveness in improving the grid EE.

authors: N/A

title: UDFStudio: A Unified Framework of Datasets, Benchmarks and Generative Models for Unsigned Distance Functions_supp1-3668763.pdf
authors: Yu-Shen Liu
abstract: Unsigned distance functions (UDFs) have emerged as powerful representation for modeling and reconstructing geometries with open surfaces. However, the development of 3D generative models for UDFs remains largely unexplored, limiting current methods from generating diverse open-surface 3D content. Moreover, mainstream 3D datasets predominantly consist of watertight meshes, revealing a critical challenge: the absence of standardized datasets and benchmarks specifically tailored for open-surface generation and reconstruction. In this paper, we begin by introducing UDiFF, a novel diffusion-based 3D generative model specifically designed for UDFs. UDiFF supports both conditional and unconditional generation of textured 3D shapes with open surfaces. At its core, UDiFF generates UDFs in the spatial-frequency domain using a learnable wavelet transform. Instead of relying on manually selected wavelet transforms, which are labor-intensive and prone to information loss, we introduce a data-driven approach that learns the optimal wavelet transformation from UDFs datasets. Beyond UDiFF, we present the UWings dataset, comprising 1,509 high-quality 3D open surface models of winged creatures. Using UWings, we establish comprehensive benchmarks for evaluating both generative and reconstruction methods based on UDFs.

title: LED Lighting Influence on the Radiation Response of Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers
authors: A. Facchini, L. Weninger, A. Morana, L. Mescia, T. Robin, J. Mekki, N. Kerboub, A. Boukenter, E. Marin, Y. Ouerdane, S. Girard
abstract: We studied the radiation response of two different Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers based on radiation hardened optical fibers when an external LED lighting is used to photobleach these fibers to limit the radiation-induced attenuation (RIA) and then the amplifier gain degradation (GD). Noticeable results in terms of RIA and GD reduction are achieved by using LED lighting; however, the best results were achieved through the combination of blue LED lighting with pump injection into the tested fibers.

title: Fully Cooperative MADRL for Energy Efficient Resource Allocation in D2D Communications
authors: Yong Chen, Zhixin Liu, Yazhou Yuan, Xinping Guan
abstract: Device-to-Device (D2D) communication is considered a key technology for improving data transmission rates and reducing energy consumption. To overcome the drawbacks of centralized resource allocation schemes, such as their dependence on global information, this paper investigates the joint spectral subband selection and power control problem for D2D communication in cellular networks, where wireless channels are dynamically changing and a distributed resource allocation scheme is developed. The optimization objective is to maximize the energy efficient (EE) of all D2D pairs while satisfying the minimum transmission rate requirements of both cellular users to the base station (BS) and D2D links. To address the issue of EE resource allocation, this paper proposes a full cooperative multi-agent double deep Q-network distributed algorithm based on low-dimensional fingerprints and soft update framework (FMDL). Through this algorithm, each agent can utilize its locally observed information to cooperatively maximize the system's EE. The analysis of uplink communication cost and computational complexity demonstrates that the proposed algorithm significantly reduces both the uplink transmission overhead and the computational burden on the BS. The simulation results demonstrate that compared to the benchmark algorithm, FMDL significantly improves the EE of all D2D pairs.

title: Learn Geospatial Correlations for Remote Sensing Image Change Detection
authors: Jia Liu, Jingxuan Ju, Wenhua Zhang, Fang Liu, Jingxiang Yang, Liang Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Change detection plays a critical role in Earth’s surface surveillance via remote sensing images which usually have strong geospatial correlations in surface entities including specific global layout of different scenes and contextual dependencies of local regions or objects. To capture such geospatial correlations and facilitate recognition of changed regions, based on attention mechanism, we propose a geospatial correlation feature enhancement network (GCFENet) for remote sensing image change detection. In the network, to capture global scene context and change details, a dual feature interaction (DFI) module is firstly designed. Then, to model and fuse different correlations for each scale, a three branch multi-correlation attention (MCA) block is proposed which models local distributions, global scene dependencies and pixel-wise semantic prior respectively, and associates them via a self-attention module to capture the semantic distributions within the scene and between different regions. In the MCA network, a Gaussian filter based frequency separation (GFS) module is designed to generate the global scene dependencies and local distributions, which are used as the value and the query in self-attention respectively. Then a local semantic modeling (LSM) module is proposed to assign pixel-wise semantic prior, which is used as the key to generate correlations with the query and the value. This GCFENet enables the attention mechanism to compute similarity not only based on pixel-wise feature matching but also by perceiving the geospatial distribution correlations of ground objects and bi-temporal spatial correspondences. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate the state-of-the- art performance of the GCFENet. The code will be opensourced in [https://github.com/4043-lab/Change-detection] once accepted.

title: Neutral-Point Potential Oscillation Suppression and Common-Mode Voltage Elimination in Three-Level Inverter-Fed Dual Three-Phase PMSM Drives
authors: Shuyang Lu, Zicheng Liu, Dong Jiang, Ronghai Qu
abstract: Neutral-point potential (NPP) oscillation and common-mode voltage (CMV) adversely affect motor systems fed by three-level neutral-point-clamped (3L-NPC) inverters. NPP oscillation increases current harmonics and degrades capacitor lifespan, while CMV induces electromagnetic interference and corrodes motor bearings. This article proposes a modulation-based method for a dual three-phase permanent-magnet synchronous motor (DTP-PMSM) system powered by dual 3L-NPC inverters with isolated neutrals, which effectively suppresses NPP oscillation and eliminates CMV. Using a double-modulation-wave strategy, NPP balance is achieved, and the specific zero-sequence component required to eliminate CMV is derived. A phase-shift modulation scheme is designed for pulse-width modulation sequences to enable pulse-edge cancellation for total CMV elimination, while capacitor voltage offsets are effectively eliminated by adjusting the zero-level duration of each phase. The proposed scheme not only improves the total harmonic distortion (THD) of the output current but also further reduces common-mode electromagnetic interference (CM EMI). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves accurate NPP control while significantly reducing the peak CMV, root-mean-square (rms) leakage current, and CM EMI noise.

title: Fuzzy Rule-guided Multi-view Differentiable Representation Learning with Dual-Space Information Extraction
authors: Wei Zhang, Jun Zhou, Guanjin Wang, Zhaohong Deng, Weiping Ding, Witold Pedrycz
abstract: Effectively extracting discriminative information from multi-view data remains a key challenge in multi-view learning. Existing methods typically focus on exploring inter-view consistency via linear or nonlinear transformation. While nonlinear methods tend to yield better performance, their limited transparency and interpretability limit their practical applications. Takagi-Sugeno-Kang Fuzzy Systems (TSK-FS), as a rule-based model with high interpretability, have been applied to multi-view tasks. However, prior methods either rely solely on antecedent components for nonlinear modeling or integrate deep neural networks into the consequent part, thereby compromising model interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose Fuzzy Rule-guided Multi-view Differentiable Representation Learning (FRMVDRL). Specifically, in our framework, antecedent parameters of TSK-FS are first used to map data into highdimensional fuzzy space. Then during the learning of consequent parameters, a dual information extraction mechanism is proposed to jointly capture shared knowledge across views and view-specific knowledge. Moreover, a second-order geometric structure preservation mechanism is constructed to exploit structural information at both the instance and the instance-pair level. To enhance discriminability of the learned representations, a biorthogonal constraint alongside a Shannon entropy mechanism is introduced. Finally, to balance model performance and interpretability, we introduce a novel multi-view differentiable optimization strategy that incorporates learnable parameters to expand the solution space while preserving the structure of traditional optimization. Extensive experiments on benchmark multi-view datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the FRMVDRL.

title: Open-Set Deepfake Detection: A Parameter-Efficient Adaptation Method with Forgery Style Mixture
authors: Chenqi Kong, Anwei Luo, Peijun Bao, Haoliang Li, Renjie Wan, Zengwei Zheng, Anderson Rocha, Alex C. Kot
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Open-set face forgery detection poses significant security threats and presents substantial challenges for existing detection models. These detectors primarily have two limitations: they cannot generalize across unknown forgery domains or inefficiently adapt to new data. To address these issues, we introduce an approach that is both general and parameter-efficient for face forgery detection. Our method builds on the assumption that different forgery source domains exhibit distinct style statistics. Specifically, we design a forgery-style-mixture formulation that augments the diversity of forgery source domains, enhancing the model’s generalizability across unseen domains. In addition, previous methods typically require fully fine-tuning pretrained networks, consuming substantial time and computational resources. Drawing on recent advancements in vision transformers (ViT) for face forgery detection, we develop a parameter-efficient ViT-based detection model that includes lightweight forgery feature extraction modules and enables the model to extract global and local forgery clues simultaneously. We only optimize the inserted lightweight modules during training, maintaining the original ViT structure with its pre-trained weights. This training strategy effectively preserves the informative pre-trained knowledge while flexibly adapting the model to the task of Deepfake detection. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the designed model achieves state-of-the-art generalizability with significantly reduced trainable parameters, representing an important step toward open-set Deepfake detection in the wild.

title: Sparse Representation Methods for Effective Data Recovery in Wireless Sensor Networks: A Review
authors: Sakib Hasnat Sayem, Jingfei He, Vishodha Waga Arachchi, Xiaotong Liu, Charitha Alahakoon
abstract: Missing and corrupted data remain persistent challenges in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs), particularly under energy constraints, harsh operating environments, and structured loss patterns. This review systematically examines sparse-representation based data recovery methods by categorizing them into two methodological classes: low-order sparsity and high-order sparsity frameworks. The analysis emphasizes how these approaches exploit spatial, temporal, and multi-attribute correlations, and critically discusses the implications of their modeling assumptions, rank selection strategies, and computational complexity. While compressive sensing and matrix completion offer favorable computational efficiency under predominantly random missingness, tensor-based methods including CP, Tucker, Tensor Train, Tensor Ring, and t-SVD often deliver improved reconstruction accuracy for multi-attribute sensing data and structured missing patterns, with increased computational and memory requirements. By clarifying recoverability conditions and performance trade-offs across these frameworks, this review establishes a unified methodological foundation for principled, comparable, and deployment-oriented advances in WSNs data recovery.

title: A Laser Scanning Device for Cryogenic Beam Steering At 20 Millikelvin
authors: Jonathan W. Dean, Avirup Roy, Joseph W. Fowler, Kelsey M. Morgan, Sae Woo Nam, Nathan Ortiz, Daniel S. Swetz, Joel N. Ullom, Joel C. Weber, Galen C. O'Neil
abstract: It is often desirable to steer light within a cryostat and have a well focussed laser spot incident upon a detector. We demonstrate the operation of superconducting transitionedge sensor microcalorimeters with a fibre-coupled laser scanning device co-located on the 20mK mixing plate of a dilution refrigerator. The scanner directs a 515nm laser beam with spot size <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< 40\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula>m over a range of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 14mm in two orthogonal axes (covering 784mm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>). We present the optical elements of the system, show that the detector noise spectrum is unchanged when the scanner is operating or off, and observe less than 340 nW power dissipation in our use-case. This device will allow detector calibration up to 10 keV on a pixel-by-pixel basis with simultaneous X-ray measurements. Further, this device will enable more effective characterisation of certain detector artifacts including cross-talk, position dependences, and drift corrections.

title: Self-Adaptive Density Peak Clustering through Aggregation of Multiple Similarity Matrices in Heterogeneous Information Networks
authors: Yongjie Liang, Qinggeng Jin, Wujie Hu, Junjie Wu, Yu Liang, Jinzhao Wu, Bizhong Wei
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: Clustering plays a pivotal role in analyzing heterogeneous information networks (HINs). Existing clustering algorithms for HINs primarily rely on metapaths, which not only overlook valuable information in directly neighboring nodes but also require fitting or neglecting cutoff distances and clustering centers. This paper introduces a novel clustering algorithm, SDPClus, which addresses these limitations by simultaneously considering the network structure, semantic information, and the Gini Index. SDPClus dynamically calculates the optimal cutoff distance, employs a critical point to autonomously identify cluster centers, and detects outliers. Additionally, it generates multiple similarity matrices, each assigned a weight to indicate its importance level. The aggregation of weight information from these matrices produces a distance matrix. Experimental results on three real datasets demonstrate that SDPClus consistently outperforms seven other algorithms across various evaluation metrics, highlighting its superior clustering effectiveness.

title: Authenticity, Aesthetic Quality, and Audience Engagement in AI-Generated Virtual Performance: A Structural Equation Modeling Approach
authors: Hui Wang, Peter Nigel Power
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: This study focuses on the audience perception mechanisms of AI-generated virtual performances. A conceptual model is constructed with algorithmic aesthetic quality, perceived authenticity, immersion, mediated intimacy, satisfaction, and intention to rewatch and share as core variables. This model is empirically tested using a PLS-SEM approach. Based on data from 303 valid questionnaires, the study confirms the significant role of algorithmic aesthetic quality and perceived authenticity in enhancing immersion and mediated intimacy. These factors contribute directly and through mediation to audience satisfaction, with satisfaction being a key factor driving rewatch and sharing intentions. Predictive testing of the model demonstrates that the framework possesses both strong explanatory power and robust out-of-sample predictive power. This research enriches the analytical framework for the evaluation of virtual performances theoretically and provides empirical evidence for the creation and promotion of AI-generated performances in practice.

title: Construction and Dynamic Evolution of an Urban Renewal Policy Knowledge Graph Integrating Multi-Source Data
authors: Wenchao Liu, Jinghao Gu, Minmin Li, Wenhua Guo, Yafei Wang, Shunli Wang, Naping Wang, Yuhan Zhang
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: As urban development enters a stage of stock optimization and high-quality renewal, systematic analysis of urban renewal policy knowledge has become essential for refined urban governance. However, existing policy management relies heavily on manual interpretation and suffers from knowledge fragmentation and limited support for temporal evolution analysis, constraining effective policy utilization. This paper proposes a large language model (LLM)-driven method for constructing an urban renewal policy knowledge graph, forming a technical framework of ontology modeling, automated extraction, graph construction, and evolutionary updating. By analyzing the spatio-temporal semantic characteristics of multi-level and multi-type policies, regulations, and technical standards, policy entities, attributes, and relationships are formally defined. An LLM integrated with domain knowledge is employed to automatically extract entities and relations from unstructured texts. Incorporating policy versioning and geographic information, the proposed knowledge graph supports version traceability and semantic connectivity. Experiments on national and major municipal policy documents result in a multi-dimensional policy knowledge graph containing 1,962 entities and 2,491 relationships, achieving 91.7% entity recognition accuracy and an 88.5% F1-score for relation extraction. The constructed knowledge graph demonstrates strong structural integrity, semantic relevance, and query capability, supporting intelligent policy retrieval and policy evolution analysis, and providing technical support for data-driven urban governance.

title: Deep Networks and Internet of Medical Things for Tracking the Post Surgical Recovery Condition: A Comparative Approach
authors: Surendra Mohan Samal, Aparna Sibadutta Mishra, Moupiya Bose, Adyasha Swain, Suprava Ranjan Laha, Debasish Swapnesh Kumar Nayak
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Proper post-operative recovery tracking is crucial for the early identification of complications and optimal patient outcomes. This paper introduces a computer-vision-based, sensor less technique fusing DL models with the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) to predict a patient's recovery status Healthy or Unhealthy. Estimated vital signs such as heart rate (HR), blood pressure (BP), temperature, SpO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, and hemoglobin are obtained from facial photographs and video inputs by applying machine learning algorithms. A synthetic dataset of 10,000 features is reduced in dimension to less than 2,000 dimensions by using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and autoencoders. Three models are compared: a Baseline Deep Neural Network (DNN), Autoencoder + Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and Residual MLP. Among these, Residual MLP yields the best classification accuracy of 94.7%, beating others because of improved gradient flow in deep architectures. A Streamlit-enabled web interface makes real-time vitals estimation and prediction possible. The findings highlight the viability of contactless, AI-based post-operative monitoring and stress the need for both feature compression and sound model selection. Real-world dataset expansion and mobile health deployment remain future directions.

title: Automation of Decision Support in Simulation-Based Digital Twins in Manufacturing: A Survey
authors: Ohad Daniel, Sanja Lazarova-Molnar
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Internet of Things: Systems, Management and Security (IOTSMS)
abstract: Simulation-based Digital Twins are increasingly used for decision support in manufacturing. This paper examines peer-reviewed studies to evaluate how, and to what extent, decision support is automated in existing Digital Twins implementations. We analyze each implementation across six dimensions: (i) the domain of application, (ii) the main goal, (iii) the methodology used to develop or extract the Digital Twin model, (iv) the decision support methodology, (v) the level of automation, and (vi) the main gaps and limitations of the implementation. Across the reviewed studies, automation remains partial; no implementation achieved fully autonomous end-to-end operation of the Digital Twin. Building on this analysis, we identify key enablers and barriers to automating decision support in Digital Twins, with a primary focus on the manufacturing domain. Based on these findings, we outline research priorities to help close the decision support automation gap in Digital Twins, emphasizing the importance of human oversight, risk-aware control, and data privacy and security. In addition, we emphasize the importance of generalizability and scalability, and the evaluation of decision outcomes and Artificial Intelligence components. Overall, this review clarifies the current state of automation in decision support within Digital Twins and highlights targeted areas for future development toward fully autonomous decision support in Digital Twins.

title: Brain Tumour Detection Using Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN)
authors: S Karthik, K Snehanvitha, Addanki Sai Kumar, Dattatreya Teella, R Naga Shreya, Shalini Ramanathan
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Brain tumor detection is a vital and challenging task in medical diagnosis, requiring highly precise and efficient methods to improve patient treatment outcomes. Traditional approaches rely on classical machine learning and deep learning techniques, which, while effective to a degree, often struggle with capturing the complex and high-dimensional patterns present in medical imaging data. A significant limitation of these conventional methods is their inability to process and analyze intricate features efficiently, which can impact diagnostic accuracy. To address these challenges, this work proposes a novel Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN) framework that combines quantum computing principles, via quantum circuits, with classical machine learning architectures implemented through tools like PyTorch and PennyLane. This hybrid approach enhances feature extraction and classification capabilities, leading to superior performance. Experimental results demonstrate that the QCNN model achieves an improved accuracy of 90 percent on the test dataset, surpassing traditional methods and showing promise for advancing brain tumor detection technology. This improvement is supported by consistent decreases in training and validation loss during model training, highlighting the robustness and efficacy of the quantum-enhanced model.

title: Fusing Quaternion Rotations and Star Operations: A TLSNet-Based High-Precision 3-D Indoor Visible Light Positioning System
authors: Tianyi Cao, Lei Jing, Shaorui Sang
abstract: This paper presents a deep-learning-enhanced visible light positioning (VLP) system for accurate 3D localization under arbitrary receiver orientations. In a 3D VLP system with a single LED, multiple photodiodes, and an IMU, the commonly used Euler-angle representation is replaced with sign-consistent unit quaternions as inputs to deep-learning models. By further enforcing explicit constraints to achieve a one-to-one, order-invariant mapping, this reparameterization resolves ambiguities and rotation-order dependency, providing a more structured orientation input and improving the achievable performance of the learning-based pipeline under a fixed system configuration.
