title: Deep Learning Approaches for Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis: a Comprehensive Review
authors: Mahdi Jafari Asl, Saba Haji Molla Rabie
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that severely affects memory, cognition, and daily functioning. Early and reliable diagnosis plays a critical role in improving treatment strategies and patient quality of life. In recent years, deep learning (DL) techniques have shown remarkable potential in medical imaging and neurophysiological signal analysis, enabling automated and accurate detection of AD and related conditions such as mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). This paper provides a comprehensive review of 17 recent studies published between 2023 and 2025, which applied various <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D L$</tex> architectures, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs), transformers, and hybrid models, to electroencephalography (EEG), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and multimodal datasets. The reviewed works adopted diverse feature extraction strategies such as discrete wavelet transform (DWT), synchrosqueezing, power spectral density (PSD), and intrinsic time-scale decomposition (ITD), combined with advanced classifiers and interpretable AI frameworks. A comparative analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of each approach, dataset availability, and diagnostic accuracy. Finally, key challenges, including limited dataset diversity, lack of interpretability, and generalization issues, are discussed, along with future directions such as explainable AI, federated learning, and multimodal fusion, which hold promise for advancing AD diagnosis.

title: Improving Generalization in MRI-Based Deep Learning Models for Total Knee Replacement Prediction
authors: Ehsan Karami, Hamid Soltanian-Zadeh
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Knee osteoarthritis (KOA) is a common joint disease that causes pain and mobility issues. While MRI-based deep learning models have demonstrated superior performance in predicting total knee replacement (TKR) and disease progression, their generalizability remains challenging, particularly when applied to imaging data from different sources. In this study, we show that replacing batch normalization with instance normalization, using data augmentation, and applying contrastive loss improves generalization. For training and evaluation, we used MRI data from the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI) database, considering sagittal fat-suppressed intermediate-weighted turbo spin-echo (FS-IW-TSE) images as the source domain and sagittal fat-suppressed three-dimensional (3D) dual-echo in steady state (DESS) images as the target domain. The results demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in classification metrics across both domains by replacing batch normalization with instance normalization in the baseline model, generating augmented input views using the Global Intensity Non-linear (GIN) augmentation method, and incorporating a supervised contrastive loss alongside the classification loss to align representations of samples with the same label. In the source domain, this approach achieved an accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$74.12 \pm 2.90$</tex>, an F 1 score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$74.57 \pm 3.33$</tex>, and a ROC AUC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$80.65 \pm 2.83$</tex>, outperforming the baseline model, which scored <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$71.29 \pm 4.43,69.76 \pm 4.58$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$77.79 \pm 4.66$</tex>, respectively. In the target domain, the method achieved an accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$70.04 \pm 2.49$</tex>, F1 score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$67.30 \pm 3.57$</tex>, and ROC AUC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$78.12 \pm 1.97$</tex>, compared to the baseline's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$52.87 \pm 3.17$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$18.98 \pm 16.89$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$59.33 \pm 6.20$</tex>. The GIN method with contrastive loss performed better than all evaluated singlesource domain generalization methods when using 3D instance normalization. Comparing GIN with and without contrastive loss (for both normalization types) showed that adding contrastive loss consistently led to better performance.

title: A Multimodal Ensemble Learning Framework for Parkinson’s Disease Detection & Staging in Neurodegenerative Disorder
authors: Shashank Awasthi, Tadiwa Elisha Nyamasvisva, Juliana Rosmidah Binti Jaafar
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Parkinson’s disease is a degenerative disorder, which impairs motor functions as well as non-motor faculties. Early and correct diagnosis is an essential part in the management of patients. This paper introduces a fusion-driven ensemble learning framework that fuses multimodal MRI, voice and handwriting datasets to improve the diagnostic accuracy of Parkinson's disease. Both modalities are treated respectively by a dedicated machine learning algorithm (K-nearest neighbor (KNN)) for MRI, Decision Trees for voice and support vector machine (SVM) for signature-based classification. Ensemble learning is used to fuse the outputs of these models, which integrates the diverse predictions rooted in individual classifiers and leads to a cooperative final prediction. The ensemble approach helps generalization, bias mitigation and multi-modal variations in patient data. Furthermore, patients are evaluated for "on" and "off" times using the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS) and staged by disease severity levels. It outperforms the single-modality models, showing more convincing and robust results, thus providing a new tool for Parkinson's disease detection as well as monitoring at the early stage. The analyzing process of multi-source health data fusion is still challenging, because the method that considers both multiple source features and relationships among sources to diagnose disease simultaneously is few, especially for method which can be generalized easily.

title: Sentiment Analysis for Brand Reputation Using ML and Fuzzy Systems
authors: Abhijit Biswas, Sabyasachi Dasgupta, Priya Grover, Nidhi Phutela, Neetu Mittal
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The rapid growth of user-generated content has turned sentiment analysis into an essential tool for brand reputation management. Conventional Machine Learning models, though precise, tend to be non-interpretable for actionable insights. This paper suggests a hybrid approach that integrates machine learning classifiers with a Fuzzy Logic System to improve sentiment classification and reputation scoring. TF-IDF and Word2Vec are used to extract text features, and Support Vector Machine, Random Forest and Logistic Regression models are used to classify them. The outputs, subjectivity, and polarity scores are passed through a Mamdani-type Fuzzy Inference System to obtain a continuous Brand Reputation Index on a 0–100 scale. Experiments on Sentimentl40 and Amazon Reviews datasets indicate that the hybrid approach outperforms individual classifiers in accuracy and interpretability. The Fuzzy Logic System component is able to extract linguistic vagueness effectively, providing human-interpretable mapping of sentiment to reputation scores. Introducing real-time data streams and optimizing fuzzy rule sets for domain-specific applications are future tasks.

title: Scalable Noise-Resilient Hybrid Quantum–Classical Generative Models for Advanced AI Applications
authors: M.Lenin Kumar, P. Pavan Kumar, Sri Gudisela Ravi Kanth, S. Nagaveni, P. Radhika, Venkateswarlu Chiluka
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The practical use of quantum generative models has been limited to scalability issues and noise in the hardware used to implement the model in versions, however quantum generative models have demonstrated to be useful in learning accurate data distributions that are hard to predict, including complex aspects of semantic information, art, naturalistic data, speech, electrodynamic quantum systems, among others. The paper suggests a generative assessable and noisy hybrid quantum AI quantification and suggests mixing adaptive quantum circuit design, error- Delhi trained, and multi-computer running. The adaptive circuit design minimizes the two-qubit gate interactions that enhances robustness and is still expressive. The mistake-conscious training is dynamically optimized to adjust circuit parameters to noise calibration at a constant-time, thus allowing it to operate stably with gate error rates up to 0.12%. To manage scalability, generative computation is spread among a number of relatively small quantum processors by the framework, with an almost linear scaling efficiency. On IBM quantum hardware, the model is demonstrated and validated on three applications; synthetic data generation to boost machine learning, medical image synthesis, and molecular structure generation to discover drugs. Findings indicate that quantum resource consumption is cut down by about 35 percent without compromising the generative quality, which proves that quantum-assisted AI systems can be used in the near future.

title: Design and Simulation of a CubeSat for Natural Disaster Detection Using Environmental Sensors
authors: Ayman Dhawan, Shweta Loonkar, Karishma Desai
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: This paper evaluates the design and evaluation of a CubeSat Prototype capable of detecting natural disasters such as floods, droughts, wildfires and cyclones by using an array of low cost onboard environmental sensors. A specific combination of sensors, such as humidity, light, temperature, pressure, intensity and a gyroscope, are employed depending on the type of disaster. Multiple environmental parameters beyond predefined thresholds, determine the detection of these natural disasters, adaptable to varying geographical conditions. CAD software is used to model the CubeSat's structural and sensor layout, and the orbital simulations are conducted to assess earth coverage and feasibility in lower Earth Orbit. The result of the hardware and software testing confirm that the CubeSat could transmit alert data to the nearby users within about five minutes of detecting the threshold. The results of the Prototype testing further confirm that the alerts were successfully delivered in 75% of cases, which in turn confirms performance reliability within the current system design. This CubeSat is a lightweight, modular and cost-efficient solution compared to the conventional land-based or high-cost airborne monitoring systems. The compact size of the CubeSat reduces launch expenses, while the modular nature of the sensor systems makes it adaptable across diverse environments. Besides this, the use of drag sail for controlled deorbiting further enhances sustainability. This CubeSat prototype offers a remarkably affordable solution in space-based monitoring systems to provide near real-time disaster alerts, especially in regions with limited ground infrastructure.

title: Effective Connectivity Alterations within the Cortico–Basal Ganglia Circuit Associated with Motor Skill Learning
authors: Mohammad Rezaei, Alireza Talesh Jafadideh, Fariba Bahrami, Shahzad Tahmasebi Boroujeni
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: We studied whether a short time training program leaves directionally specific changes within the resting-state cortico–basal ganglia network. Thirteen healthy adults trained for two weeks to draw shapes with their foot (mainly using knee joint movement). Speed rose by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 24 \%$</tex> and distance-to-target fell by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 15 \%$</tex> as reported in another study on the same dataset [1]. Resting-state fMRI was acquired before and after training periods. Using spectral dynamic causal modeling, we estimated directed coupling within a hypothesisdriven motor area, including supplementary motor area (SMA), primary motor cortex (M1), dorsal premotor cortex, thalamus, caudate, putamen, pallidum, and a cerebellar relay. Group analysis revealed selective changes: stronger right SMA → left M1 influence, stronger thalamus → striatal drive (to caudate and putamen), and stronger putamen → pallidum coupling; in contrast, left M1→left putamen decreased. These shifts suggest that practice increases top-down drive from planning to execution and reinforces basal ganglia pathways that favor the trained action, while down-weighting less efficient corticostriatal routes. The results provide directional evidence that even brief practice reorganizes cortico–basal ganglia communication at rest and align with models in which reinforcement- and error-based processes jointly shape skill.

title: MobMetrics: A Modular Architecture for Computing and Visualizing Mobility Metrics
authors: Márcio P. C. Filho, Lucas Novais, Leon L. Lausberg, Peter Sevenich, Paulo H. L. Rettore, Bruno P. Santos
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Software Defined Systems (SDS)
abstract: To support the analysis of mobility traces and the processing of mobility metrics, we propose MobMetrics, a web-based system designed to process mobility traces and extract comprehensive mobility metrics. The system is an opensource application that enables users to upload raw mobility data (geospatial or n-dimensional Cartesian data) and obtain a wide range of metrics, categorized into four groups: social, spatial, kinematic, and temporal. These metrics enable detailed pattern analyses of entities’ movement behavior. Furthermore, the platform integrates a metric-based multidimensionality reduction technique and graphs/charts to visualize both metrics and traces. As a modular, easy-to-extend, and open-source project, it allows users to add their own metrics and visualizations. We analyzed mobility traces with different characteristics by comparing mobile entities and their respective labels, aiming to analyze, compare, and cluster them.

title: IoT Smart Door Lock with Wireless Key Sharing for Short Term Multi-Level Building Access
authors: Bernardo Cabral Marques, Miguel L. Pardal, Rui Policarpo Duarte
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Internet of Things: Systems, Management and Security (IOTSMS)
abstract: IoT technology has rapidly grown in recent years, both in terms of research and product development. The usage of IoT technology is growing in a variety of Smart Home applications, such as Smart Home Security, as a result of the development of technologies like 5G network and BLE as well as the appearance of faster and more compact embedded systems. In this project, we implemented a Smart Door Lock capable of being controlled by the user's smartphone in a user-friendly and secure way, using BLE and IoT technology. This system focuses on capabilities that let the user remotely grant access to his Smart Door Lock, which makes it a great resource for rental housing environments. The final developed system could be given a level 4 classification on the Technology Readiness Level scale, which is equivalent to a technology validated in a lab, and it could easily be improved to a level 5 classification, which corresponds to a technology validated in a relevant environment.

title:
An X-Band CMOS Class-F
                    <sub>23</sub>
                    QVCO-Based PLL Using Drain-Capacitor-Coupled Technique
authors: Po-Yuan Chen, Jun-Liang Chen, Hong-Yeh Chang
publish: 2026 IEEE 26th Topical Meeting on Silicon Monolithic Integrated Circuits in RF Systems (SiRF)
abstract: In this paper, an X-band quadrature voltage-controlled oscillator (QVCO}-based phase-locked loop (PLL) is implemented using 0.18-µm CMOS process. The proposed QVCO not only utilizes Class-F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">23</inf> topology for each differential oscillator core to further minimize the noise contribution by waveform shaping, it also provides an innovative drain-capacitor-coupling methodology to generate quadrature signals for PLL implementation. Besides, a 2-bit switched-capacitor array (SCA) is adopted in the proposed QVCO for locking range (LR) extension. The proposed quadrature PLL demonstrates a lowest phase noise of -104 dBc/Hz at 1-MHz offset and a lowest rms jitter of 250 fs, which integrated from 1 kHz to 40 MHz. The chip size is 1.6×1.1 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, and the total dc power consumption is 68 mW. Compared to the reported silicon-based PLLs, this design achieves comparable performance and is suitable for advanced quadrature clock generations.

title: Integrating FusionNet-4 and WWGWO: A Novel UAV-Based Approach to Multi-Class Natural Disaster Classification
authors: Gourav Mondal, Rajesh Kumar Dhanaraj
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: UAV-MCND is a novel UAV-based multi-class natural disaster classification framework that marries the Hybrid WWGWO algorithm into the FusionNet-4 ensemble. The pipeline condescendingly goes into details regarding image acquisition, image pre-processing, optimization-based UNet segmentation, feature extraction, WWGWO feature selection, and classification. UnionNet-4 combines all levels of features extracted by ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, VGG-19, and EfficientNet for the better classification of disasters. WWGWO feature selection balances exploration and exploitation with Walrus Optimization, integrated into Water Wheel Plant Optimization to select features with the highest discriminative power. On evaluation of this system on Rescue Net and xView2 datasets, it is observed that it has far exceeded the level of accuracy at 98.9%, F1-score of 0.973, and MSE of 0.0135 (80:20 split) achieved by any other CNN and hybrid model coexisting in the research realm. An ablation study is noteworthy to realize the individual contribution of each component. This system thus ensures that it provides the much-needed robustness for real-time UAV deployments and outperforms all existing methods in accuracy, precision, and generalization for the whole gamut of disasters whose occurrence it can detect. Future deployment will integrate Vision Transformer architectures and multi-modal data sources for better situation awareness.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Deep Learning Architectures and Ensemble Strategies for Accurate Brain Tumor Segmentation
authors: Youssouf Bouzidi, Oussama Bouguerra, Youcef Brik, Bilal Attallah, Mohamed Djerioui, Ibraheem Shayeai, Zool Hilmi Ismail, Abdelwahhab Boudjelal, Ayyoub Berra
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: Accurate segmentation of brain tumors from Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a critical step in diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient monitoring. While manual segmentation remains the clinical standard, it is time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability. To address these limitations, this paper presents a comprehensive comparative evaluation of advanced deep learning architectures for automated tumor delineation. We systematically assess a range of models, from a baseline U-Net to state-of-the-art architectures such as Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), DeepLabV3+, and U-Net++, each enhanced with pre-trained encoders including EfficientNet and SE-ResNet50 to leverage transfer learning. In addition, two ensemble strategies—Voting and Stacking—are implemented and analyzed to further enhance segmentation accuracy. Experimental results show that the FPN with an EfficientNet-B4 encoder achieves the best performance among single models, reaching a Dice score of 0.8235, while the Stacking Ensemble attains the highest overall Dice score of 0.8263. These findings indicate that, although the Stacking Ensemble yields the most accurate segmentation, the FPN architecture offers a highly competitive and computationally efficient alternative for clinical deployment. The presented analysis provides a reliable benchmark and practical guidance for selecting appropriate models in brain tumor segmentation tasks.

title: GCT-LICalib: Targetless Online LiDAR–IMU Calibration via Ground Plane Motion Constraints and Continuous-Time Optimization
authors: Tongsheng He, Ping Wang, Debasis Das, B. T. Maharaj
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: Accurate extrinsic calibration is critical for high-performance LiDAR–IMU fusion in intelligent vehicles. Existing methods face two key limitations: offline, target-based approaches require fiducial patterns and controlled environments, making them impractical for real-world deployment; online targetless methods, though more flexible, typically assume rich 3D motion excitation—rarely satisfied during routine planar driving—leading to unobservable Z-axis extrinsic parameters. To address this, we propose GCT-LICalib, a targetless online calibration method that operates under standard planar motion by introducing a Ground-Plane Motion (GPM) constraint. This geometric prior leverages the structure of ground-constrained vehicle motion to compensate for insufficient vertical excitation. Within a continuous-time batch optimization framework, we model the IMU trajectory using B-splines and jointly optimize residuals from IMU preintegration, LiDAR point-to-surfel alignment, and the GPM constraint, naturally handling asynchronous measurements. Experiments on the M2DGR dataset demonstrate that GCT-LICalib achieves sub-centimeter translation and subdegree rotation accuracy using only ordinary driving trajectories.

title: Data driven approach for the electric vehicle charging using supervised machine learning
authors: Abhinav Saxena, Divya Verma, Dinesh Kumar Singh, Sheikh Safiullah, Swapnil Srivastava, Smriti Sachan
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The paper presents the electric vehicle (EV) charging using a machine learning controller. The huge sample of 1320 data have been taken for the EV charging station. Further, a supervised machine learning closed loop method in the EV charging system. Further, an objective function has been developed in terms of state of charge (SOC), voltage, battery size etc. This objective function will be trained by using machine learning method by considering the 1320 data set. The various pattern of SOC, charging time and other performance parameters like accuracy, and precision have been estimated with the method. It is observed that performance parameters like accuracy, and precision are found to be better with supervised machine learning method in comparison to existing methods. It also increases the range of SOC and charging time for the batter handling of EV with the consumers.

title: Virtual Modeling and Simulation of DC Motor Dynamics
authors: Abhishek S S, Anand P S, Akshatha T S, Vignesh V S, Sreeram K, Preetha P K
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Smart and Intelligent Systems (SISCON)
abstract: An emulator, a customized device replicating dynamic system or software functions, often incorporates Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs). FPGAs, chosen for benefits such as reduced power consumption, heightened processing speed, code reuse, and cost savings, surpass alternatives like microcontrollers, microprocessors, and Application Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs). Despite FPGA applicability varying based on specific demands, they can serve as a versatile test bed, even for Electric Vehicles (EV). This work details the development of a DC shunt motor emulator. Initial tests establish the continuous-time transfer function, leading to the creation of a discrete-time model. Utilizing a second-order digital Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter, the emulator is developed. The modeled speed and torque characteristics are compared with actual motor, revealing accuracy. This emulator stands out as a reliable test bed, promising applications across a spectrum of scenarios.

title: A Privacy-Preserving Blockchain and Federated Learning Model with Cryptographic Enhancements for IoT Security
authors: Rakesh Chandrashekar, Navdeep Singh, K.Mary, G NagaRaju, Yalla Jeevan Nagendra Kumar, Ashish Parmar
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The responsive merging layer successfully resolves the majority of dimensional mismatch problems when calculating combined user preference distributions. Real-world experiments extensively assessed the model's performance. The newly introduced Population-based Accuracy Improves Faster and Deeper Personalization (PA-ID-2P) algorithm optimizes recommendation models through effective reinforcement learning techniques. The recommendation system achieves better performance through supplementary training sessions and user interaction feedback mechanisms. The method of progressive refinement delivers more precise suggestions that match individual user requirements. Real-world data was used to assess the effectiveness of the proposed solution. Performance evaluation with real-world data indicates a 97.1% performance improvement. The results show our proposed framework surpasses state-of-the-art artificial intelligence and graph-based models and demonstrates better scalability. This makes it a viable option for adoption in digital and large-scale settings, effectively delivering context-aware and actionable recommendations.

title: Early Detection of Atrial Fibrillation using Real-Time Healthcare Analytics
authors: Km Meenakshi, Sanyukta Negi, Pradeep K. Kushwaha
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The most prevalent cardiac arrhythmia is atrial fibrillation (AFib), which is occurring in more than 33 million individuals worldwide and is high risk of developing the disease in heart conditions and death. Conventional methods of detection are characterized by periodic observations, in most cases overlooking paroxysmal events. Detecting early AFib with the help of uninterrupted physiological measurements and machine learning systems is a new real-time healthcare analytics framework presented in this paper. The algorithms of our system combine several data types, such as electrocardiogram (ECG), photoplethysmography (PPG), and metadata information about a patient into one unit to offer an opportunity of continuous risk evaluation and early warning functions. The proposed architecture has a sensitivity of 94.2 percent, specificities of 91.8 percent and a total accuracy of 93.1 percent in between AFib episodes and the detection latency of the system was 12.3 seconds on average. The system has a high potential of enhancing patient outcomes by intervening early in their lives and constantly serving on their monitoring.

title: Smart Assistive System for Pothole Screening using Lightweight CNN with Alert Generation
authors: Arijit Das, Madhuchhanda Dasgupta, Dalia Nandi, Oishila Bandopadhyay
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Potholes on the road pose significant challenges for independent navigation of visually impaired persons. Road damage may cause fatal accidents for these individuals. To overcome this problem, a smart assistive system is proposed in this work for pothole detection and alert generation. A novel lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture is designed to classify potholes in real-time video. The system takes each frame from real-time video captured by RGB camera as input to identify the potholes. After pothole classification, an auditory alert is generated to inform the user about the potholes. The proposed model is specifically designed to balance low latency and high reliability by making the model lightweight. The model has achieved more than 95% accuracy and precision. This work contributes to the development of assistive technologies that can enable greater mobility to visually impaired persons.

title: Enhancing Smart Grid Stability Using Adaptive Nonlinear PID Control
authors: Mohamed Shili, Salah Hammedi, Amjad Gawanmeh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources in today's power grids introduces considerable nonlinear dynamics, limiting the effectiveness of conventional linear PID controllers in stabilizing grid performance. In this paper, we present an adaptive nonlinear PID control strategy that adjusts the controller gains dynamically based on real-time measurements from the Smart Grid Stability augmented dataset. The proposed controller uses nonlinear estimation methods and explores spatio-temporal data patterns in the Smart Grid Stability dataset to help maintain stability in voltage and frequency amid time-varying renewable generation and load disturbances. Simulations using the Smart Grid Stability Augmented Dataset show that the adaptive nonlinear PID performs better than traditional PID and fixed-gain nonlinear controllers regarding transient response, robustness, and overall stability. This study presents a new approach to using nonlinear adaptive PID controllers in smart grids and presents a data-driven framework that can be generalized to other renewable-intensive electrical systems.

title: Sentiment Classification of Gofood Reviews with IndoBert: A Case Study of Food Ordering Apps in Indonesia
authors: Bisri, Rezki Yunanda
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: This study focuses on developing a sentiment classification system for GoFood customer reviews using the IndoBERT model. The research began by collecting a dataset consisting of 210,621 customer reviews from various digital services, which was then filtered to focus only on GoFood- related reviews. To improve data quality, preprocessing techniques were applied, including text cleaning, normalization, and semi-supervised learning through pseudo-labeling and self- training. After refinement, a final dataset of 6,324 reviews was obtained. The classification model was trained using IndoBERT and compared against several baseline models, including CNN, SVM, XGBoost, and Naive Bayes. The model’s performance was evaluated using standard classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1score. The results showed that IndoBERT outperformed the other models, achieving the highest accuracy of $96 \%$, highlighting the effectiveness of transformer-based models in sentiment analysis of Indonesian- language customer reviews.

title: Generation of Synthetic Data of Barley Grass Growth using Generative Adversarial Network
authors: Ferry Wahyu Wibowo, Akhmad Dahlan, Muhammad Koprawi, Wihayati
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: The limited availability of representative longitudinal datasets is a significant challenge in developing artificial intelligence models for barley growth analysis, primarily because field measurements are expensive, time-consuming, destructive, and difficult to perform at high temporal resolution. This study aims to develop a generative approach based on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) as a solution for generating realistic and biologically consistent synthetic datasets, which can be used as substitutes or complements to real data. The implemented solution includes plant height, tissue water content, and chlorophyll, which can utilize the optimized GAN. This model was optimized through feature normalization, a sigmoid activation, a low learning rate, increased generator capacity, and label smoothing to reduce training instability. The test results showed excellent reconstruction performance, with Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and coefficient of determination ($\mathbf{R}^{2}$) of 2.402 and 0.830 for plant height, 7.381 and 0.918 for water content, and 0.072 and 0.936 for chlorophyll, respectively. These values indicate that the model can accurately maintain the global growth structure and temporal dynamics of each key parameter. These findings demonstrate that a properly configured GAN approach can generate synthetic plant growth datasets that are statistically equivalent to field data, thus having great potential for use in predictive model training, scenario-based precision agronomic simulations, and improving the efficiency of sustainable agricultural research.

title: Improving the Accuracy and Reliability of Autonomous Vehicle Navigation Through Deep Learning Innovations
authors: Rachna Rathore, Jay Singh, Shikha Yadav, R Sujitha, Anandakumar Haldorai, Sheela S
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: In the evolving landscape of autonomous vehicles, this project investigates advanced deep learning technologies that enable high-level navigation capabilities. Our goal is to address the inherent complexity and challenges of autonomous navigation by integrating state-of-the-art algorithms and innovative approaches, including end-to-end learning systems, reinforcement learning, and multi-sensor fusion. This study is significant as it demonstrates how modern deep learning methodologies can substantially enhance decision-making, adaptability, and overall system performance in diverse and dynamic environments. Despite rapid advancements in autonomous vehicle technology, navigating dense and unpredictable surroundings remains a major challenge. This project explores cutting-edge deep learning techniques to significantly improve autonomous navigation. By examining recent methods such as end-to-end learning, reinforcement learning, simulation-to-real transfer learning, multi-sensor fusion, and neural architecture search, we aim to advance the perception, interpretation, and environmental interaction capabilities of autonomous systems. Our findings propose a unified deep learning framework that strengthens decision-making, increases adaptability to varying conditions, and improves the overall safety and efficiency of autonomous transportation. The results highlight the critical role of deep learning in overcoming current limitations and provide valuable insights into the future of autonomous vehicle navigation and its potential to transform urban mobility.

title: Multi-Variable Genetic Algorithm Optimization of Algal Bioethanol Production: A Dual-Strain Simulation Approach
authors: Samsurizal, Yulisya Zuriatni, Muhammad Zulham Kentjie, Rudina Okvasari, Septianissa Azzahra, Iwa Garniwa Mulyana K
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: The increasing demand for sustainable energy has led to intensified research on bioethanol production from renewable sources. Microalgae, specifically Chlorella vulgaris and Spirulina platensis, have shown significant potential for bioethanol generation due to their high biomass productivity and carbohydrate content. This study presents a dual-strain simulation approach to optimize bioethanol production using a Genetic Algorithm (GA). Environmental parameters such as $\mathbf{p H}$, light intensity, and temperature were considered for optimization. The GA was implemented separately for each strain to determine the optimal cultivation conditions that maximize ethanol yield. The simulation results demonstrated that Chlorella vulgaris improved from $51.00 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ to $63.75 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L} (+12.75 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L},+25 \%)$, while Spirulina platensis increased from $\mathbf{3 2 . 6 4 ~ g} / \mathrm{L}$ to $\mathbf{4 0 . 8 0 ~ g} / \mathrm{L}(+\mathbf{8 . 1 6 ~ g} / \mathrm{L},+\mathbf{2 5 \%})$ under GA-optimized conditions. These outcomes confirm the ability of computational optimization to significantly enhance strainspecific bioethanol productivity. However, the results are based on simulation assumptions—such as fixed carbohydrate fractions and ideal environmental stability—which may not fully capture biological variability in real-world cultivation. This work provides a computational framework to improve microalgae-based bioethanol systems and supports the development of adaptive, green energy technologies, with future directions including experimental validation and multi-objective optimization (yield, cost, and emissions).

title: An AI-Assisted Approach to Patient-Specific 3D Modeling and Stress Analysis of the Temporomandibular Joint from CBCT Images
authors: Mohammad Akhlghi, Masoud Shariat Panahi, Sina Salehpour, Morad Karimpour, Hadi Ghatan Kashani
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Accurate assessment of temporomandibular joint (TMJ) biomechanics requires patient-specific models that capture anatomical variability with high fidelity. This study presents an AI-assisted method for biomechanical modeling and stress analysis of the TMJ, integrating deep learning-based segmentation and 3D reconstruction from cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT). CBCT scans are processed using a pretrained UNET-R network, fine-tuned with a small dataset to segment the mandible, maxilla, and temporal bone. The segmented geometries are reconstructed into 3D models, converted into volumetric finite element (FE) meshes, and combined with a modeled TMJ disc representing the soft tissue between the mandibular condyle and maxilla. The FE model is then subjected to simulated incisal and unilateral molar loading to estimate stress distribution within the disc. The proposed method enables rapid and reproducible generation of patientspecific TMJ biomechanical models, reducing manual segmentation effort and computational cost while preserving clinical accuracy. This approach demonstrates strong potential for personalized diagnosis, treatment planning, and biomechanical research on TMJ disorders.

title: Force-Length Behavior in Dynamic Exercise
authors: Erfan Farahani, Manizheh Zakeri, Mohammad-Reza Sayyed Noorani
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: The force-length characteristics of the biceps from resistance exercise training were studied, and the most important role of range of motion (ROM) under three contraction conditions <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0-90^{\circ}, 90-140^{\circ}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-140^{\circ}$</tex> during elbow flexion) was presented during the standing dumbbell biceps curl. Muscle force and mechanical tension are important for Hill-based muscle modeling, and ROM is an important determinant of both. A total of eight controlled repetitions were carried out by five healthy male subjects with a variety of training experiences. Motion capture data were collected with a Motion Analysis system and processed in OpenSim with a modified Arm26 model to extract elbow joint kinematics and normalized biceps muscle lengths. These lengths were filtered and used to calculate the active force-length modifier, which generated continuous force-length curves for the long and short heads of the biceps for all ROMs. The methodology produced stable physiologically realistic force-length relationships and was shown to be a reliable method for use in resistance training analysis. In summary, the workflow provides a practical reference for the quantification of muscle mechanics and can be used to understand the influence of ROM on muscle force in Hill-based models and inform evidence-based training protocols.

title: Multi-Objective Deep Reinforcement Learning for Personalized Autonomous Driving: Advances, Challenges, and Future Directions
authors: Jay Jithendra, Naman Kumar, Rashmi Naveen Raj
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: Intelligent transportation system is a synergistic integration of sensor technologies, internet of things, artificial intelligence, communication technologies, and big data analytics, collectively enabling optimization of traffic flow and mobility, improvement of safety, and significant reduction of congestion. Autonomous driving is an application made possible by an intelligent transportation system that can overcome the shortcomings of the conventional driving system. Moreover, it indirectly improves the productivity by mitigating the congestion, reducing fuel consumption, and lowering CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emission thereby contributing to a sustainable and environmentally as well as the user-friendly transportation ecosystem. Reinforcement learning has emerged as promising solution to automate the sequential-decision making strategies of autonomous driving. But, the traditional reinforcement learning algorithms are better suitable for small state-action space environments and are single-objective in nature. For realworld deployment, autonomous driving systems necessitate user preference-aware frameworks to effectively balance competing criteria and enhance personalized driving experiences. Hence, this paper focuses on the advancements in multi-objective deep reinforcement learning for various decision-making scenarios, such as path planning, speed control, and steering angle adjustment, etc. as well as for developing personalized driving strategies. This survey presents the advances, research challenges and future scopes on a timely topic and serves as a good reference for the researchers.

title: Comprehensive Design and Analysis of Overcurrent Relay Protection and Coordination for a 220 MW Grid-Connected Power Plant
authors: Salim Djeriou, Assia Bounif, Abderrahim Zemmit, Ismail Ghadbane, Riyadh Rouabhi, Mouiad Naidji
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: Conducted a comprehensive study on the development and protection of a 220 MW grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) power plant using ETAP software. The research encompassed full system modeling, including PV array configuration, inverter selection, and voltage step-up transformer design, to ensure optimal performance and grid compatibility.Performed detailed fault analysis and developed relay coordination and protection zone detection strategies to enhance system reliability and stability. Emphasis was placed on overcurrent relay coordination, fault detection, and system behavior under multiple fault conditions.Simulation results confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed coordinated protection schemes in maintaining grid reliability, preventing equipment damage, and supporting the seamless integration of large-scale renewable energy sources into the Algerian power network.

title: Bridging the Gap: Patient Education and Self-Management in Knee Osteoarthritis
authors: Shivangi Pathania, Navjyot Trivedi, Chander Prabha, Durgesh Nandan, Shilpa Hudnurkar
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Knee Osteoarthritis (KOA) is a progressive joint disorder and a leading cause of pain and disability among older adults worldwide. While conventional treatment often focuses on surgery and medication, recent research highlights the importance of patient education in KOA management. Our literature review examines the role of patient education within physiotherapy programs for KOA, based on studies published globally in Scopus-indexed journals between 2015 and 2025. Evidence shows that combining exercise therapy with structured education improves pain, mobility, quality of life, and patient confidence in managing symptoms. Programs like GLA:D and STEP-KOA demonstrate that education delivered in clinical or digital formats encourages long-term behavior change. The review also discusses key factors for successful interventions, including patient readiness, digital literacy, and barriers such as travel limitations or low health literacy. Despite positive findings, gaps remain in culturally adaptable, early-stage KOA education suitable for diverse populations. The review emphasizes the need to integrate information technology, behavioral science, and patient preferences into KOA care. Overall, patient education is a crucial, cost-effective, and sustainable addition to physiotherapy for KOA , empowering individuals to actively manage their condition and improve outcomes.

title: Generalized Class-X Power Amplifier Operating Under Inverse Class-E Harmonic Constraints
authors: Sheetal Verma, Y. Mary Asha Latha, Jayanta Mukherjee
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: This paper presents a novel design methodology for a high-efficiency power amplifier (PA) that effectively combines the generalized Class-X theory of maximally flat waveforms with the specific constraints of the Inverse Class-E (Class-E-1) PA. By enforcing the Inverse Class-E conditions on the second <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(Z_{2}\right)$</tex> and third (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Z_{3}$</tex>) harmonic load impedances, which were selected from the available design space, and analytically solving constrained Class-X equations to determine the single optimal fundamental load (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Z_{1}$</tex>). This approach guarantees the waveform flatness necessary for maximizing the theoretical efficiency of PA and improving robustness against device parasitics. A PA prototype, designed using a GaN HEMT at 1 GHz frequency, was simulated using the Harmonic Balance technique. The results demonstrate a peak Drain Efficiency of around 82 % and an Output Power of 41 dBm. Furthermore, the drain voltage waveform verification confirms the desired maximally flat transition region, validating the effectiveness of the hybrid Class-X Waveform Engineering technique.

title: A Peer-to-Peer Blockchain Protocol for Secure and Anonymous V2G Energy Trading
authors: Amrendra Singh Yadav, Ayashkant Mishra, Niharika Keshari, Deepti Mishra, Nachiketa Tarasia, Rabindra Kumar Barik
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) networks are emerging as a crucial component of modern smart grid (SG) systems, enabling bidirectional energy exchange between electric vehicles (EVs) and charging stations (CSs). This capability helps stabilize energy demand while contributing to broader environmental and social objectives. However, integrating V2G systems presents serious security and privacy challenges. Key concerns include preserving data integrity, ensuring trustworthy mutual authentication, and protecting participants' identities. Existing approaches are still limited, and the reliance on centralized structures leaves networks vulnerable to single points of failure. This study introduces a blockchain-based framework for secure energy trading within V2G networks to overcome these issues. The framework incorporates three core entities: EVs, CSs, and a Utility Center (UC) and is organized into three main phases: registration, search, and authentication. In the registration phase, the identities of EVs and CSs are securely protected. The search phase streamlines participant enrollment and facilitates key generation. During authentication, both parties confirm each other's legitimacy, and transactions are immutably recorded on the blockchain through Merkle Root Hash validation. Security analysis demonstrates the framework's resilience against common cyberattacks. At the same time, performance evaluation confirms its scalability and efficiency in real-world V2G environments, achieving competitive results regarding communication overhead, computational cost, registration time, and authentication performance.

title: Implementation of RAFT Algorithm and BadgerDB to Improve Data Transactions in Multi Group Sharded Cluster
authors: Pragya Vaishnav, Ningthoujam Avichandra, Linesh Raja
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Recently, data has become a valuable asset for any kind of organization. The fundamental requirement of the organization is to keep the data secure and consistent. It is necessary to reduce the risk of data loss in the case of a failed system. In this paper, the author developed a RCBClutser for a solution for data consistency in cloudnative databases to implement the technologies and techniques: BadgerDB used to read-heavy workloads and support for fully serializable transactions. RAFT applied to provide consistency and fault tolerance. Jump Consistent Hash was implemented to distribute key value evenly among storage buckets. Multi-Group RAFT provided scalability and greater flexibility to the proposed work. This approach is capable of providing high performance in data transactions without any failure. Tables 1 and 2 present the data storage and retrieval processes in milliseconds with keyvalue.

title: Improved Efficiency of Selective Active Filtering of the Dominant Harmonic Currents Using a Robust PQ Technique-Based Single-Phase APF
authors: Badreddine Babes, Noureddine Hamouda, Assam Zorig, Badi Ridha, Khaled Belhouchet, Uwe Rädel, Anwar Zorig
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: This work presents a single-phase active power filter (SP-APF) controlled using a modified P-Q theory for selective harmonic suppression and power factor correction. The proposed approach integrates a self-tuning filter (STF) within the instantaneous power theory framework to accurately and selectively extract the harmonic reference components of the load current. A major advantage of the STF is its ability to directly isolate the active and reactive power components associated with specific harmonic currents, enabling targeted mitigation of dominant low-order harmonics such as the 3rd, 5th, and 7th. The DC-link voltage is regulated by a proportional-integral (PI) regulator to ensure stable and fast dynamic behavior, while a hysteresis current controller is used to drive the voltage-source inverter (VSI). MATLAB/Simulink simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm in compensating harmonic currents both individually and simultaneously, achieving a reduction in total harmonic distortion (THD) to approximately 5%.

title: Quantifying the Impact of Data Scale and Quality on Neural Machine Translation for Low-Resource Language Pair
authors: Ningthoujam Avichandra Singh, Boynao Kshetrimayum, Ningthoujam Justwant Singh, Thoudam Doren Singh, Shilpa Sharma
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: This paper analyzes a Transformer Architecture model for Manipuri-English machine translation, focusing on data quality and size. We leverage Self-Attention Mechanism, Multi-Head Attention, and Positional Encoding. Translation quality is assessed using BLEU, chrF, TER, and qualitative evaluations. Our findings indicate that larger, even lower-quality, datasets yield better performance than smaller, highquality ones for this low-resource language. Experiments with datasets of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17,366,33,744$</tex>, and 76,454 samples resulted in BLEU scores of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.9,14.5$</tex>, and 16.4, respectively, demonstrating significant translation quality improvements with increased data, even with varied quality.

title: Breast Cancer Classification Using Digitized Mammograms with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: A Sujin Jose, Jhansi Vazram Bolla, G.Kaushik, Dinesh Kumar Yadav, Ankam Sreejyothsna, Ginni Nijhawan
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Breast cancer is a major killer of women, therefore finding it early is crucial for better possibilities for therapy and a greater chance of surviving. Although mammograms is a common diagnosis tool, it has limitations due to factors like noise, limited contrary, and the fact that thick healthy tissues and cancerous ones look very similar. This study introduces a CAD system that uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) to distinguish between normal, benign, and malicious digital mammography. The suggested framework uses mammography datasets like DDSM and MIAS to perform sophisticated image processing, extraction of features and classifications. Convolutional, pooling, fully linked, and softmax layers make up the DCNN architecture, which is fine-tuned using hyperparameters to achieve its optimum efficiency. Reducing and eliminating are image processing methods that guarantee high-quality input data. There is no longer any need for human feature engineers thanks to the end-to-end DCNN model's integration of features prediction and categorisation. The goal of the model is to decrease the subjective and unpredictability of human interpretations in breast cancer detection by using labelled datasets and contemporary computing capabilities to aid radiologist. By demonstrating how DCNN-based CAD applications can improve the repeatability and dependability of breast cancer identification and grouping, this study shows how diagnostics procedures can be transformed. On both the MIAS and DDSM datasets, the suggested DCNN approach outperforms all others with a flawless overall accuracy of 100%.

title: Addressing Class Imbalance Using Difficulty-based Oversampling with Variance Control
authors: Zahra Asgharzadeh Bonab, Sina Shamekhi
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Learning from imbalanced data is one of the difficult challenges in the field of deep learning and computer vision. Despite continuous research progress in the last few decades, learning from data with an imbalanced distribution of classes remains an important and active area of research. In real-world problems, imbalanced data often limits the applicability and practicality of deep models. The aim of this paper is to introduce a novel solution to deal with this problem in a targeted and applicable format for classification. This paper presents an innovative oversampling strategy that operates directly in feature space, rather than in pixel space. Unlike common methods such as SMOTE or ADASYN, this approach utilizes Instance Hardness level and Adversarial Neighborhood standard deviation to generate artificial instances that are aware of and fit the embedding distribution. Experimental results on three known image datasets show that this approach improves minority class recall and increases overall classification stability. The proposed approach is evaluated using deep learning architectures as embedding extractors and emphasizes the importance of different feature-embedding representations. The approach is model-independent and offers superior performance in terms of both effectiveness and computational efficiency compared to similar approaches.

title: Modeling method for multi hazard coupling in power distribution system
authors: Dexiang Li, Ling Li, Huijie Sun, Yunhua Qin, Li Luo
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: To improve the power supply guarantee capability under extreme weather conditions, this paper prosents an optimization model for site selection and capacity determination of distributed wind-solar-storage resources in pre-disaster stage of extreme events. Batts model for typhoon wind speed and the failure rate model were established, and the Monte Carlo method was used to simulate the fault scenario and obtain the fault line; The optimization in pre-disaster stage aims to minimize economic costs and network losses. The adaptive discrete particle swarm optimization algorithm is used to solve the optimization model for distributed site selection and capacity determination of wind-solar-storage enengy.By comparing and analyzing the load recovery results and economics of different strategies through numerical examples, the feasibility and superiority of the model proposed in this paper were verified.

title: Mesoscopic Behavior of Single-Carrier Avalanche in Narrow-Bandgap Semiconductors
authors: Shuning Liu, Runzhang Xie, Qianlong Kang, Haonan Ge, Min Luo, Anna Liu, Hao Xie, Lu Chen, Xiwen Liu, Qing Li, Weida Hu
abstract: The mesoscopic stochasticity of single-carrier avalanche is the dominant factor governing macroscopic noise in narrow-bandgap avalanche photodiodes (APDs). In mercury cadmium telluride (HgCdTe) devices, this randomness induces local electric field fluctuations that trigger gain-limiting band-to-band tunneling (BBT) noise. Traditional macroscopic models inherently average out these statistical fluctuations, while purely microscopic simulations are often computationally prohibitive for iterative design. To bridge this gap, this work presents a micro–mesoscopic coupled Monte Carlo framework developed to analyze avalanche chain dynamics at the critical mesoscopic scale. The analysis reveals that the avalanche statistics are governed by a coupled, 2-D parameter space defined by the spatial standard deviation (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol {\sigma }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) and the mean free path (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol {\ell }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). We demonstrate that the macroscopic signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is determined by a complex competition among multiple noise mechanisms, involving Auger, BBT, phonon, and excess noise mechanisms within this parameter space. Guided by this analysis, an acceleration meso-lattice (AML) structure is proposed to actively engineer the mesoscopic state near a precisely optimal operating point. By regulating carrier dynamics to suppress stochastic fluctuations at their origin, the AML structure suppresses the physical conditions for BBT noise and achieves significant SNR enhancement. This research shifts the design paradigm from macroscopic geometric optimization to direct mesoscopic dynamics regulation, offering a new physical pathway to transcend fundamental device limits.

authors: N/A
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Software Defined Systems (SDS)

title: EMPC-RIS: Failure Diagnosis Method for Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces Based On Electromagnetic Physical Constraints
authors: Jiansheng Bai, Jinjie Yao, Yeye Liu, Kun Li, Xingcheng Han, Liming Wang
abstract: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS), as the core technology for 6G wireless communications and Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC), play a vital role in enhancing signal quality and improving communication efficiency. To address the critical issue of damaged or contaminated RIS elements during design and deployment, this paper proposes a failure diagnosis method based on Electromagnetic Physical Constraints (EMPC). First, a low-dimensional measurement matrix is generated via spatial encoding of sparse signals from impaired RIS elements, subject to physical constraints imposed by the RF device structure. Subsequently, to accurately model the multi-modal characteristics of signal distributions in multipath channels, a Scaled Gaussian Mixture (SMG) model is adopted to fit the distribution of sparse coefficients. Leveraging the spatial structural correlation within the RIS array, a center-oriented parameter propagation acceleration strategy is designed, significantly enhancing computational efficiency. Building upon this foundation, the model parameters of the RIS are mapped onto a Riemannian manifold using Information Geometry (IG) theory. This transforms the fault diagnosis problem into one of measuring the information distance between coordinates on the manifold. A fast Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD)-based difference detector is designed to rapidly diagnose the locations and quantity of failed RIS elements. The experimental evaluation comprehensively validates the method's performance through extensive numerical simulations (encompassing noise impacts, device parameters, diverse fault modes, and channel models) and practical measurements in a microwave anechoic chamber. The results are analyzed from multiple dimensions to discusss the method's effectiveness, robustness, and practical applicability.

title: Conference Calendar
authors: N/A

title: Speech Recognition for Access Control: A Comparative Analysis of MFCC and PLP Features using CNN-Based Classification
authors: Salah Khennouf, Adil Bakri, Imad Eddine Benamor, Boudjellal Fadi, Youcef Brik
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: Speaker recognition has emerged as a promising biometric approach for secure and contactless access control. This paper presents a comparative analysis of two widely used acoustic feature extraction techniques Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) and Perceptual Linear Predictive (PLP) coefficients for speaker recognition systems based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The proposed system is designed to classify speaker identities by analyzing short voice samples in a controlled access scenario. Both MFCC and PLP features were extracted from a curated dataset and used to train identical CNN architectures to ensure fair performance evaluation. The models were assessed based on accuracy, precision, recall, and training convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that while both feature sets provide strong classification performance, MFCC features yielded slightly higher accuracy and faster training convergence in our tests. These findings highlight the effectiveness of CNNs in voice-based biometric systems and provide valuable insights into feature selection for real-time, secure access control applications.

title: 3D Construction and Realignment of Object for Interaction in Metaverse Space
authors: Chirag Garg, Manali Arora
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: In this paper, we introduce an innovative AI/ML-based approach for dynamically constructing and adapting 3D user avatars and objects within Metaverse environments, enhancing immersive interactions. The methodology centers on leveraging machine learning algorithms to continuously monitor user behavior and interaction patterns in real-time, enabling automatic scaling, realignment, and repositioning of avatars and objects based on the specific activities performed by the user, including objects purchased in the Metaverse relative to the user's body avatar. The system tracks and analyzes various physical and contextual factors, such as user movements, gestures, and environmental changes, applying learned models to adjust avatar and object characteristics in alignment with user actions and context. This ensures interactions feel natural and intuitive, improving the virtual experience. Additionally, the method incorporates a feedback loop, where system adjustments are evaluated and refined based on user responses and activity outcomes, fostering continuous improvement in real-time interactions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly enhances the realism, responsiveness, and immersion of avatars and objects in the Metaverse, delivering a more engaging and interactive virtual reality experience.

title: Designing Iot-Enabled Automatic Inventory Management System for Business Purposes
authors: Shivasri Bhavana, Padmalaya Nayak, Saraswati Bhakare, A. Ushasree, Phaneendra Babu Bobba, J Siva Naga Jyothi
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Inventory management systems are very crucial for businesses to control their stock, keep a track of their stock, and optimize inventory levels to meet users' demand while minimizing costs. In this regard, we have proposed an inventory management system with very low cost by exploiting the properties of the Internet of Things (IoT) and a cloud server. Additionally, we have used an HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Sensor, an Arduino Nano Microcontroller, a white breadboard, and a laptop to design the automatic Inventory management system. The HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Sensor is used to sense and detect objects, connected to the Arduino Nano. When any item is moved from the inventory, the sensor senses and sends a signal to the microcontroller. A Python script is run on the same port continuously as a listener connected to the Arduino Nano. The Python code is invoked, which calls a pickup Application Programming Interface (API). This API is designed using Oracle APEX to update the inventory status of the corresponding item from “In Stock” to “Picked Up” in the cloud database. All backend services, including the database, REST APIs, and monitoring dashboard, are built using Oracle APEX. The dashboard offers real-time inventory insights, enabling users to track items that are picked up, shipped, or in stock. This end-to-end flow enables efficient, automated inventory tracking with minimal manual intervention. Finally, A laptop is used to monitor the inventory status.

title: Finite Element Study on Bone Quality and Boundary Conditions
authors: Fatima Wayzani, Mohammadjavad Matin Einafshar, Ata Hashemi
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Background: Dental implant stability is critical to long-term implant success and is influenced by bone quality, implant geometry, and boundary conditions. Modal analysis provides a non-invasive method to assess implant stability through evaluation of the system's natural frequencies. Objective: This study investigates how boundary constraints, mandible size, bone quality, and bone-substitutive layers affect the natural frequency of dental implants. Materials and Methods: A three-dimensional finite element model of a biphasic healing titanium dental implant—defined here as an implant initially engaging both cortical and cancellous (trabecular) bone—was developed using micro-CT data. Two mandibular geometries (“long” and “short”) were modeled. Three boundary conditions were applied by fixing the mandible at (1) mesial/distal sides, (2) the inferior surface, and (3) all three sides. Bone quality was categorized using the Lekholm and Zarb classification: •Type I: dense cortical bone only •Type II: thick cortical and dense trabecular bone •Type III: thin cortical and dense trabecular bone •Type IV: thin cortical and sparse trabecular bone Additionally, bone-substitutive layers (magnesium phosphate, hydroxyapatite, PMMA) at two thicknesses (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0 0} \boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex> m and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 0 0} \mu \mathrm{m})$</tex> were included. Results: Natural frequencies were significantly higher in the short mandible model (e.g., <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$13,959 ~\text{Hz}$</tex> vs. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3,826 ~\text{Hz}$</tex> for bone type IV). Increased boundary constraint raised the first mode frequency from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,786 ~\text{Hz}$</tex> (one side fixed) to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5,650 ~\text{Hz}$</tex> (three sides fixed). The highest frequencies occurred in Type I bone (up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$83,958 ~\text{Hz}$</tex>). Bone-substitutive layers caused frequency changes of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<1 \%$</tex>, indicating minimal impact. Conclusions: Bone quality is a dominant factor in implant stability, with denser bone yielding higher frequencies. Boundary condition modeling significantly affects frequency results. Thin bone-substitute layers do not notably influence vibrational response, although they remain important for osseointegration.

title: Physics-Guided BiLSTM-Attn Network for RCS Prediction of Non-Standard Road Targets
authors: Kaibo Feng, Ying Zhan, Ming Wang, Gaofei Fan
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: Radar Cross Section (RCS) is a key physical quantity that characterizes a target's ability to reflect incident electromagnetic waves. The angular sequence of RCS can offer critical feature information for target identification, which is often lost due to sparse point clouds. To address the limitations of traditional RCS measurement methods, such as low data quality and high cost/time-consuming processes, this study proposes a physics-guided deep learning-based prediction method. The approach first employs the electromagnetic simulation software FEKO for 3D modeling and data acquisition, generating high-quality RCS data as a substitute for physical measurements. The RCS prediction is then formulated as a sequence-to-sequence task, where a BiLSTM-Attn network (Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory with Attention mechanism) is trained on a noise-corrupted dataset. The attention mechanism functions as an adaptive weighting mechanism, selectively focusing on informative sequence elements and thereby eliminating the need for complex signal post-processing typically required in conventional measurements. Comparative experiments with baseline models demonstrate that the proposed network maintains superior robustness and prediction accuracy even under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions, providing a solid foundation for subsequent practical deployment.

title: Efficiency Assessment of Human Motion Detection with Multi-Class SVM Approach
authors: Suhina Majumder, Agrima Dixit, Abhinav Pathak, Parag Ravikant Kaveri, Ali Assafi Mahamat, Aanya Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) is achieved using sensor data to classify six activities: walking, walking upstairs, walking downstairs, sitting, standing, and lying. The model being used was a Multi-Class SVM trained on prepared statistical features, employing a Polynomial Kernel, and then validated on one participant, used for testing instead of participants that were previously seen. This demonstrates the power of the model and its real-world usages, not only for animation timing, but also for establishing judgment of appraisal terminal on the aspects of clinical measures. A multiClass SVM was an effective classifier in modelling the non-linear patterns described by motion data, and subsequently had accurate predictive ability. As compared to simpler classifiers, an SVM was also more generalizable to participant’s unseen from training, further highlighting real-world capabilities of human activity recognition.

title: A Holistic Survey of IoMT Security: Integrating Machine Learning, Federated Learning, and Explainable AI
authors: Hazik Khurshid Zargar, Archita Bhattacharyya, Mrinal Kanti Deb Barma, Rabindra K. Barik
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) represents a paradigm shift in healthcare delivery, enabling continuous remote monitoring and personalized patient care through interconnected medical devices. However, this interconnectivity introduces significant cybersecurity vulnerabilities that threaten patient safety and data privacy. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of IoMT security challenges and proposes an integrated framework that combines Machine Learning (ML), Federated Learning (FL), and Explainable AI (XAI) for robust cybersecurity defense. Our systematic review examines current threats across device, network, and server layers, analyzes recent attack vectors targeting Cardiac Implantable Electronic Devices (CIEDs), and evaluates emerging security solutions. The proposed framework addresses critical vulnerabilities while maintaining regulatory compliance with FDA guidelines, GDPR, and HIPAA standards. Through the analysis of recent cybersecurity incidents that affect healthcare systems and the examination of protection mechanisms, this study contributes to the advancement of secure healthcare systems. The integrated approach demonstrates superior performance in threat detection while preserving patient privacy through federated learning architectures and ensuring transparency through explainable AI mechanisms.

title: ACP-Net: A Steel Defect Detection Network Based on Multi-Scale Fusion and Precise Localization
authors: Bangde Cao, Yanfang Yin, Changwenyan Chen, Zhixuan Chen, Yilin Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: In modern industrial production processes, steel is susceptible to various surface defects due to multiple factors such as processing technology and external environment. Detection of steel surface defects is crucial for ensuring product quality and performance and reducing safety hazards. Addressing the challenges of losing minute defect features, large scale variations in steel defects, and highly irregular defect morphologies in steel surface defect detection, this paper proposes a steel defect detection network based on multi-scale fusion and precise localization. First, an adaptive downsampling module, ADown, is introduced in the feature extraction stage. Its parallel pooling and convolution path fusion strategy effectively alleviates the information loss problem caused by traditional downsampling, ensuring a high retention rate for minute defects and fine-grained features. Second, at the feature fusion level, we design a coordinate attention-weighted bidirectional feature pyramid network, CA-HSFPN. This network not only achieves adaptive fusion of multi-scale features through learnable weights, enhancing the model’s robustness to defects of different scales, but also utilizes a coordinate attention mechanism to accurately capture the spatial location information of defects, significantly suppressing interference from complex backgrounds such as steel surface reflections and textures. Finally, in the bounding box regression stage, the MPDIoU loss function is adopted. By directly minimizing the diagonal distance between the predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, the localization accuracy and convergence speed for irregularly shaped defects with abnormal aspect ratios are effectively improved. Experimental results show that mAP@0.5 reaches 77.59% and 79.15% on the NEU-DET and GC10-DET datasets, respectively, and mAP@0.75 is 3.78% and 8.59% higher than the baseline network, respectively, providing an efficient and reliable end-to-end solution for solving the practical problem of steel defect detection.
