title: Infinite Families of Optimal Codes Over Non-unital Non-commutative Rings From Simplicial Complexes
authors: Yanan Wu, Tingting Pang, Nian Li, Yanbin Pan, Xiangyong Zeng
abstract: In this paper, several infinite families of codes over the extension of non-unital non-commutative rings are constructed utilizing general simplicial complexes. Thanks to the special structure of the defining sets, the principal parameters of these codes are characterized. Specially, when the employed simplicial complexes are generated by a single maximal element, we determine their Lee weight distributions completely. Furthermore, by considering the Gray image codes and the corresponding subfield-like codes, numerous of linear codes over Fq are also obtained, where q is a prime power. Certain conditions are given to ensure the above linear codes are (Hermitian) self-orthogonal in the case of q = 2; 3; 4. It is noteworthy that most of the derived codes over Fq satisfy the Ashikhmin-Barg’s condition for minimality. Besides, we obtain two infinite families of distanceoptimal codes over Fq with respect to the Griesmer bound. By puncturing the Gray image codes and subfield-like codes, several classes of projective codes are presented.

title: UAV-Based Jamming Detection for Large Language Model-Enabled Wireless Networks
authors: Qiaoxin Chen, Liang Xiao, Pengcheng Wang, Jieling Li, Haoyu Chen, Hongbin Jin, Ying-Jun Angela Zhang
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been used for uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)-based jamming detection by selecting hypothesis-test thresholds on received signal strength (RSS) to safeguard large language model (LLM) inference over wireless links. However, existing methods struggle against smart jammers with dynamic jamming probability. In this paper, we propose a UAV-based jamming detection scheme for LLM-enabled wireless networks to detect smart jamming. The proposed scheme augments traditional wireless features with an LLM-inferred communication environment indicator derived from multimodal sensing (e.g., images/videos from mobile devices), improving accuracy beyond RSS alone. Our approach jointly optimizes the test threshold and the UAV’s sniffer channel assignment, even with a limited number of antennas. We achieve this via a RL policy that leverages UAV imagery, e.g., suspicious radio devices and measuring relative distances, to accelerate detection speed. We implement the proposed scheme on a UAV equipped with a universal software radio peripheral to alert on jamming attacks in a wireless network integrated with a 7-billion-parameter vision-language multimodal LLM. Experiments with a UAV at 3 m detecting a smart jammer during image–text LLM inference across three mobile devices and an edge server demonstrate improvements in both detection accuracy and speed.

title: A 1.16 e-rms Temporal Random Noise, 123-dB High Dynamic Range, 2.988- μ m Pitch 3-Mpixel Three-Stacked Digital Pixel Sensor for Versatile Applications
authors: Min-Woong Seo, Yong-Suk Choi, Sanggwon Lee, Yeongseok Shim, Gihwan Cho, Daehee Bae, Masamichi Ito, Jaehun Jeong, Hiroyuki Sugihara, Si-Gyoung Koo, Sung-Jae Byun, Hyukbin Kwon, Youna Lee, Bumjun Kim, Su-Hyun Han, Suksan Kim, Wonoh Ryu, Kwan-Sik Kim, Yongjun Kim, Seung-Sik Kim, Junghoon Jung, Sun-Young Yoo, Jinkyeong Heo, Seung-Chul Shin, Heesung Shim, Jae-Kyu Lee, Jonghyun Go, Jaihyuk Song
abstract: This article presents a 3-Mpixel (Mp) three-stacked digital pixel sensor (DPS) featuring the world’s smallest pixel pitch of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.988~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m, achieving a low temporal random noise (RN) of 1.16 e-rms and a high dynamic range (HDR) of 123 dB in global-shutter (GS) operation mode for versatile applications. To realize both the low RN and HDR performance, the developed DPS employs a pixel-parallel analog-to-digital converter (P-ADC) architecture with an extremely narrow noise bandwidth (BW), as well as a split photodiode (PD) structure combined with signal chopping operation to mitigate the LED flicker phenomenon. In addition, the DPS leverages a three-stacked sensor architecture, which enables a compact pixel footprint while enhancing on-chip image signal processing (ISP) capabilities and supporting efficient HDR merging. Chip-level evaluations confirm the superior performance of the proposed DPS, demonstrating its potential for high-performance imaging in mobile, automotive, machine vision (MV), and other advanced applications.

title: AI-Empowered Multivariate Probabilistic Forecasting: A Key Enabler for Sustainability in Open RAN
authors: Vaishnavi Kasuluru, Luis Blanco, Cristian J. Vaca-Rubio, Engin Zeydan, Albert Bel
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: This paper explores the role of multivariate probabilistic forecasting in improving O-RAN operations, focusing on network sustainability aspects. A comprehensive analysis of its potential benefits and challenges, as well as its integration into the O-RAN architecture are described. The paper first presents an overview of the O-RAN architecture and components, followed by an examination of power consumption models relevant to O-RAN deployments and the challenges associated with traditional deterministic models in resource allocation. We then examine the performance of several state-of-the-art probabilistic multivariate forecasting techniques namely, Gaussian Process Vector Autoregression (GPVAR), Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) and non-probabilistic multivariate technique namely, Multivariate Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) and explain their implementation details and provide their evaluations. The simulation results show the effectiveness of these techniques in predicting Physical Resource Block (PRB) utilization and optimizing resource allocation. In particular, significant energy savings – around 20-30%– are achieved, depending on the percentile of the used probabilistic forecasting techniques. The benefits of considering probabilistic forecasting techniques compared to multi-variate LSTM are also analyzed. Our results emphasize the potential of probabilistic forecasting to improve energy efficiency and sustainability in O-RAN operations.

title: AEZGO: A novel approach for mapping mangrove canopy height using UAV lidar data and Sentinel-2 images
authors: Lingshan Chen, Bo Wan, Zongzhu Chen, Cheng Cheng, Minghui Wu, Penghua Qiu, Dezhi Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate mangrove canopy height mapping is essential for assessing blue carbon and protecting coastal communities. Yet, existing methods face dual challenges: (1) spectral saturation causing systematic bias (underestimation of tall trees and overestimation of short trees), and (2) spatial heterogeneity of mangrove ecosystems impairing model generalizability across large areas. This study develops an Automatic Ecological Zoning and Geo-balanced Optimization (AEZGO) approach integrating Sentinel-2 imagery, UAV lidar data, and mangrove ecological knowledge to address the challenges. The approach involves: (1) Automatic Ecological Zoning via an Adaptive Weighted Partitioning Algorithm based on four ecological knowledge types (environmental factors, spectral characteristics, canopy height-spectral correlations, and administrative boundaries) to reduce spatial heterogeneity, and (2) Geo-balanced Optimization integrates sample balancing, feature selection, and spatial covariates to resolve spectral saturation in spectral-height relationships. Applied to Hainan Island, China, the approach achieved significantly higher accuracy (R² = 0.74, RMSE = 1.12 m) compared to traditional random forest (R² = 0.59, RMSE = 1.34 m). Notably, it reduced balanced mean errors (aME) by 42% for tall trees (>7 m, 2.57 to 1.49 m) and 37% for short trees (<3 m, -0.76 to -0.48 m). The resultant mangrove canopy height map revealed an average height of 3.77 m across Hainan's mangroves. Compared to the existing mangrove canopy height maps from other studies, our approach yielded the most accurate results for Hainan Island. The proposed approach shows potential for broader application, thereby supporting large-scale precision management of mangrove-related tasks such as carbon sink accounting and the evaluation of coastal protection services like windbreak and wave dissipation.

title: YOLO-AUG: Enhancing Wildlife Monitoring on Far-Edge Devices with Dynamic Color-Space Augmentation and Efficient Learning
authors: Jean Pierre Asdikian, Sebastian Troia, Omran Ayoub, Kivilcim Yilmaz, Giacomo Sguotti, Mengyao Li, Marco Marcon, Guido Maier
abstract: Edge and far-edge computing devices are increasingly employed in applications requiring localized, low-latency processing despite their limited computational resources. One such application is wild animal monitoring in agricultural environments, which is vital for crop protection and disease prevention. Deep Learning (DL)-based models, especially those designed for image recognition, provide highly effective tools for identifying and monitoring wildlife. However, their performance is significantly constrained by two key challenges: the limited computational resources of edge and far-edge devices, and the variability in image quality caused by environmental factors. These limitations can compromise the robustness and reliability of the models, reducing their effectiveness in real-world applications. To address these challenges, we empirically evaluate the feasibility and overhead of supervised, transfer-learning, and semi-supervised training regimes for YOLO-based (You Only Look Once) detectors in resource-constrained settings, quantifying the practical trade-offs between detection performance and training overhead. Within this evaluation, we define and assess YOLO-AUG, a lightweight training-time augmentation module integrated into the YOLO training pipeline that applies on-the-fly multi-color-space transformations (e.g., Hue/Saturation/Value (HSV) and Commission Internationale de l’Éclairage Lab (CIELab) variants) to improve robustness in wildlife monitoring, without modifying the detector architecture. We also analyze memory constraints and power consumption on representative far-edge hardware (e.g., Graphical Processing Unit (GPU)-equipped trap cameras) and provide practical deployment guidelines that quantify trade-offs between accuracy, training time, and resource usage. Our evaluation underscores the trade-offs between these methodologies to provide robust and efficient monitoring solutions for our boar, wolf and deer detection scenario, moving toward a more automated workflow that reduces human labeling effort in our target deployment setting, crucial for both agricultural management and wildlife conservation.

title: SEC : Enabling MLLMs for Low-Latency IoT Video Analysis via Semantic-Aware Edge-Cloud Collaboration
authors: Mengyu Yang, Ye Tian, Peizhuang Cong, Lanshan Zhang, Gongli Xi, Song Wang, Wendong Wang
abstract: The rapid proliferation of IoT-enabled cameras has driven increasing demand for low-latency, intelligent video understanding in real-world applications such as smart cities and industrial automation. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer unprecedented capabilities in semantic reasoning and natural language-based video comprehension, their deployment in latency-sensitive IoT environments remains challenging due to high computational costs and sequential decoding bottlenecks. Moreover, conventional edge-cloud video analysis frameworks often rely on semantic-agnostic frame sampling, leading to information loss or redundant data transmission. In this paper, we propose SEC, a semantic-aware edge-cloud collaborative framework for efficient and accurate video analysis. SEC introduces a task-aware key frame selection mechanism at the edge to maximize semantic relevance while minimizing bandwidth usage, and a novel adaptive speculative decoding framework with tree-based parallel generation on the cloud to accelerate MLLM inference. Extensive experiments under realistic edge-cloud deployment settings on four video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed SEC framework achieves superior performance, significantly reducing the end-to-end inference latency while improving the accuracy, a rare win-win in latency-critical IoT systems.

title: APULSE: A Scalable Hybrid Algorithm for the RCSPP on Large-Scale Dense Graphs
authors: Nuno A. D. Soares, António M. R. C. Grilo
abstract: The Resource-Constrained Shortest Path Problem (RCSPP) is a fundamental NP-hard optimization challenge with broad applications ranging from network routing and logistics to autonomous navigation. Although a rich body of exact RCSPP solvers exists, their practical applicability is severely limited on large-scale, dense graphs, where classical label-setting methods suffer from exponential growth in the number of non-dominated labels. This limitation is particularly critical for Unmanned Ground Vehicle (UGV) mission planning, where route generation is performed prior to deployment using high-resolution terrain representations and heterogeneous threat models that naturally induce dense grid-based graphs and strict travel-time budgets. This paper introduces APULSE, a hybrid search algorithm designed to address this scalability gap by combining A* heuristic guidance, aggressive Pulse-style pruning, and a time-bucketing mechanism that bounds memory consumption through a controlled relaxation of the dominance relation. As a consequence of this discretization, APULSE is not guaranteed to return the exact RCSPP optimum for all instances; instead, it offers a tunable accuracy–efficiency trade-off governed by an auto-tuning parameter that adjusts temporal granularity. A comprehensive computational study evaluates APULSE against state-of-the-art exact solvers and Beam Search heuristics on a large, dense UGV planning graph. Under the default auto-tuning configuration, APULSE matches the empirical reference solution returned by exact solvers in 8 out of 9 evaluated scale and slack configurations, with a maximum observed optimality gap of 0.01% in the remaining case, and empirically converges to the reference solution as the bucketing resolution is increased. At the same time, APULSE remains orders of magnitude faster and substantially more memory-efficient than exact methods on the largest dense instances. These results demonstrate that APULSE enables practical constrained route generation and rapid what-if analysis on dense graphs within operationally realistic decision-making windows.

title: PalmRachis-BiLSTM-Attn: An Anatomically Guided Explainable Deep Learning Framework for Spatial–Temporal Progression Modeling of Date-Palm Leaf Diseases
authors: Mansoor Alghamdi, Ahmad Abadleh, Sami Mnasri, Malek Alrashidi, Ibrahim S Alkhazi
abstract: Early and interpretable detection of foliar diseases in date palms is essential for sustainable agriculture in arid regions, yet conventional deep-learning models treat symptoms as static visual patterns without considering their biological progression along the rachis. This study introduces PalmRachis-BiLSTM-Attn, a bio-inspired and explainable deep-learning framework that models palm-leaf pathology as a spatial–temporal progression process guided by the anatomical ordering of the rachis. The method begins with a CNN backbone that extracts textural cues from leaf images, which are then reorganized into rachis-aligned sequences and processed through a bidirectional LSTM with temporal attention to capture both forward (disease spread) and backward (regression) vascular dynamics. An integrated explainability module combines Grad-CAM spatial maps with temporal-attention timelines, generating biologically meaningful “where–when” visualizations of disease development. Evaluated on an eight-class date-palm leaf dataset, the framework achieves 98.1% classification accuracy and outperforms static CNNs and non-anatomical sequence baselines across all metrics (precision = 0.988, recall = 0.984, F1 = 0.984). By aligning model behavior with real infection pathways, PalmRachis-BiLSTM-Attn advances progression-aware, transparent, and biologically grounded disease monitoring, offering a deployment-oriented foundation for IoT-enabled crop-health systems and future smart-agriculture.

title: BioFPT: Biosignal Feature Pyramid Transformer for self-supervised representation learning from ECGsignals
authors: Haobo Meng, Caiyuan Zhang, Fangfang Jiang, Ziyu Zhu, Ao Sun, Junxin Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis represents a promising field for deep learning applications in clinical diagnostics. However, practical use of current methods is still constrained by their heavy reliance on large amounts of labeled data, as well as limitations in processing efficiency and signal quality. To address these challenges, we present BioFPT (Biosignal Feature Pyramid Transformer), a novel self-supervised learning framework designed for ECG signal. The proposed framework incorporates a Split Mask-Join (SMJ) transformation for a Pre-training strategy, complemented by an overlapping embedding mechanism that eliminates positional encoding requirements. The efficiency of the architectural design is enhanced through a Spatial Reduction Attention (SRA) transformer, which achieves a reduction in computational complexity without performance degradation. Comprehensive evaluation of seven public ECG datasets comprising over 94,000 sub jects demonstrates BioFPT's effectiveness, an accuracy improvement of 4.2% and a parameter reduction of 14.8% compared to state-of-the-art models. Furthermore, it maintains robust performance across diverse pathological conditions and signal qualities. The proposed architecture rep resents a significant advancement in self-supervised ECG analysis, particularly suitable for scenarios with limited labeled data availability. Moreover, its versatile architecture shows promise for broader applications across various biosignals.

title: Risk-Constrained Learning of Personalized Driving Behaviors for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Yuepeng Wang, Xun Shen, Takuji Arima
abstract: Achieving intelligent driving assistance hinges on a precise understanding and prediction of human driving behavior, which requires reliable and interpretable behavior models. However, existing neural network–based approaches have limited capability in capturing drivers’ latent intentions and personalized preferences, and their lack of interpretability constrains prediction accuracy and the adaptability of assistive strategies. Human driving behavior can be formalized as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), integrating goal pursuit, cognitive risk perception, and interaction intentions with surrounding vehicles. To address these challenges, this study proposes a personalized driving behavior modeling framework based on inverse reinforcement learning (IRL), enabling the inference of individual reward functions and the characterization of personalized behavioral traits. The framework first establishes a driver cognitive model grounded in the concept of a risk field, describing the driver’s subjective assessment and interaction tendencies toward surrounding vehicles in terms of risk perception and courtesy intention. This formulation quantifies individual risk preferences and safety boundaries. A personalized reward structure is then constructed by incorporating behavioral features, and the driver’s implicit preferences are recovered through IRL. By extracting state–action features from naturalistic driving data, the framework infers reward parameters that represent intrinsic behavioral motivations, enabling interpretable and data-driven modeling of personalized driving behavior. Finally, the learned personalized reward model is integrated into a reinforcement learning framework as a guidance signal, allowing the agent to generate adaptive, human-like driving behaviors consistent with individual psychological characteristics and preferences. Experimental results on naturalistic highway data verify that the proposed approach effectively reproduces diverse driving styles and enhances behavioral interpretability.

title: Towards Sports Betting as a Financial Asset: An Investigative Analysis of Risk, Investment Potential, and Future Perspectives
authors: René Manassé Galekwa, Jean Marie Tshimula, Etienne Gael Tajeuna, Kyandoghere Kyamakya
abstract: Sports betting has grown into a large, data-intensive market; however, research on prediction and capital allocation remains fragmented. Most forecasting studies overlook leverage and path-dependent portfolio dynamics, whereas Kelly based frameworks assume frictionless markets and perfectly known probabilities. These gaps have contributed to the failure of several betting funds that developed accurate predictive models but lacked a robust risk-management discipline. To this end, we develop an integrated framework that embeds forecasting, stake sizing, and leverage control into a single portfolio management architecture. First, we model recursive leverage dynamics that generate superlinear growth under favorable outcomes and convex collapse during adverse sequences. Second, we introduce an uncertainty-adjusted Kelly criterion that uses the Kullback–Leibler divergence to penalize model misspecification and reduce stake sizes. Third, we embed them in a three-tier architecture: bet sizing, portfolio CVaR limits and leverage caps. Controlled simulations demonstrate that uncertainty-adjusted staking reduces the ruin probability from 78% to below 2% while preserving 85% of the growth potential, and that VaR-based leverage limits prevent extreme drawdowns (92% vs. 41%). The empirical validation of real-world data collected from the English Premier League confirms that uncertainty quantification improves risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe ratio gains of 30%) and enhances diversification benefits. All results can be reproduced by following the code available at https://github.com/Zabat/betting-risk-management.

title: Evaluating Critical Barriers to Industry 4.0 Adoption in the Thai Automotive Sector Using an Integrated Fuzzy BWM-PROMETHEE II-DEMATEL Framework
authors: Tossapol Kiatcharoenpol, Pornwasin Sirisawat
abstract: This study investigates the barriers to the adoption of Industry 4.0 (I4.0) in the Thai automotive industry, which is a major economic growth and export competitiveness driver. It aims to offer evidence-based prioritization of barriers and causal relations to inform firms and policymakers in the transformation of smart manufacturing. The methodology follows three stages of multi-criteria decision-making model. Based on a literature survey and expert knowledge, the integration of Fuzzy BWM-PROMETHEE II was used for prioritization. Then Fuzzy DEMATEL is employed to illuminate the causal relationship among critical barriers. The Fuzzy BWM results highlight Customization, Flexible Production, Human–Machine Collaboration, and Cybersecurity as the most influential practices supporting I4.0 implementation. While analysis of Fuzzy PROMETHEE II and DEMATEL together identifies High Initial Investment, Supply Chain Integration as critical barriers and dominant causal drivers that influence other dependent barriers. Addressing these two factors initially helps accelerate digital readiness and enhance transformation performance. The study presents the advanced systematic ranking of I4.0 adoption barriers in the Thai automotive industry. The integration of Fuzzy BWM-PROMETHEE II-DEMATEL framework has a novel methodological contribution and also provides useful decision support to strategic planning and resource allocation.

title: Automated Load Forecasting in Power Systems Using Deep Learning Models with LSTM Networks
authors: Penugonda Suresh Babu, A Sujin Jose, Y. Geetha, Navdeep Singh, Dinesh Kumar Yadav, Ankam Sreejyothsna
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Load forecasting is an essential factor in the stability of today’s power systems as well as their performance. In this work, the authors propose an automatic method for load forecasting based on deep learning models coded in Python. The paper uses Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) as a model and the forecast of electricity demand is based on time series data from household power consumption. The LSTM model was trained on a large set of data and used feature scaling and sequence generation in order to improve its accuracy in prediction. The architecture of the model with the LSTM layers in sequence can define long temporal dependencies and, therefore, make accurate predictions of the future load demand. The results that are shown in Table 3 and compared to actual load data confirmed the model effectiveness in the short-term electricity load forecasting with high precision. This self-adaptive forecasting process has the potential to be used in real time applications in smart grid systems to enhance the demand side management and hence the performance of power systems. This is particularly evidenced by the availability of the implementation in Python alongside strong libraries like TensorFlow and Scikit-learn for use in energy management systems.

title: Advancing Autoencoders and Non-Local Means with Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Shirin Ashtari Tondashti, Navid Adib, Mehran Alyali, Mahdis Yaghoubi, Seyed Kamaledin Setarehdan
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Speckle noise in Intravascular Ultrasound (IVUS) images poses a significant challenge, necessitating early elimination of these artifacts. In this study, two novel methods were designed: a denoising convolutional autoencoder and a modified non-local means algorithm using particle swarm optimization (PSO) for automatic parameter selection. This research delved into assessing the efficacy of various denoising models, encompassing spatial domain techniques (median filter, non-local means, optimized non-local means), transform domain methods (wavelet, curvelet), and deep learning approaches (denoising autoencoder). A wide array of data augmentation techniques was employed during the training of the autoencoder model. Evaluation was conducted using two key metrics: Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) and Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR). The findings indicate the robustness of the autoencoder model with the proposed augmentation strategy across different speckle noise variances, highlighting its superiority over traditional methods. Moreover, the study underscores the enhanced performance of modified non-local means with particle swarm optimization (PSO) compared to manual non-local means.

title: Hybrid Fuzzy Density-Based Clustering and SARIMANARNet Model for Accurate Solar Irradiance Forecasting in Solar Energy Systems
authors: Sugeng Purwanto, Ibnu Hajar, Bayu Aji, Syamsir Abduh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Clustering is a fundamental technique in unsupervised learning for identifying patterns in unlabeled data. However, realworld data often contain noise and exhibit complex nonlinear patterns, necessitating hybrid approaches to enhance clustering accuracy and stability. This study proposes a hybrid method called Fuzzy Density-Based Hybrid Clustering (FDHC), which integrates Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN) and Fuzzy C-Means (FCM). DBSCAN is selected for its ability to detect density-based clusters and eliminate noise, while FCM introduces flexibility through fuzzy membership to handle data ambiguity, resulting in more adaptive segmentation. Furthermore, this research incorporates a hybrid forecasting model by combining Seasonal AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA) with a Nonlinear AutoRegressive Neural Network (NARNet) to improve the precision of solar irradiance predictions. SARIMA effectively models seasonal patterns, whereas NARNet captures nonlinear dynamics that SARIMA alone cannot detect. FDHC and SARIMA-NARNet complement each other in managing data complexity, with FDHC providing high-quality clustered inputs that enable tailored forecasting for each segment. Evaluation using the Davies-Bouldin Index (DBI) and the Silhouette Score demonstrates optimal clustering performance, with a Silhouette Score of 0.338 and a DBI of 0.892. The hybrid SARIMA-NARNet model also outperforms standalone SARIMA in predictive performance, as assessed by Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). These results highlight the potential of the integrated approach to support more efficient solar energy systems through dependable irradiance modeling.

title: An LSTM-XGB Feature Fusion Model Based on Dynamic Degradation Trends for Remaining Useful Life Prediction of Aero-Engines
authors: Yunfeng Ma, Zhenglong Zhu, Miao Li, Chunyu Wei, Yubin Fan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechatronic Engineering and Artificial Intelligence (MEAI)
abstract: Aero-engine performance and reliability are crucial for mission success and maintenance costs. With advancements in sensing and data acquisition, data-driven remaining useful life (RUL) prediction has become a key focus in engine health management. However, the strong nonlinearity and time-varying degradation of turbofan engines challenge traditional models. This paper proposes an LSTM-XGB fusion model for RUL prediction using the C-MAPSS dataset. By extracting multi-scale statistical and temporal features through sliding windows, LSTM captures degradation dynamics, while XGBoost performs nonlinear fusion. Experimental results show improved prediction accuracy and generalization under varied operational conditions, providing a robust framework for aero-engine health prognosis.

title: Efficient 3D Reconstruction from Dual-View Perspective Images via Tri-Level Geometric Attention and Lightweight Channel Upsampling
authors: Yuandong Ma, Yong Zhang, Rupeng Yan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechatronic Engineering and Artificial Intelligence (MEAI)
abstract: Real-time 3D reconstruction from dual-view perspective images remains limited by weak interpretability, loss of high-frequency structures, and insufficient deployment efficiency. These issues are particularly critical for safety-oriented inspection systems, where rapid structural recovery and reliable edge fidelity outweigh conventional dense reconstruction accuracy. Furthermore, the lack of a unified geometric reasoning framework hinders effective cross-view fusion, aggravating texture degradation during inference. To overcome these limitations, we propose a geometry-driven reconstruction framework that integrates a Tri-Level Geometric Attention (TGA) hierarchy with a lightweight Gated Channel Upsample Block (GCUB) atop a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) backbone. TGA consists of: Epipolar Attention for epipolar-line-aligned positional priors; Local Attention for neighborhood structural aggregation; and Global Compensation Attention, which employs a learnable FFT-based frequency selector and cross-shaped compensation with decaying weights to restore missing high-frequency cues. This tri-level design significantly improves geometric consistency and texture preservation. Complementarily, GCUB performs squeeze-excitation channel gating to remove redundant responses while coupling cross-attention to stabilize dual-view feature interaction, forming an efficient and compact lifting pathway suited for real-time deployment. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our approach achieves real-time Gaussian-field reconstruction with marked improvements in texture sharpness, edge stability, and computational efficiency compared to classical and learningbased baselines.

title: Three-Phase LCL-filtered Quasi-Z Source Inverter with MIMO Sliding Mode Controller
authors: Mansouri Houssam Eddine, Billel Talbi, Idris Messaoudene, Moussa Abderrahim Mehiris, Abdelbasset Krama, Abbes Kihal
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: This work presents a new multi-input multi-output (MIMO) sliding mode control (SMC) method using an LCL filter for a three-phase quasi-Z-source inverter (qZSI). The proposed SMC scheme facilitates simultaneous regulation of both the DC link and AC output of the qZSI. Its merits include low control gain needs, simplicity of implementation, resistance to parametric changes, and load voltage steady-state error removal. Integration of a space vector modulator synchronized with the shoot-through states yields constant switching frequency operation. A proportional-integral (PI) controller provides the reference for the DC-side inductor current. The efficacy of the proposed control methodology is evaluated through simulations conducted in MATLAB/ Simulink, considering both linear and nonlinear load conditions.

title:
Machine Learning Prediction of Properties across U Values and Phases: A Case Study on TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
authors: Huanyu Xu, Jincang Zhang, Baoyu Xu, Dongbo Dai, Huiran Zhang, Shengzhou Li
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning, Computational Intelligence and Pattern Recognition (MLCIPR)
abstract: Machine learning (ML) has become an effective tool for predicting material properties. In density functional theory (DFT), the Hubbard U correction mitigates localized-electron errors; however, selecting an appropriate U is challenging because it varies across materials, phases, and targeted properties, and conventional trial-and-error scanning over many U values is computationally costly. ML provides an efficient alternative by learning U-dependent trends and enabling rapid property prediction without extensive calculations. Here, we trained regression models to reproduce the U-dependent energy, bandgap, and relative energy of TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> with high accuracy and stable. Transfer-learning tests further show that adding only a few samples from a new phase, typically around three, is sufficient to recover high predictive accuracy, demonstrating that U-dependent trends can be effectively transferred across TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> phases.

title: MSMTAN: A Multi-Scale Multi-Task Attention Network for Stock Trend Forecasting
authors: Haijiao Xu, Yuchen Wu, Yilin Wu, Liang Xie, Qian Zhang, Wanyi Li
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: Predicting stock trend represents a fundamental challenge in non-stationary multivariate time series, hindered by complex multi-scale characteristics and the constraints of isolated task learning approaches. Traditional methods often fail to simultaneously capture short-term fluctuations and long-term trends, while single-task models overlook the intrinsic relationships between related prediction tasks (e.g., price regression, trend classification, and stock ranking). To overcome these limitations, we introduce MSMTAN, a Multi-Scale Multi-Task Attention Network designed for stock trend prediction. The model integrates multi-scale temporal inputs to learn dynamic high-order spatial relationships among stocks using a spatio-temporal hypergraph module. Additionally, a task-relational attention mechanism adaptively transfers knowledge across tasks, enhancing prediction accuracy and practical utility. Comprehensive evaluations on real-world benchmarks demonstrate that MSMTAN achieves superior performance over contemporary methods in both forecasting accuracy and practical utility. The proposed framework offers a novel solution for multi-task stock time series analysis, with potential applications in smart grid load forecasting and climate change trend analysis.

title: Comparative Analysis of CFAR Algorithms using 77GHz Radar Sensor Data in FPGA Driven Implementation
authors: Sneha R Prasad, Vandana G S, Pathipati Srihari, Mahaveera K
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Radar signal processing plays a vital role in detecting targets under varying noise and clutter conditions, where real-time adaptive techniques, such as the constant false alarm rate (CFAR), are essential. This paper presents a hardware-based comparison of three CFAR algorithms cell averaging (CA-CFAR), greatest of cell averaging (GOCA-CFAR), and smallest of cell averaging (SOCA-CFAR) implemented on the Xilinx Zynq-7 ZC702 FPGA for real-time radar target detection. Radar data from the AWR1642 mmWave sensor with DCA1000EVM are stored in block RAM (BRAM) and processed using Verilog HDL. The algorithms are evaluated in terms of detection accuracy, timing, resource utilization, and power. The design achieved a maximum operating frequency of 98.2 MHz, a throughput of 1.571 Gbps, and moderate hardware utilization (74 BRAMs, 4500 LUTs), with power consumption between 234 mW to 238 mW. A worst negative slack (WNS) of –5.182 ns observed, reflecting timing challenges with large real radar sensor data where the system maintained accurate detection.The comparative results show that GOCA-CFAR outperformed CA-CFAR and SOCA-CFAR, about 12% higher throughput and accurate detection results, confirming the suitability of the Zynq-7 ZC702 FPGA for embedded radar signal processing applications.

title: Adaptive UAV Swarm Leader Selection Using a Fuzzy Logic Multi-Index Framework
authors: Simarjeet Singh, Kanishk Kumar, Vikas Bhaneriya, Amit Mathur
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) swarms have become increasingly essential in fields of defence, surveillance, disaster management, and environmental monitoring, it demands robust, adaptable and coordination mechanisms. One of the main challenges in swarm operations is choosing an optimal leader UAV to manage the swarm in changing environments. Conventional leader selection methods which rely on static criteria often fail to adapt to dynamic conditions, leading to a decline in performance. In this paper, we introduce a novel Fuzzy Logic Multi-Index Framework for adaptive leader selection in UAV swarms. Our approach combines three key indices-Energy Efficiency Index (EEI), Communication Reliability Index (CRI), and Physical Stability Index (PSI) which are derived from realtime UAV parameters. Using fuzzy logic these indices are dynamically combined to calculate a comprehensive Leader Index (LI) for each UAV, allowing the swarm to select the most suitable leader in real time. The proposed framework provides a decentralized, scalable, and robust solution that effectively handles uncertainties in UAV operations and ensures continuous adaptation to both environmental and operational changes. Through detailed simulations to identify the optimal leader, we show that the multi index approach enhances overall performance and outperforms traditional methods across key metrics. The results indicate that the system is particularly effective in environments with rapidly changing conditions, making it a practical solution for real world UAV swarm deployments. This research contributes to the advancement of autonomous swarm management and paves the way for the development of more resilient multi agent systems.

title: Analysis on Utilizing Human-Centered Networks, an Experimental Verification of The Reserve Allocation Movement Process on Order Systems
authors: Maheswari Chundru, Ravi Babu Narahari, Arun Kumar S, Seetharam K, Arif Jahan Pattan, B.Mahaboob
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Enterprise enterprises benefit greatly from the effective use of human resources. Using the human capital of a business more efficiently reduces the cost of creating new goods and services, increases worker efficiency, and adds value to the enterprise. Employees within the business build a network of professional ties as they work together to create products and advantages for the company. In this research, we present a method depending on the human-centric networking disclosed through the event logs to assist decision-making about the distribution of human resources inside an organization. In particular, methods for allocating resources in companies are made using knowledge gathered from human-centric networks developed during the information systems' operational phases. We clarify the optimal resource allocation determined by various metrics by using social network measurements and acceptability possibilities for human resource allocation. For the purpose of planning human resource allowances in accordance with these networks, the overall architecture and situations are also outlined. In addition, we verify the idea using an experimental validation using real-world data sets. This strategy will provide a brilliant response to help companies allocate resources within their own organizations.

title: An IoT Low-Cost Optical Sensor to Detect the Presence of Diluted Drugs in Water
authors: Sandra VicianoTudela, Iman Valizadeh, Lorena Parra, Sandra Sendra, Jaime Lloret
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Internet of Things: Systems, Management and Security (IOTSMS)
abstract: In coastal areas, saltwater and freshwater converge, creating high biodiversity and significant pollution issues from industrial waste, microplastics, fertilizers, and medications. These substances disrupt ecosystems and species' life cycles that regulate key physiological processes, even affecting humans. Chromatography is the most commonly used technique for detecting drugs in water, but it is also expensive and requires specialized personnel. This study used optical sensors around a tube to detect drugs (Dacortin, Diazepam, and Prednisone) and baking flour in water by measuring light absorption at different wavelengths (visible, infrared, and ultraviolet). Data changes were processed using an Arduino Uno microprocessor, configured as an IoT device. The concentrations were 0, 10, 15, and 30 mg/100 mL. The results display several models using MATLAB. K-nearest neighbor showed the best performance with 100% accuracy and the shortest training time (0.71 seconds). Ultraviolet sensors provided the most variable and impactful data, while red and green sensors showed similar results, and blue had higher dispersion. Infrared sensors were less sensitive, requiring future adjustment for improved analysis. Regression models showed high performance, the best being Rational Quadratic Gaussian Process Regression, achieving a Root Mean Squared Error of 0.2 mg/100 mL in training and 0.01 mg/100 mL in testing. Some training errors were attributed to outliers caused by electronic or sample issues, highlighting the need for outlier filtering. Test dataset errors were minimal and consistent with training results, validating the model's accuracy.

title: Resonant Surface Acoustic Wave Sensors for in-Situ Monitoring of Cutting Forces in Turning Processes
authors: Marie Horlbeck, Sebastian Junghans, Jan Dege, Benedict Scheiner, Fabian Lurz
publish: 2026 IEEE Topical Conference on Wireless Sensors and Sensor Networks (WiSNeT)
abstract: Effective monitoring of machining processes requires robust and cost-effective sensors capable of capturing dynamic forces. This study investigates a turning tool holder with an integrated 2.4 GHz resonant surface acoustic wave (SAW) sensor for measuring cutting forces under varying feed rate conditions. Reference measurements were performed using a calibrated dynamometer. The comparison between the SAW sensor and the dynamometer data revealed strong agreement, confirming that the integrated sensor captures the progression of the cutting force. These results demonstrate the potential of SAW-based sensor technology for real-time monitoring of forces in machining processes.

title: An Ensemble Machine Learning and LLM-Augmented Framework for Real-Time Diabetes Risk Prediction and Lifestyle Guidance
authors: Surajit Das, Jitendra Pradhan, Samaleswari P. Nayak, Biswajit Sahoo
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Diabetes is a major global health concern, projected to affect more than 850 million people by 2050. Early risk prediction is crucial for timely intervention and improved patient outcomes. This study presents a machine learning framework for diabetes prediction using a clinical dataset, with class-weighted learning to address imbalance synthetic oversampling. Multiple models are evaluated, including LR, SVM, KNN, DT, RF, BRF, EE, and variants of Gradient Boosting, where RF achieved the best balance of accuracy and robustness. The model's reliability is ensured using SHAP interpretation. The best-performing model is deployed in a web application for real-time predictions and personalized lifestyle guidance to assist the user in real time. The result demonstrates the effectiveness of the ensemble models and the potential of combining predictive analysis with LLM for patient-centric health care solutions.

title: High-performance diabetic retinopathy detection using MobileNetV3-Large with RGB and CLAHE fundus images
authors: Ishaq Aiche, Youcef Brik, Bilal Attallah, Hegazy Rezk, Ibraheem Shayeai, Zool Hilmi Ismail
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: As a critical microvascular complication of diabetes mellitus, diabetic retinopathy (DR) represents one of the principal contributors to avoidable vision impairment globally. Leveraging deep learning methodologies provides a computationally efficient pathway toward timely diagnosis, especially within resource-constrained healthcare settings. This study used the publicly available Aptos 2019 dataset to evaluate the efficacy of the MobileNetV3-Large architecture for binary classification of DR (present or absent). Both unprocessed RGB (red, green, and blue) images and CLAHE-enhanced retinal fundus images were used to train and evaluate the model. Extensive experiments were carried out, systematically varying batch sizes and learning rates to optimise performance. When trained using RGB images, the MobileNetV3-Large model achieved an accuracy of 97.82%, a recall of 98.12%, and an F1 score of 97.86%. Models trained on CLAHE-processed images also performed robustly, achieving maximum accuracies of 97.68%, recalls of 98.12% and F1 scores of 97.72%. These results highlight the efficiency and reliability of the MobileNetV3-Large architecture for DR detection. Slightly better performance was observed for models trained on RGB images than for those using CLAHE enhancement. This work demonstrates the potential of lightweight deep learning models to enable scalable, real-time screening for diabetic retinopathy.

title: ADC-DSP-Based Wireline Receiver Datapath for Modern High-Speed SerDes
authors: Gain Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE 26th Topical Meeting on Silicon Monolithic Integrated Circuits in RF Systems (SiRF)
abstract: While non-return-to-zero (NRZ) signaling and 4-level pulse amplitude modulation (PAM-4) with analog equalizers have served earlier generations of serializer-deserializer (SerDes), they become inadequate for communicating over lossy electrical channels at beyond 100 Gb/s datarate. Consequently, analog-to-digital converter (ADC) and digital signal processor (DSP)-based receivers have emerged as the dominant architecture for long-reach applications with > 100 Gb/s, offering powerful equalization and support for advanced algorithms such as sliding-block DFE (SB-DFE) and maximum-likelihood sequence estimation (MLSE). This paper briefs recent advances in ADC-DSP-based wireline receivers, highlighting overall system and DSP equalizers. In addition, discrete multitone (DMT) modulation is discussed as a promising candidate for next-generation interconnects, with multiple proof-of-concept implementations in advanced CMOS nodes.

title: Iot Based Avoid Fire Accident EV Vehicle with Multiple Fault detection Using AI
authors: S. Kanimozhi, Vishwa A, Viswa R, Yugeshwar K
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Electric cars (EVs) are increasingly more adopted as a sustainable transportation alternative, but the chance of fire injuries because of battery faults stays a critical protection subject. This paper provides an Internet of Things (IoT)-primarily based gadget designed to monitor and mitigate fire risks in EVs via multi-fault detection and actual-time response. The system integrates a voltage sensor, temperature sensor, flame sensor, Arduino microcontroller, NodeMCU (IoT module), LCD display, relay module, and a buzzer to make sure continuous monitoring of battery health and environmental conditions. The voltage sensor ensures the battery operates within secure electrical limits, whilst the temperature sensor detects overheating—an early indicator of thermal runaway. A flame sensor gives an additional layer of protection via identifying combustion. Upon detecting extraordinary situations, the system initiates computerized responses: triggering alarms, showing indicators at the LCD, and disconnecting the energy supply using a relay to prevent damage or hearth escalation. NodeMCU enables remote records transmission to a cloud platform, in which customers can access battery fitness statistics in real time via a cellular or web interface. This enables predictive maintenance, fault analytics, and informed decision-making. Local and cloud-primarily based indicators improve the system’s reliability and responsiveness.The design is modular, low-fee, and scalable for integration into various EV systems. By combining on-device intelligence with cloud connectivity, the proposed gadget enhances operational safety, prolongs battery life, and reduces fireplace-related dangers—supporting broader adoption of EVs in a safer and smarter way.

title: AI-Assisted Design of a Reusable Piezoelectric Shoe Sole for Wearable Energy Harvesting
authors: Nilesh Bhaskarrao Bahadure, Jagdish Chandra Patni, Deepak Parashar, Bhoomi Shah, Akanksha Kulkarni, Nishita Nilesh Bahadure
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Piezoelectric energy harvesting offers a promising approach for converting mechanical vibrations into usable electrical energy, making it a viable alternative power source for wearable electronics and structural health monitoring applications. This study focuses on the design and development of a novel reusable shoe sole integrated with advanced piezoelectric materials to enable sustainable energy generation during human motion. Key components of the system, including piezoelectric materials, transducer architecture, and power conditioning circuitry are examined to optimize energy extraction efficiency. Recent advancements in piezoelectric material innovation, such as lead-free ceramics, polymer-based composites, and single-crystal structures, are highlighted for their enhanced performance and reduced environmental impact. Overall, this work presents a comprehensive overview of the latest progress in piezoelectric energy harvesting technologies and demonstrates their potential to power self-sustaining IoT and wearable devices across engineering, defense, and aerospace sectors.

title: Energy-Efficient Comparator Architecture for Flash ADC using FinFET Technology in Radar Applications
authors: Neeraja B, Vasanth K, Chandrasekhar E, Chandra Sekhar P, Dharani S, Rashmitha B
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: In this work, a 3-bit flash Analog-to-Digital Converter using a priority encoder and a comparator circuit is implemented in 18 nm FinFET technology. Two comparator topologies namely, the Strong-Arm latch comparator and the Double-Tail comparator circuits are individually integrated into the ADC architecture. The designs are developed and simulated using Cadence Virtuoso, and performance is evaluated under both conventional CMOS and FinFET technologies. The Strong-ARM comparator in FinFET offered delay of 32.232 ps and power consumption of 47 μW, while the Double-Tail comparator has 72.14ps of delay and power consumption of 124 μW. These results indicate that the Strong-Arm latch comparator implemented in FinFET technology is a more energy-efficient and high-speed solution, making it well-suited for next-generation ultra-low-power ADCs in Radar applications.

title: Comparative Analysis A CNN-LSTM Deep Learning Model for Assessment and Remedial Placement of School Students
authors: Mohd Hizami Bin Ab Halim, Suriani Mohd Sam, Norliza Mohamed
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: This paper presents a CNN-LSTM hybrid deep learning model designed for classification of school students requiring remedial placement across multiple subjects, including mathematics, reading, and writing. Traditional assessment methods often fail to capture temporal learning progress and behavioral indicators that are crucial for early intervention. The CNN extracts spatial features from handwritten or scanned student assessments, while the LSTM captures temporal performance patterns over time. The model was evaluated using a dataset consisting of $\mathbf{1, 5 0 0}$ primary school student records, including scanned diagnostic test images and sequential academic performance scores. This dataset reflects both spatial and temporal dimensions of student performance. The CNN-LSTM model was compared to standalone CNN, LSTM, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Random Forest models. Evaluation on a synthetic global dataset simulating real-world diversity yields a classification accuracy of $93.4 \%$, outperforming standard machine learning methods. Results show that CNN-LSTM outperforms traditional models in accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC, demonstrating its potential for intelligent educational interventions.

title: A Review of Visual Perception and Generation Technologies in Intelligent Transportation Scenarios
authors: Guo Yang, Yunjie Wang, Bo Qiu, Jian-Ping Wu, Shaojiang Zheng, Jia-Wei Liu
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: Autonomous driving, situated at the intersection of artificial intelligence and robotics, holds vast potential across transportation, manufacturing, agriculture, and modern services. As a foundational component, vision-based environmental perception systems have made notable strides from 2015 to 2025. This report critically reviews key advances and trends in visual perception for autonomous driving, focusing on four dimensions: (1) visual scene generation using generative AI, particularly diffusion models, to provide high-fidelity data for system testing; (2) 3D occupancy prediction for fine-grained scene understanding and its benchmark performance; (3) end-to-end driving models that integrate perception and planning, evaluated in complex scenarios; and (4) large-scale models, including world models and multimodal foundation models, and their potential to enhance cognitive intelligence in autonomous systems. Each section discusses underlying principles, representative models, metrics, and application prospects, concluding with future directions for visual perception in autonomous driving.

title: Hybrid 3D Dual-Path Transformer and Residual Attention U-Net for Efficient Brain Tumor Segmentation from MRI Scans
authors: Sriramkumar R, Murugan P, Rakshita R, Lakshmanan M, Joshuva Arockia Dhanraj, Godhandaraman T
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Segmenting brain tumors in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans has been a challenging issue with medical imaging, because of the great diversity in tumors regarding their sizes, shapes, and textures. Traditional 3D convolutional neural networks (3D-CNNs) have been shown to be very good at extracting local features, but bad at capturing relationships across large distances. To overcome this drawback, the current study offers a hybrid deep-learning architecture, which is the 3D Dual-Path Transformer Residual Attention U-Net (3D DPT-RAUNet) to realize an accurate segmentation of the brain-tumor. The architecture proposed will combine two orthogonal modules: 3D Residual Attention U-Net branch that will focus on fine-grained learning of local features, and Transformer-based Dual-Path Encoder which will focus on learning global spatial cues in the entire MRI volume. Tumor-relevant regions are selectively highlighted by means of the attention mechanism, and gradient propagation stabilized by residual connections is achieved by the model to achieve better convergence. Experiments with the BraTS2023 dataset using substantial preprocessing include bias field correction, intensity normalisation, and patch extraction show that the 3D DPT - RAUNet obtains a mean Dice similarity coefficient of 0.957 which is significantly better than current 3D U-Net and Transformer-based models and is at a low cost of about 18 - percent. The findings highlight the superior performance, strength, and clinical practicability of the hybrid architecture in automating the analysis of brain-tumors.

title: Wearable Biosensors for Continuous Monitoring of Physiological Signals: A Review and Future Perspectives
authors: R. Ayyappa, Ramanuj Tewan, Tulkin Buzrukov, Muyassar Allaberganova, Mirjalol Madaminov, Angira Sharma
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Together with a wide range of smart devices, big data, cloud services, wireless connectivity, and small electronics and intelligent sensors, these developments have not just made wearable biosensors possible but also extended human lifespans. This is accomplished through a variety of apps, such as those that monitor physiological signals, provide personalized medical treatment, and offer suggestions for enhancing user expertise. Nevertheless, several issues pertaining to low energy usage, accuracy, ease, and dependability have constrained the use of wearable biosensors in medical settings to monitor physiological signals. This study introduced a new wearable sensor that measures physiological signals continuously: blood pressure (BP), heart rate (HR), and electrodermal activity (EDA). The design of the suggested sensor is explained in depth, and then the assessment that was conducted using a biosensor on participants to collect data is discussed. The stress categorization technique reduces feature collection redundancy by utilizing mutual data and genetic algorithms in a coupled manner. To additionally adjust machine learning hyperparameters, Bayesian optimization is used. The findings show that, when utilizing the NB method for 2-stage and 3-stage stress categorization, a blend of EDA, BP, and HR produces the maximum categorization accuracy of 98.3% and 97.1%, respectively. However, for 2-stage and 3-stage stress categorization, EDA and HR alone exhibit similar accuracy of 97.1% and 95.3%, respectively. Additionally, the research reveals proof that stress rates are lowered when engaging in meditative audio. These results demonstrate ways wearable sensors and ML may be used to detect and regulate stress in educational settings in real time.

title: Productive Sentiment Analysis with Various Feature Extraction Techniques Using Machine Learning Classifiers
authors: Priyanka Suresh Aher, Baisa Laxman Gunjal
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Productive sentiment analysis has emerged as a critical area of research to interpret user opinions embedded in large volumes of text data from social media platforms. Despite significant advancements, the field still faces substantial challenges, including noisy and unstructured content, high dimensionality, and semantic ambiguity inherent in user-generated comments. This study focuses on an end-to-end framework for sentiment analysis that encompasses systematic text comment data collection from diverse social media sources, robust preprocessing, and advanced feature extraction strategies. The preprocessing pipeline involves tokenization, normalization, stop-word removal, and lemmatization to ensure consistent and clean text representations. To enhance the discriminative power of sentiment features, multiple feature extraction techniques were applied, including lemmas, n-grams, natural language processing (NLP)-based embeddings, and co-relational feature mapping. These representations were evaluated using three machine learning classifiers: Naïve Bayes (NB), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost). A stratified cross-validation approach ensured unbiased performance assessment. Experimental results demonstrated that NLP-based feature extraction consistently outperformed other methods by effectively capturing contextual dependencies and semantic nuances. Specifically, the NLP features achieved an accuracy of 96.50 % with XGBoost, 96.30 % with SVM, and 95.50 % with NB. These findings underscore the significance of sophisticated feature engineering in enhancing sentiment classification performance. The proposed approach provides a productive and scalable solution adaptable to varied social media contexts.

title: Navigating the New Frontier: Security Vulnerabilities in Social IoT Compared to Conventional IoT
authors: Deborah Oladipo, Elhadj Benkhelifa, Tamara Zhukabayeva
publish: 2025 12th International Conference on Software Defined Systems (SDS)
abstract: The Integration of social networking capabilities with Internet of Things (IoT) technology has established Social Internet of Things (SIoT) as a new paradigm through which connected devices interact and cooperate socially. The integration of social networking with IoT brings greater connectivity along with better user experiences and automated systems but develops new security challenges which differ greatly from those in conventional IoT networks. This paper reveals new security attack vectors specifically impacting Social Internet of Things (SIoT) while exploring the distinct security challenges that emerge from device connectivity. Social Internet of Things (SIoT) presents distinctive security risks because its interconnected devices experience attacks that take advantage of their social network connections. This paper explores the weaknesses built into SIoT systems structure with special attention to risks created by direct device communication as well as between users and devices together with adaptable trust management. The comparison between emerging threats and traditional IoT security challenges is made to highlight the need for specific mitigation strategies. This article develops a complete approach to deal with threats specifically related to SIoT while delivering practical recommendations to improve the security and resilience of socially connected IoT networks.

title: Research on Visible-Infrared image fusion method based on deep learning
authors: Qingze Jiang, Kai Li, Xiaoyang Hu, Runmin Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechatronic Engineering and Artificial Intelligence (MEAI)
abstract: Based on the SwinFusion architecture, this study introduces improvements to enhance the performance of visible and infrared image fusion. First, to strengthen the model’s capability in modeling multi-scale features, a low-rank sparse representation module from the representation learning-guided infrared and visible fusion network (LRRNet) is incorporated between the shallow and deep feature extraction stages. Second, in the multi-modal feature fusion part of the backbone network, the L1 norm-based fusion strategy from SwinFuse is adopted to more effectively integrate information from different modalities. Experimental results demonstrate that the improved model significantly enhances the structural consistency and visual quality of fused images on the DroneVehicle dataset, more accurately extracting and preserving the feature distributions of each modality, thereby validating the effectiveness of the proposed improvement strategies.

title: Bus Odyssey: The Intelligent College Bus Tracking Companion
authors: Neetha K, Melvin Varghese, Milan Mani Paul, Vaishnav K Rao, Viswanath D
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Smart and Intelligent Systems (SISCON)
abstract: In today's intelligent and technology-dependent era traditional bus tracking methodologies are not an effective and efficient solution. Traditional tracking methodologies waste the time of commuters. This paper aims to develop a system for efficient tracking of college buses. The system integrates a GPS NEO-7M module with an ESP-32. The GPS module continuously captures the real time location of the buses and transmits this data to the ESP 32 for processing. These data are uploaded to a cloud-based real-time database, namely Supabase. This makes it very efficient to store and retrieve this data without much lag. A Flutter-based mobile application fetches this data and displays it on a map. This enables users to track the live location of the bus. The app has a feature to set custom notification. The user can enter custom latitude-longitude values to set a notification point, and when the bus reaches that point a notification will be sent by the app. The system was set up in a bus run via the Koonammavu-Paravoor route route. The data was fetched every 8 seconds to accurately determine the position of the bus and display it on the app. The proposed system is cheaper than other existing systems due to the use of Supabase an OpenstreetMap. This system also has the feature to set custom notification points by the user, which is not seen in other systems.

title: A Channel-Selective, Bandwidth-Reconfigurable Wideband Low Noise Amplifier with Dual-Frequency N-Path Filters
authors: Soumya Gupta, Sashank Krishnamurthy, Ashoke Ravi, Ofir Degani, Arun Natarajan
publish: 2026 IEEE 26th Topical Meeting on Silicon Monolithic Integrated Circuits in RF Systems (SiRF)
abstract: The paper presents a reconfigurable channel-selective wideband low-noise amplifier (LNA) incorporating dual-frequency, symmetrically staggered, differential N-path filters (NPF). The use of staggered NPFs in conjunction with current summation enables a tunable 4th-order bandpass response for a range of channel bandwidths. An integrated 14nm CMOS prototype, with a compact active area of 0.114mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, consumes up to 41.8mW demonstrating a wide operating frequency range from 1.5 GHz to 3.5 GHz, reconfigurable bandwidth from 70MHz to 370 MHz, an in-band gain of 15.5 dB and a minimum noise figure (NF) of 5.5 dB.

title: Building 3D Images from 2D CT Slices: Insights into Pulmonary Diseases
authors: Asma Parveen I. Siddavatam, Prachit P. Paralikar, Vedang Khandagale, Mohd. Sufiyan Siddiqui, Swarali Dhobale
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: The distinction between COVID-19 and Community Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) is an important challenge in medical imaging due to overlapping features. To address this, a comparative study was done by creating 3D models of CAP, COVID, and healthy lungs from 2D CT slices based on the Marching Cubes and Ray Casting algorithms. Furthermore, because rib fractures may affect pulmonary function and disease progression, 3D reconstruction of rib structures was also performed using the same methods. To enhance the accessibility of 3D visualization technology for general practitioners and patients, a comparative performance evaluation of 3D rendering techniques was conducted on a CPU-based laptop and a GPUbased laptop, examining computational efficiency and image quality The research will seek to offer insights for maximizing 3D medical imaging workflow while providing wide applicability in clinical environments.

title: Comparative Evaluation of Extractive Summarization Algorithms on Multi-Topic News Articles in Portuguese
authors: Namita Mishra, Nijeoma Joaozinho Indi, Satya Ranjan Dash, Aloka Fernando, Bashir Maina Saleh, Tesfa Tegegne Asfaw
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: This study addresses the increased demand for short multitopic news summaries, which are essential in today's information-rich environment. It assesses how successfully extractive summarization algorithms summarize a 27-paragraph Portuguese news article with two subjects, aiming at a 5 sentence reference summary (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3: 2$</tex> split). Ten methods were tested: length-based, TF-IDF, TextRank, BERT, LexRank, reduction Algorithm, Luhn's Algorithm, KLSum, LSA, and fuzzy clustering for sentence selection based on sentence length, word frequency, graph linkages, and meaning similarity. ROUGE scores revealed LexRank leading at 67.64 ROUGE-1, followed by Luhn's (63.78) and LSA (62.91), with BERT trailing at 32.00. These results support basic graph and traditional approaches over complicated ones for Portuguese news, which will guide better summary tools and future study.

title: Heart Rate Extraction of Moving Target with mm-Wave FMCW Radar and Depth Camera
authors: Baole Yang, Dachuan Mei
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: Long-term monitoring of heart activity has the potential to reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD), and heart rate is one of the key indicators of heart activity. Using millimeter-wave radar and other non-contact means to extract heart rate has achieved profound results. However, most of this research focuses on heart rate extraction of stationary targets. This condition limits the application scenarios of radar-based non-contact heart rate extraction because people would not stay in the same position for the long term. This paper proposes a new method combining millimeter-wave radar and a depth camera to extract the heart rate of a moving target. We use a depth camera to capture the human motion trajectory and the depth profile to eliminate the impact of wide-range motion on heart rate extraction. We use heartbeat harmonics less affected by body swaying to restore the heartbeat. We use the variational mode decomposition (VMD) algorithm to decompose the obtained heartbeat signal and improve the accuracy of heart rate extraction through convolution. Experimental results show that our method can extract the target’s motion trajectory in the presence of static clutters and moving interference. The proposed method achieves an absolute error of 4.4 beats per minute (bpm), with respect to the electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring device.

title: The Impact of Custom Layer in CNN with GlobalAveragePooling2D and Dropout for Tomato Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Prayoga, Guruh Fajar Shidik, Purwanto
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Tomatoes are one of the most valuable commercial agricultural commodities in the world and a staple ingredient in many global cuisines; however, they are highly susceptible to various diseases. If these diseases are not detected early, the impact can be devastating, ranging from significant crop losses to the potential for large-scale crop failure. The PlantVillage Dataset is now the most extensive publicly available collection of leaf images for disease identification. In this dataset, plant pathologists have identified 54,309 images of healthy and diseased leaves from 14 different plant species. There are 18,160 tomato leaf images, divided into nine disease categories and one healthy category. This research utilizes the MobileNetV2 architecture as the foundation for the model, optimized for computational efficiency. To enhance the model’s performance and generalization capabilities, the base architecture was customized by adding a GlobalAveragePooling2D layer, several dense layers, and a Dropout layer. These modifications aim to achieve better accuracy and generalization without sacrificing computational efficiency, making it suitable for applications on devices with limited resources. The findings demonstrate that incorporating custom dense layers into a pre-trained CNN enhances its ability to classify complex plant disease datasets accurately. The use of MobileNetV2 with custom layers improves accuracy by 99.40%, demonstrating its effectiveness in handling lightweight models. These results provide actionable insights for developing efficient and accurate models for agricultural disease diagnosis, with potential applications in real-time plant health monitoring and management systems

title: A Compact K-Band Power Amplifier Employing Transformer-Based Load Optimization for High PAE and Output Power Over a Wide Bandwidth
authors: Joonseok Park, Hyunsoo Kim, Changkun Park
publish: 2026 IEEE Topical Conference on RF/Microwave Power Amplifiers for Radio and Wireless Applications (PAWR)
abstract: In this study, we present a K-band CMOS power amplifier (PA) that achieves high output power and power-added efficiency (PAE) by employing transformer-based load optimization technique. To this end, we optimize the load impedance of the power stage by controlling the output transformer, thereby minimizing the loss typically incurred in output matching. Furthermore, we propose a technique to minimize chip area by integrating additional inductance, normally required for matching, into the secondary winding of the transformer. To verify the feasibility of the proposed structure, the PA is designed using 65-nm RFCMOS. The measured results show a P1dB of 23.4-24.2 dBm, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P}_\text{SAT}$</tex> of 24.4-25.0 dBm, and a peak PAE of 29.7-32.6% across the 15–20 GHz frequency range. When 5G NR modulated signals are used, the output power and PAE measured over the same frequency range are 17.1-18.4 dBm and 12.7-16.7%, respectively, with an EVM below –25 dB.

title: SmartECushion: Design of a solution to detect remote working postures based on sensors and Deep Learning models
authors: Enzo Camargo, Eduardo Caballero, Cesar Salas
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: The increasing use of computers at home—driven by telecommuting, virtual education, and digital entertainment—has led to a growing incidence of musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs). These conditions often begin with pain in the neck, shoulders, back, and lower back, and are associated with prolonged adoption of improper postures without rest or active breaks. Such sedentary habits not only compromise physical well-being but also reduce productivity and contribute to increased healthcare costs and absenteeism. SmartECushion is a comprehensive solution for detecting improper postures during remote work. It integrates an ergonomic cushion embedded with ten force sensors, an Arduino Mega 2560 + ESP32 module for data acquisition, and a hybrid deep learning pipeline combining MLP and CNN architectures. Implemented using TensorFlow 2 and Keras 3, the model features a CNN branch to capture the temporal dynamics of sensor data and an MLP branch that incorporates user demographic information (gender, height, weight, BMI, and waist circumference). The model achieves 87.7% accuracy and a weighted F1-score of 87.5% on data from unseen users. For deployment, ONNX inference is served via a FastAPI microservice, decoupled from an automatic retraining service, all orchestrated using Docker containers to ensure scalability and continuous model updates.

title: Added Value of Synthetic T1/T2-Weighted MR Images in the Segmentation and Staging of Meningioma
authors: Masoud Noroozi, Sayna Jamaati, Peyman Sharifian, Mahsa Karbasi, Esmaeil Gharepapagh, Alireza Karimian, Hossein Arabi, Sahar Rezaei
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Accurate pre-operative classification and volumetric definition of intracranial meningiomas are paramount to the development of appropriate surveillance, surgical, and radiotherapeutic approaches. The traditional post-contrast T1-weighted MRI (T1c) is used clinically but is time-consuming to contour and not always available or against indications. To assess how a bias-corrected native-T1/ T2-weighted ratio (T1(n)/T2(w)) map, combined with fully automated segmentation and grading networks, can enhance meningioma work-up without gadolinium. The novelty of this research lies in the use of T1n/T2w information to generate synthetic images, replacing the need for four separate MRI sequences. The BraTS-MEN multi-centre dataset (685 scans to be segmented and 868 scans to be graded) was skullstripped and registered to the atlas. A 3-D nnU-Net V2 was trained to segment tumors using (i) T1c and (ii) T1n/T2w volumes. The resulting masks were either presented directly or supplemented with the four mpMRI channels in a 3-D ResNet-18 to predict WHO grades 1-2. Performance was measured as Dice, IoU, accuracy, and class-based sensitivity/specificity. T1c performed the best in terms of geometry Dice (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$92.12 \pm 4.14 \%$</tex>) and IoU (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$86.35 \pm 11.3 \%$</tex>). The T1n/T2w map nonetheless maintained a clinically satisfactory Dice of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$82.1 \pm 11.68$</tex> with decreased false-positive voxels in neighboring dura. The ratio-based pipeline was superior to the T1c model in all global measures (accuracy 0.61 vs 0.427; mean Dice 0.558 vs 0.425) and the sensitivity of high-grade (>= WHO II) tumor was over twofold higher (0.70 vs 0.31). The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{T} 1 \mathrm{n} / \mathrm{T} 2 \mathrm{w}$</tex> ratio map, as a gadolinium-free contrast agent, and the nnU-Net V2 in segmentation and ResNet-18 in the classifier demonstrate strength in automated grade assessment accuracy and preventing the under-treatment of aggressive meningiomas. An open-source, hardware-light, and easily reproducible workflow indicates a potential avenue of non-invasive, contrast-sparing pre-operative assessment that could be validated in a multi-institutional prospective setting.

title: Welcome Messages
