abstract: Black holes, traditionally studied as solutions of Einstein's equations, exhibit rich thermodynamic behavior, including well-defined phase transitions. In this work, we establish a unified topological framework to describe various black hole phase transitions, including Davies-type and extremal phase transitions. Our approach reveals a deeper underlying structure in black hole thermodynamics, demonstrating that these seemingly distinct transitions can be connected through topology. Furthermore, we show that the classification of phase transitions can be enriched by the introduction of non-zero topological charges, offering a novel perspective beyond conventional thermodynamic analysis. These findings not only strengthen the thermodynamic interpretation of black holes but also open new avenues for exploring phase transitions in gravitational systems from a topological standpoint.

title: Analysis of Soft Tissue Tumors Using CNN Classifier
authors: Piyush Bagla, Amit Kumar Mishra, Manohar Singh, Priyansh Kapruwan, K P Sharma, Radhika Malhotra
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Soft-tissue tumors (STTs) encompass a diverse group of tumors that arise in non-skeletal and non-epithelial tissues, presenting significant challenges in detection and classification. The inconsistency in their origin and behavior hinders the application of conventional diagnostic methods. While histological procedures are considered the gold standard, they can be labor-intensive and susceptible to errors arising from observer interpretation, leading to potential misdiagnosis. Consequently, there is an increasing demand for more efficient, consistent, and accurate diagnostic measures. In the realm of healthcare, machine learning (ML) techniques have demonstrated a potential to simplify complex diagnostic tasks, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The objective of this research is to enhance the identification and categorization of STTs using CNNs, with a specific focus on improving the accuracy of treatment, particularly radiation therapy. We will apply CNN models to the information obtained through multiparametric magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to extract crucial tumor features that may be overlooked by existing methods. This automated approach offers greater reliability by employing a superior tumor classification system that minimizes variability in results, which is influenced by the observer, and diagnostic errors. In particular, our approach targets skinrelated soft-tissue tumors (STTs), where accurate diagnosis is paramount for treatment. The enhanced retrieval of pertinent imaging data will assist physicians in distinguishing between various tumor types and provide valuable information for treatment decision-making. Ultimately, this technology may facilitate the expedited conduct of the diagnostic process and the quality of tumor characterization, ultimately benefiting patients and optimizing radiation therapy plans.

title: Development of an Arabic voice control system using convolutional neural networks CNN
authors: Adil Bakri, Youcef Brik, Ely Cheikh Aida, Said Fahem, Salah Khennouf
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: This work focuses on the development and realization of a system designed to be controlled by Arabic speech recognition, aimed at improving the autonomy of people with reduced mobility. The system integrates a convolutional neural network (CNN) for voice command classification, combined with a hardware architecture based on Raspberry Pi 4 (voice processing) and Arduino Uno (motor control). Two acoustic feature extraction techniques, namely Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCC) and the short-time Fourier transform (STFT), are evaluated and compared. Experimental results demonstrate an overall accuracy of 98% with MFCC, slightly surpassing TFCT (97%). The system recognizes five Arabic commands with an optimal response time, validating its effectiveness under realistic conditions. This solution opens up prospects for inclusive assistive technologies adapted to Arabic linguistic contexts.

title: Hybrid AE-LSTM-CNN Model for Intelligent Intrusion Detection in IoT Networks
authors: Manjit Kumar Nayak, Debasis Gountia, Niranjan Kumar Ray
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: The cyber attack has been growing rapidly on IoT(Internet of things). Existing security mechanisms are no longer sufficient to detect and mitigate these attacks. Intrusion Detection Systems have come up as the second line of defense; however, classic techniques have always had problems with novel and complex attacks. This paper presents a proposed hybrid deep learning-based intrusion detection system that combines Autoencoder, LSTM, and CNN architectures in a complementary fashion to achieve accurate and robust intrusion detection. The evaluated system has been tested with the NSL-KDD dataset, where a stratified cross-validation technique is applied for the generalizability of the result. The approach demonstrates very high accuracy, precision, and recall and is markedly superior to traditional and single-model deep learning approaches. Comprehensive literature reviews are provided, motivation and objectives are discussed, research gaps are identified, and the workflow, model architecture, dataset characteristics, and result analysis have been detailed.

title: Risk Analysis of Employment Agreements Using a BERT-Based Fusion Model
authors: Pragya Rajput, Simran, Heemaal Jaglan, Dheeraj Joshi
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Although employment agreements are generally the basis of employer-employee correlation, they are typically thick legal documents with provisions that present high risks to either or both of the parties. Identifying these risks is a time-consuming and monotonous manual review subject to human error. The paper discusses the necessity of an automated, precise and efficient solution by proposing the development of a new deep learning model that will assess the risks in the employment contracts. We present a hybrid model, combining a Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) layer with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network. The architecture builds on the deep contextual knowledge of language that BERT-provides, the salient local features extraction of CNN, and long-range sequential dependencies by BiLSTM. The model was developed and evaluated on a hand-labeled dataset that contains employment agreement clauses categorized as either risky or not risky. Our results indicate that the fusion model achieves an impressive F1-score of 0.91, outperforming baseline systems like the regular BERT model. The findings suggest that combining different NLP techniques can significantly improve how contracts are analyzed, helping make the review process clearer and more reliable for workers, companies, and lawyers who aim to minimize risks in agreements.

title: Smart Electronic Voting Machine with Biometric Authentication and Accessible Features
authors: Gurram MadhuPriya, Vislavath Indra, J. Swetha Priyanka
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: India is democratic nation. The establishment of a secure, transparent, and inclusive voting system is necessary to enhance democratic practices. This study proposes a Smart Electronic Voting Machine (EVM) with biometric authentication and accessibility features for voters with visual impairments. The technology prevents multiple voting attempts and impersonation by utilizing fingerprint recognition to authenticate authorised voters. The machine provides audio instructions in local languages so that visually impaired people can follow the voting process step-by-step. An LCD screen and a physical voting button make up the design’s user-friendly interface. The STM32 microcontroller, which offers reliable performance, low power consumption, and dependability, is used in its implementation. The design’s user-friendly interface consists of an LCD display and a physical voting button. Its implementation makes use of the STM32 microcontroller, which offers credible performance, low power consumption and reliabilty.

title: Compact High Isolation Narrow-Band 120 GHz Travelling-Wave SPST with λ/4 Transformer
authors: Quentin Courte, Martin Rack, Yiyi Yan, Andreia Cathelin, Jean-Pierre Raskin, Dimitri Lederer
publish: 2026 IEEE 26th Topical Meeting on Silicon Monolithic Integrated Circuits in RF Systems (SiRF)
abstract: This paper presents the design methodology and measurement results of an ultra-compact SPST switch designed in 28 nm FD-SOI technology at 120 GHz. Including the lambda/4 impedance transformer, the core area is only 0.0055 mm2, which presents significant reduction compared to most designs using the same Travelling-Wave topology. Measured isolation is also better than 45 dB around the 106–116 GHz BW and better than 40 dB from DC up to 160 GHz. Peak Insertion Losses (IL) at 110 GHz are -3.26 dB for standard resistivity substrate (Std), commercially available for this technology, and can be increased up to -2.76 dB thanks to High-Resistivity substrates (HR).

title: Detecting Human-Written and AI-Generated Phishing Emails with DistilBERT and Explainable AI
authors: Athira Anil Kumar, T P Imthias Ahamed, Nellimala Abdul Shukoor
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Phishing remains a major cybersecurity threat, and the rise of large language models (LLMs) has enabled attackers to generate convincing, grammatically fluent phishing emails that evade traditional filters. Existing research has primarily addressed binary detection (phishing vs. legitimate), often relying on either classical machine learning or transformers in isolation, with limited focus on AI-generated phishing and interpretability. This paper presents a transformer-based framework for tri-class classification, distinguishing between legitimate emails, human-written phishing, and AI-generated phishing. We finetune DistilBERT on a hybrid dataset that combines legitimate, human-phishing, and synthetic AI-phishing emails to evaluate its detection capability under realistic conditions. To enhance transparency, we integrate explainability methods (LIME and SHAP), providing insight into token-level model decisions and highlighting distinct linguistic cues across the three classes. Experimental results show that DistilBERT achieves near-perfect accuracy (≈99−100%) in all categories, with strong precision and recall for the harder-to-detect AI-generated phishing class. The contributions of this work are threefold: (1) demonstrating the effectiveness of transformer based model for phishing detection in a tri-class classification setting ( legitimate emails, human-written phishing, and AI-generated phishing ), (2) establishing an interpretable and reproducible evaluation protocol for future benchmarking, and (3) incorporating explainable AI (XAI) techniques, such as SHAP and LIME, to provide transparency into model predictions and improve trustworthiness.

title: Non-Invasive Detection of Atherosclerosis and Aneurysm via Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy: A Finite Element Simulation Study
authors: Shaghayegh Shokri, Rasool Baghbani, Masoomeh Ashoorirad
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Atherosclerosis and aneurysm are among the most dangerous and well-known diseases of the cardiovascular system, causing 19.8 million deaths in 2022, of which 85% died due to heart attack or stroke. Therefore, early detection of these conditions can play a significant role in preventing their various complications. In this study, electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used as a non-invasive method for diagnosing both diseases. The primary objective of this paper is to assess the discriminative capability of EIS between normal tissue, atherosclerotic tissue, and aneurysmal tissue, as well as to investigate how changes in geometric parameters in these two pathological types affect the tissue's impedance response. For this purpose, a three-dimensional model of healthy and diseased tissues was developed using COMSOL software. The results showed that diseased tissues exhibit distinct impedance characteristics compared to normal tissue; Significant increases in electrical impedance are observed in atherosclerosis, while decreases are seen in aneurysm, which are among the identified features. Overall, findings from this study indicate that electrical impedance spectroscopy can be used as a complementary, rapid, cost-effective, and real-time method for detecting both atherosclerosis and aneurysms. Furthermore, accurate numerical modeling can serve as a valuable tool for the initial design of EIS-based diagnostic devices.

title: Face Detection and Masked Face Recognition with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNS)
authors: Barun Kumar Arya, Praveen Kumar Rai, Pankaj Kumar
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: In this paper, face detection and masked-face recognition have been integrated with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to solve the major issues within contemporary security systems. As face masks become common and biometric identification and recognition are increasingly required, traditional methods of recognition frequently cannot perform in a restricted environment. To address this shortcoming, we suggest a smart deep learning model that uses the latest CNN models to perform accurate face detection and face recognition, even when the subject wears a mask. The system is highly accurate, robust, and efficient, and that makes it a good candidate in real-life security applications. This arrangement overcomes the limitations of the old algorithms by utilizing the potential of CNNs to acquire sophisticated patterns across extensive data sets to improve identity verification in sensitive settings. In the end, our effort will lead to a resiliency in security solutions that will be capable of operating in dynamic and mask- worn environments.

title: Four Port X-mmWave MIMO Antenna for HighSpeed Wireless Applications
authors: Malini Soman, Manish Sharma, Seema, Pradeep Kumar
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: In this research paper, a single-port, dual-port, and four-port multiband MIMO antenna is reported. The antenna with MIMO version is designed on a thin Rogers substrate occupying a net volume of 20.0 mm×20.0 mm×0.254 mm. The irregular polygon of 6 sides is used as a radiating-patch, which is connected to a tapered feed and a rectangular ground printed on the opposite surface. The antenna occupies dual impedance bandwidths of 9.14GHz-13.05GHz (X-band); 17.48GHz-39.05GHz (FR2 band). The MIMO antenna maintains isolation of more than 15.0 dB with ECC<0.01, DG>9.98 dB, TARC <-10.0 dB, and CCL <0.15 b/s/Hz. The maximum peak gain of 5.89 dBi and efficiency of more than 84% with stable radiation patterns find the proposed MIMO antenna well-suited for wireless applications.

title: Drug Abuse Detection Systems Based on Machine Learning Using Data Fusion of Biomedical Sensor Network: A Comparative Study
authors: Marwa Adel Abdul Reda, Sadik Kamel Gharghan, Mohammed Saeed Mohammed
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Drug abuse is a significant health challenge and problem worldwide due to its impact on society and its stability. Methamphetamine stands out in particular due to its highly addictive nature and profound neurological and physiological effects. Methamphetamine use causes significant changes in an individual’s vital signs, complicating the task of early and accurate diagnosis for healthcare providers. Therefore, researchers sought to develop real-time, non-invasive detection systems based on biomarkers combined with artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms. The present paper compares the performance of eight machine learning algorithms, AdaBoost, Naïve Bayes, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, Neural Network, Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Support Vector Machine, based on a dataset of vital signs for normal and meth users. The results show that the Random Forest achieved an accuracy of 97.2%, the highest performance among other algorithms in this study. In conclusion, modern diagnostic techniques provide comprehensive and accurate information on drug abuse.

title: Arm Model for Elbow Motor Control
authors: Mohammad-Reza Sayyed Noorani, Abbas Jafarpour Mahalleh, Kimiya Khojand
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: This study aimed to develop a model-based reinforcement learning (RL) framework designed to partially emulate central nervous system (CNS) learning processes for goal-directed motor control. The RL model, implemented using a double deep Q-learning (DDQN) algorithm, interacting with a biomechanical arm model served as the simulated environment. The environment comprised a Hill-type musculoskeletal representation of the biceps brachii and triceps brachii muscles, enabling elbow flexion-extension over a range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{1 3 5}{}^{\circ}$</tex>. Within this setup, the RL agent received state information, including elbow joint angle and velocity, from the environment and generated muscle activation signals as control outputs. These signals acted on the Hill-based biomechanical model, allowing the agent to learn reaching toward specified target through iterative episodes. To validate biomechanical realism, forward dynamics simulations were performed in OpenSim using a customized arm model driven by the RL-generated excitations. Results demonstrated that the agent successfully acquired stable and biologically plausible motor strategies.

title: Towards Smarter Agriculture: Deep Learning-Based Multistage Detection of Leaf Diseases
authors: S. N. Tirumala Rao, Koyyalamudi Venkata Ramesh, Bangaru Surya Prasad, Thullibilli Nagaiah, Shaik Abdul Nabi, Yerrarapu Sravani Devi, Sireesha Moturi
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Farmers are currently facing a dilemma in identifying plant pathogens. Why is this? Using image data from plant leaves, scientists are currently exploring ways to use deep learning to identify plant-related diseases. PlantVillage Dataset is being utilized in this project to showcase approximately 38 categories of plant leaves. However, for better training and evaluation, only 10 classes with 200 images each were selected to ensure balanced learning. The preprocessing techniques for image size and CLAHE are the first ones. These steps help in enhancing image quality and bringing out clearer disease features. Features are extracted from the images using PCFAN. We used CV2 (Computer Vision 2), and EfficientNet_B0 is used to pinpoint specific illnesses. The model was trained using transfer learning and achieved strong classification accuracy of 97.9% on the testing dataset and 96.2% accuracy on unseen validation data. Evaluation metrics like precision, recall, and F1-score consistently remained above 95% across all disease categories. Red Fox Optimization is used for the precise segmenting of affected areas, improving the focus on diseased regions and achieving segmentation accuracy above 94%. This overall approach supports early and reliable disease identification in plants.

title: Autonomous Delivery Drone
authors: Nishanth N, Nethaji PA, Vishal KS, Jayanthi Vinay Kumar, Abhishek S
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Smart and Intelligent Systems (SISCON)
abstract: This paper contains presentation of the control system for an autonomous UAV (unmanned air vehicle). The complete explanation of hardware and software solutions. A system that enables delivery drones to autonomously navigate and deliver packages at various locations and without the need for any external markers as currently used. This developed by recent advancements in deep learning that can identify sites at which to deliver packages. The proposed. We are using Mission Planner software for planning the path for package delivery.

title: An 85–110 GHz Class-AB SiGe Power Amplifier Using Doubly-Tuned Transformer and Over-Neutralization with 13.7% Peak PAE
authors: Eren Vardarli, Austin Ying-Kuang Chen, Michael Schröter
publish: 2026 IEEE 26th Topical Meeting on Silicon Monolithic Integrated Circuits in RF Systems (SiRF)
abstract: An 85–110 GHz two-stage differential class-AB power amplifier (PA) implemented in a 130-nm SiGe:C BiCMOS technology is presented. An over-neutralization technique is applied to enhance gain, output power, and efficiency while maintaining overall stability. In addition, a doubly-tuned staggered transformer for interstage matching is employed to further extend the bandwidth (BW). At 94/105 GHz, the measured power gain, saturated output power <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{P}_{\text{SAT}})$</tex>, and output-referred 1-dB compression point (OP1dB) are 16.2/13 dB, 9.4/9.6 dBm, and 6/7.6 dBm, respectively. The PA achieves a 1-dB large-signal power BW of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$> 25$</tex> GHz, from 85 GHz to >110 GHz (limited by the measurement setup). The measured power-added efficiency (PAE) at peak/6-dB power back-off (PBO) is 13.7 / 7.5% and 10.5/6.7% at 94GHz and 105 GHz, respectively. The PA consumes a DC power of 41.9 mW from a 1.6-V supply and occupies a total chip area of 0.36 mm2, including the pads.

title: Making Root Cause Localization on FPGA Simulation Tools Robust
authors: Xinlong Zhang, Shikai Guo, Zhenkan Fu, Pingchu Dong, Ning Wang, Xiaochen Li, He Jiang
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None

title: Resilience-Oriented Allocation of FI and RCS Considering Multi-Stage Collaborative Restoration with Improved Integer L-Shaped Method
authors: Dingyuan Luo, Yiheng Bian, Zheng Zhou, Qiming Yang, Gengfeng Li
abstract: Extreme disasters pose significant threats to distribution networks. While the allocation of Fault Indicators (FIs) and Sectionalizing Switches (SSs) can mitigate these impacts by accelerating fault localization and isolation, widespread deployment is often constrained by limited budgets. To address this, this paper proposes a resilience-oriented joint allocation optimization method for FIs and Remote Control Switches (RCSs). A two-stage stochastic programming model is constructed with the objective of minimizing the Loss of Load Energy (LLE). This model comprehensively incorporates the time-sequence of multiple faults, Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) patrolling strategies, and collaborative restoration measures involving fault isolation and repair crews. To efficiently handle the integer variables within the optimization, an improved integer L-shaped method is employed. The effectiveness of the proposed method is verified on a modified IEEE-123 system.

title: Erdős goes Neural Pipeline-Based User Pairing in Wireless Communication Networks
authors: Sharan Mourya, Pavan Reddy, SaiDhiraj Amuru, Kiran Kumar Kuchi
abstract: The next-generation Internet-of-Thing (IoT) systems require support for massive device connectivity, low latency, and high spectral efficiency. Scheduling the users on the same time-frequency resources while separating them spatially can help in realizing the aforementioned requirements. selecting users to be scheduled together while minimizing interference and maximizing throughput (known as user-pairing) is essentially an NP-hard optimization problem. Recently, deep neural networks have emerged as a solution to solve NP-hard wireless resource allocation problems in real-time. In this paper, we propose an unsupervised graph neural network (GNN) approach to efficiently solve the user pairing problem. Our proposed method utilizes the Erdős goes neural pipeline to significantly outperform other scheduling methods such as k-means and semi-orthogonal user scheduling (SUS). At 20 dB SNR, our proposed approach achieves a 49% better sum rate than k-means and a staggering 95% better sum rate than SUS while consuming minimal time and resources.

title: Quantifying Methane-Climate Interactions in Eastern Saudi Arabia Using Geospatial and Machine Learning Modelling
authors: Md Masudur Rahman, Roman Shults, Arfan Arshad, Rajendran Sankaran, Thanh-Nhan-Duc Tran, Hatem Magdy Keshk, Asif Raihan, Venkataraman Lakshmi, Mahfuzur Rahman
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Methane (CH₄) is a potent greenhouse gas that plays a critical role in global warming, yet its relationship with climate variables remains underexplored, particularly in Eastern Saudi Arabia, which hosts over 70% of the nation's oil fields. This study presents the comprehensive analysis of the spatiotemporal variability of CH₄ and its climatic drivers using TROPOMI/Sentinel-5P data from 2019 to 2024. Results reveal significant annual and seasonal fluctuations, with CH₄ concentrations rising from 1892 ppb to 1927 ppb. Seasonal patterns show peak concentrations in summer and autumn and lower values in winter and spring. Spatial trend analysis indicates an average increase of 5.09 ppb yr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> (MK-test). These variations are influenced by both anthropogenic and climatic factors. To examine the relationship between CH₄ and climate variables, a geographical model is applied, achieving an overall R² of 0.82. We found that CH₄ is negatively correlated with temperature, solar radiation, and wind speed, but positively correlated with precipitation and humidity. Specifically, the mean weighted regression coefficient between CH₄ and temperature was -3.709, suggesting a decline of 3.709 ppb yr<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-1</sup> for each unit increase in temperature, which is likely due to enhanced oxidative processes under the region's high temperatures, driven by increased ozone and hydroxyl radicals (OH) that promote CH₄ degradation. The Random Forest model further quantifies these relationships, yielding an overall R² of 0.79 and an RMSE of 2.934 ppb. The feature importance analysis identifies temperature and humidity as the most influential factors, followed by solar radiation, precipitation, and wind speed. These findings provide critical insights into CH₄ dynamics in arid regions, offering valuable guidance for future research and policymaking.

title: Target-Mounted Intelligent Reflecting Surface for Electromagnetic Spoofing
authors: Qingjie Wu, Xue Xiong, Shaoe Lin, Zhen Liu, Changji Wang
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Electronic countermeasure (ECM) is crucial for preventing target exposure. However, traditional ECM technologies may exhibit limited adaptability or incur high hardware costs, and may even risk exposing the target. To tackle the above challenges, we propose in this paper an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS)-aided electromagnetic spoofing system, where an IRS is mounted on the target to redirect the reflected signals toward surrounding clusters to create decoy targets for confusing radar detection, while simultaneously making the genuine target invisible to radars. Specifically, we optimize the IRS reflection to maximize the sum received signal power at all radars from the cluster direction and ensure that each radar’s received signal power from the target direction remains below a given detection threshold. For the single-radar and single-cluster setup, we solve the IRS reflection optimization problem by using the Lagrange multiplier method and derive a semi-closed-form optimal solution, which is then generalized to the multi-radar and multi-cluster case. Furthermore, a spoofing performance upper bound for the single-radar case is obtained based on the phase alignment method, and a low-complexity closed-form solution based on minimum mean-square error (MMSE) criteria is developed for the multi-radar case. Additionally, we propose practical low-complexity estimation schemes at the target to acquire angle-of-arrival (AoA) and/or signal power gain information via a small number of receive sensing devices. Simulation results validate the performance advantages of our proposed IRS-aided electromagnetic spoofing system with the proposed IRS reflection designs, as compared to the baseline systems.

title: Federated Spatial-Temporal Synchronous Graph Convolutional Network for Cross-City Traffic Prediction
authors: Xian Yu, Yinxin Bao, Weiwei Jiang, Quan Shi
abstract: Accurate traffic forecasting plays a vital role in modern intelligent transportation systems and enables proactive traffic management and well-informed decision-making. Although existing deep learning-based models have achieved remarkable progress, they often rely heavily on complete data and struggle to generalize across cities with diverse spatial-temporal patterns and limited observations. Moreover, the direct sharing of raw data between cities is frequently infeasible because of increasing privacy concerns. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework named federated spatial-temporal synchronous graph convolutional network (Fed-STSGCN), which enables cross-city traffic prediction without exposing private data. Fed-STSGCN adopts a decentralized learning paradigm that is aligned with federated and privacy-preserving learning, which enables autonomous local model training and collaborative knowledge sharing across distributed traffic agents. The model uses a spatial-temporal synchronous graph construction strategy that jointly captures spatial correlations and temporal causality. On top of this structure, we design stacked spatial-temporal synchronous graph convolution modules to extract high-level representations and employ a cosine similarity-aware aggregation mechanism to improve global knowledge transfer under the federated setting. By supporting privacy-aware cross-region adaptation under data-scarce conditions, the proposed framework is well suited for consumer-facing intelligent transportation applications, in which traffic data are generated and processed in a distributed manner across heterogeneous sensing and computing environments. Extensive experiments are conducted on twelve cross-city forecasting tasks derived from four real-world datasets. The results show that Fed-STSGCN achieves notable error reductions across all the considered evaluation metrics, which range from 0.32%-13.22% in traffic flow prediction and from 2.04%-12.78% in traffic speed prediction, compared with the state-of-the-art baseline, thus demonstrating its robustness and effectiveness in cross-city and data-scarce settings.

title: Ultrathin Synchronous Permanent Magnet Micromotor With 400 µm Outer Diameter for in-Body Health Applications_supp1-3660600.mp4
authors: Efren Diez-Jimenez
abstract: Micromotors can be used to build up complex microtools for health applications as for example steerable catheters or optical and ultrasonic imaging system. The thinner and smaller the micromotors are, the less invasive during the use or implantation. In this article, an ultrathin synchronous permanent magnet micromotor with 400 µm outer diameter and 800 µm length is presented. The main novelty of this article is the degree of miniaturization: this motor 2.5 thinner diameter and 15 times smaller volume than previous similar micromotor in the state of the art, while keeping high torque density. The micromotor demonstrated operation up to 27000 r/min, a torque density larger than 0.3 kN·m/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> (twice larger than closest miniaturized motor) and a power consumption of 3 mW. The motor has proven to be durable over time demonstrating a lifetime of more than 70 h. The micromotor is capable to withstand and rotate loads one order of magnitude larger that its own rotor weight. Its ultrathin diameter and its high torque density permits to foresee its use in minimally invasive microcatheter tools or implantable devices with less damage during insertion processes.

title: Low-Complexity Matched Filters Removing ISI/ICI of OTFS Radar Using Partial FFT
authors: Jeong-Hoon Park, Jong-Ho Lee, Seongwook Lee, Seong-Cheol Kim
abstract: Orthogonal time-frequency space (OTFS) modulation is a promising option for its capability of integrated sensing and communication in high-mobility scenarios. However, the high computational complexity (CC) of the OTFS receiver is required to detect target signals with severe inter-carrier interference and inter-symbol interference. Therefore, we propose a low-complexity matched filter for the OTFS radar by considering three search modes for target detection in delay-Doppler (DD) domain: 1) total search to detect target with long delay and high Doppler frequency shift, 2) partial search for fast computation, 3) dense-grid partial search to reduce the straddle loss which occurs due to mismatch in target parameters and DD grid. By using the partial fast Fourier transform, CC of the proposed methods is significantly reduced compared to the conventional MF. In addition, the simulation with the proposed dense-grid partial search results in enhanced probability of detection, showing the trade-off between computations and radar performance.

title: Adaptive Object Exploration and Mapping through Meta-Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ruyu Liu, Junzhe Xu, Zhenhua Wang, Jianhua Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Object Exploration and Mapping (OEM) is a novel embodied AI task that requires an agent to autonomously explore unknown environments, simultaneously detect and localize multiple objects, and construct a semantic object-level map. To solve this challenge, we propose a two-stage framework. First, a deep reinforcement learning-based OEM framework (DRL-OEM) is proposed to leverage auxiliary perception tasks and memory-augmented policy learning to guide efficient exploration. Second, we extend the DRL-OEM to Meta-OEM, where a meta-reinforcement learning framework is employed to enhance adaptation to unseen environments with a novel key-behavior-aware prioritized sampling strategy by reusing high-reward transitions. Extensive experiments conducted on customized AI2Thor scenes across diverse layouts and object configurations demonstrate that Meta-OEM significantly outperforms strong baselines, including DRL-OEM, PEARL, and MQL, achieving up to 2× higher success rates and faster convergence during adaptation. Visual and quantitative results confirm its superior object discovery coverage, pose estimation accuracy, and generalization capability in open-world scenarios.

title: PEXP: A Scalable Parallel Tree-Based Framework for Interpreting Models on Big Data_supp1-3668673.pdf
authors: Chih-Yung Chang
abstract: The proliferation of big data has fueled the success of deep learning; however, its inherent ”black box” nature poses significant challenges for its adoption in safety-critical domains. Existing interpretable machine learning methods offer partial solutions but often struggle with model fidelity, inconsistent explanations, a lack of holistic model understanding, and critically, computational inefficiency, especially when applied to models trained on large-scale datasets. To overcome these hurdles, this paper introduces Parallel Explainer (PEXP), an innovative parallel tree-based interpretation framework designed for scalability and comprehensive understanding. PEXP initiates by generating a localized sample set around a target instance through data distribution-aware perturbations. It then computes similarity scores and employs a kernel function to weight these samples effectively. Leveraging concepts from Bagging and Boosting, PEXP efficiently constructs Parallel Ensemble Trees as its core interpretable model. This model provides feature importance-based explanations and aggregates insights across all samples to achieve a global understanding of the model's behavior on the entire dataset. Experimental results demonstrate PEXP's significant advantages over mainstream interpretable methods in both runtime efficiency and the quality of explanations, particularly crucial for big data analytics. Furthermore, a case study illustrates PEXP's application in enhancing the interpretability of video anomaly detection systems within smart cities, a domain characterized by large volumes of data and offers insights for improving Transformer-based architectures.

title: Detection Method for CDN-Spoofing DDoS Attacks Using ECS-Based LDNS Log Tracing
authors: Taniguchi Kazuya, Noriaki Kamiyama
abstract: Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) are widely deployed to accelerate Internet content delivery, yet their prevalence has also increased the frequency of Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks that exploit CDN infrastructures. A particularly critical threat arises when adversaries spoof the IP addresses of CDN cache servers to bypass firewall defenses and directly overwhelm origin servers (OS). To counter this, we propose a novel two-stage detection framework that combines Z-score-based anomaly detection with log verification at Local Domain Name Systems (LDNS). By leveraging the EDNS Client Subnet (ECS) extension, our method accurately links requests to their corresponding LDNS even under cache-hit conditions, enabling reliable log-based verification. Through theoretical analysis and simulation, we show that the proposed method achieves near-perfect recall with low processing overhead. These results demonstrate that repurposing ECS for security applications provides an effective and practical defense against CDN-bypassing DDoS attacks.

title: Project-Based Learning and Design Thinking Applied to the Improvement of Teaching and Learning in Electrical Engineering
authors: Jalberth F. Araújo, Edivaldo G. dos Santos Júnior, Tarso V. Ferreira, Henrique D. Silva
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: Traditional engineering education, centered on the instructor and lecture-based classes, ensures a solid scientific foundation but does not fully develop the cognitive skills required to meet contemporary challenges and labor market demands. To address the needs of the 21st century, it is essential to rethink teaching methodologies by incorporating digital, social, and cognitive skills, especially in the context of the Fifth Industrial Revolution. This work proposes an active methodology based on Project-Based Learning and Design Thinking, applied to electrical engineering education. The methodology aims to make students the protagonists of their learning process, fostering the development of both technical and interpersonal skills through the resolution of real-world problems. The methodology was implemented in two core courses in the Electrical Engineering curriculum, where student teams developed practical solutions to everyday problems by integrating course content with knowledge from other areas. The results show an average increase of 7.57% in the pass rate for the courses in which the methodology was applied. Moreover, 63.6% of students reported significant changes in their learning approach, contributing to improved academic performance. There was also an average increase of 60.3% in competencies and skills demanded by the current job market. The methodology proved effective and shows potential for application in other programs, promoting student agencies, meaningful learning, and better preparation for the demands of the modern professional world.

title: Low-Profile Wideband Circularly Polarized Slot Antenna for GNSS Applications
authors: Xing Yu Yang, Jing Ming He, Wang Peng Zhang, Xinxin Tian, Liang Hua Ye
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A low-profile wideband circularly polarized (CP) slot antenna is proposed. An annular slot is fed by a microstrip line to generate two CP resonant modes, and two short pins are used to link the slot antenna and the ground plane for generating an additional CP mode and enhancing the impedance matching. Then three metal pillars, two coupling patches and a ring strip are employed to improve the impedance matching and axial ratio for the profile-reduced antenna. By employing this new structure, a low-profile single-fed wideband CP antenna is designed. The presented antenna has a wide impedance bandwidth of 40.5% (1.14 – 1.72 GHz), a broad 3 dB axial-ratio bandwidth of 40.8% (1.13 – 1.71 GHz), and stable gain of 5.5 – 7.5 dBic. Therefore, the proposed design is highly suitable for global navigation satellite systems (GNSS) applications.

title: Predictive Control Over Low-Altitude Wireless Networks: Joint Trajectory Design and Resource Allocation
authors: Haijia Jin, Jun Wu, Weijie Yuan, Ruizhi Ruan, Jiacheng Wang, Dusit Niyato, Dong In Kim, Abbas Jamalipour
abstract: Low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs) have been envisioned as flexible and transformative platforms for enabling delay-sensitive control applications in Internet of Things (IoT) systems. In this work, we investigate the real-time wireless control over LAWNs, where an aerial drone is employed to serve multiple mobile automated guided vehicles (AGVs) via finite blocklength (FBL) transmission. Toward this end, we adopt the model predictive control (MPC) to ensure accurate trajectory tracking, while we analyze the communication reliability using the outage probability. Subsequently, we formulate an optimization problem to jointly determine control policy, transmit power allocation, and drone trajectory by accounting for the maximum travel distance and control input constraints. To address the resultant non-convex optimization problem, we first derive the closed-form expression of the outage probability under FBL transmission. Based on this, we reformulate the original problem as a quadratic programming (QP) problem, followed by developing an alternating optimization (AO) framework. Specifically, we employ the projected gradient descent (PGD) method and the successive convex approximation (SCA) technique to achieve computationally efficient sub-optimal solutions. Furthermore, we thoroughly analyze the convergence and computational complexity of the proposed algorithm. Extensive simulations and AirSim-based experiments are conducted to validate the superiority of our proposed approach compared to the baseline schemes in terms of control performance.

title: Historical Prediction Attention Mechanism based Trajectory Forecasting for Proactive Work Zone Safety in a Digital Twin Environment
authors: Minhaj Uddin Ahmad, Mizanur Rahman, Alican Sevim, David Bodoh, Sakib Khan, Li Zhao, Nathan Huynh, Eren Erman Ozguven
abstract: Proactive safety systems aim to mitigate risks by anticipating future potential conflicts between vehicles and enabling early intervention to prevent work zone related crashes. This study presents an infrastructure-enabled proactive work zone safety warning system that leverages a Digital Twin (DT) environment, integrating real-time multi-sensor data, detailed High-Definition (HD) maps, and a trajectory prediction model based on a historical-prediction attention mechanism. Specifically, the contributions of this study are in two folds: (i) developing an infrastructure-enabled vehicle trajectory prediction framework that leverages the Historical Prediction Network (HPNet) model integrated with Lanelet2 high-definition (HD) maps; and (ii) developing a proactive safety warning generation application that incorporates probabilistic conflict modeling and vehicle bounding-box representations. The development and effectiveness demonstration of the presented trajectory prediction model and proactive warning system is conducted using a co-simulation environment integrating Simulation of Urban MObility (SUMO) and CAR Learning to Act (CARLA) simulators. To evaluate the accuracy of predicted trajectories, we use two standard metrics: Joint Average Displacement Error (ADE) and Joint Final Displacement Error (FDE). Our analyses revealed that the infrastructure-enabled HPNet model demonstrates superior performance on the work zone datasets generated from the cosimulation environment, achieving a minimum Joint FDE of 0.3228 meters and a minimum Joint ADE of 0.1327 meters, lower than the benchmarks on the Argoverse (minJointFDE: 1.0986 m, minJointADE: 0.7612 m) and INTERACTION (minJointFDE: 0.8231 m, minJointADE: 0.2548 m) datasets. In addition, our proactive safety warning generation application demonstrates its ability to issue alerts for potential vehicle conflicts.

title: Data-Driven Analysis and Predictive Control of Descriptor Systems with Applications
authors: Yu Wang, Yuan Zhang, Jun Shang, Yuanqing Xia, Jinhui Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Despite growing interest in data-driven analysis and control of linear systems, descriptor systems (or singular systems)—which are essential for modeling complex engineered systems with algebraic constraints like power and water networks— have received comparatively little attention. This paper develops a comprehensive data-driven framework for analyzing and controlling discrete-time descriptor systems without relying on explicit state-space models. We address fundamental challenges posed by non-causality through the construction of forward and backward data matrices, establishing data-based sufficient conditions for controllability and observability in terms of input-output data, where both R-controllability and C-controllability (R-observability and C-observability) have been considered. Building on them, we then extend Willems’ fundamental lemma to incompletely controllable descriptor systems. These methodological advances Data-Enabled Predictive Control (DeePC) for descriptor systems to achieve output tracking and to maintain performance under incomplete controllability conditions, as demonstrated in two case studies: i) Frequency regulation in an IEEE 9-bus power system with 3 generators, where DeePC maintained the frequency stability of the power system despite deliberate violations of R-controllability, and ii) Pressure head control in an EPANET water network with 3 tanks, 2 reservoirs, and 117 pipes, where output tracking was successfully enforced under algebraic constraints.

title: A Ku-Band 50 W MMIC High-Power Limiter With Optimized Power Division and p-i-n Diode Structure
authors: Yidan Cheng, Jiabin Wang, Jiongjiong Mo, Zhiyu Wang, Wei Chen, Faxin Yu
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, a high-power three-stage monolithic microwave integrated circuit (MMIC) reflective limiter based on GaAs PIN diodes is presented. The optimized power division and PIN diode structure ensure uniform power and thermal distribution, effectively mitigating the formation of hot spots and thereby enhancing the reliability of the limiter. Consequently, the limiter operates stably under high-power continuous-wave (CW) conditions. Within the frequency range of 13.5–17.5 GHz, the limiter can withstand an incident power of up to 50 W (47dBm) in continuous wave mode, while maintaining an output power below 16.3 dBm (42.7 mW). The insertion loss is less than 1.5dB, and the return loss is better than 12.6 dB. Additionally, the proposed limiter features a compact chip size of only 2.2mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The reliability testing results demonstrate that the limiter can operate stably under high-power CW conditions without noticeable performance degradation.

title: Design of a Tank-Optimized 20 GHz VCO with Fourth Harmonic Extraction Achieving 193.8 dBc/Hz Peak FoM in 22 nm FDSOI
authors: S. Koop-Brinkmann, V. Lasserre, A. Dossanov, F. Padovan, A. Mazzanti, V. Issakov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: In this brief, we present design considerations on achieving highest figure of merit (FoM) for a class-B LC VCO in CMOS. Following aspects are addressed: a) systematic analysis of choosing the optimal fundamental oscillation frequency fO for achieving simultaneously low phase noise (PN) and high power efficiency, i.e., highest FoM. We show that the optimal fO is directly related to the frequency tuning range (FTR). E.g., in the given technology, an FTR of 3% results in an optimal operating range of 10 GHz to 20 GHz. A wider FTR narrows down the operation range for optimal PN and FOM; and b) we present an LC-tank step-by-step dimensioning procedure. The proposed approach is based on iterative electromagnetic (EM)-simulations of the resonant circuit components and optimization with respect to technology-independent design degrees of freedom. The terms 10 log10(L/(CRT)) and 20 log10(RT √C/L) derived from analytical expressions for PN and FoM are optimized over frequency for various Cmax/Cmin ratios. We use these considerations to design a multi-harmonic class-B LC-VCO in 22nm FDSOI CMOS, which offers two outputs - one at the fundamental (H1) and another at the fourth harmonic (H4), extracted directly from the core. The VCO achieves a highly competitive FoM at 1MHz offset with 0.4V supply of 193.8 dBc/Hz and 188.2 dBc/Hz at 20 GHz and 80 GHz, respectively. We demonstrate that class-B VCO can achieve PN and FoM outperforming class-C or class-F. It is achieved only by means of the proposed systematic LC-tank design, without any additional circuitry, such as waveform shaping, core coupling etc. Thus, core area is only 0.05mm2.

title: When Annotators Disagree: A Principled Approach to Learning with Noisy Labels
authors: Maria Sofia Bucarelli, Antonio Purificato, Andrea Bacciu, Lucas Cassano, Federico Siciliano, Anil Nelakanti, Amin Mantrach, Fabrizio Silvestri
abstract: In practical settings, classification datasets are often labeled by humans, leading to potential noise due to varying annotations from different individuals. The exact noise distribution impacting these labels is typically unknown; however, one quantity we can measure and attempt to exploit is inter-rater agreement. Building on this, our work makes key contributions: we (i) demonstrate how inter-annotator statistics can be used to estimate the label noise distribution; (ii) propose methods that leverage these estimates to train models on noisy data; and (iii) derive generalization bounds within the empirical risk minimization framework that depend on the estimated noise characteristics. Finally, we present experiments that support our findings. Our code can be found at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/amazon-science/learning under noisy labels</uri>.

title: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
authors: N/A

title: Physics-Constrained Deep Learning for Effective Atomic Number and Density Calculation of Biological Tissues in Photon-Counting Spectral CT
authors: Chengmin Wang, Tong Jia, Xiaomei Zhang, Pengfei Chen, Meng Yuan, Zhe Wang, Cunfeng Wei, Long Wei
abstract: Photon-Counting Detector Computed Tomography (PCD-CT) boasts excellent spectral utilization capability. Combined with material decomposition methods, it enables the calculation of the effective atomic number (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and density (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rho$</tex-math></inline-formula>) of scanned materials. Traditional material decomposition methods derive <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rho$</tex-math></inline-formula> from physical models using either the basis material model or the dual-effect model. However, these methods generally fail to meet high-precision decomposition requirements due to model approximations and suffer from the limitation of severe noise amplification in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> images. This study proposes a physics-constrained deep learning network that achieves high-precision joint estimation of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rho$</tex-math></inline-formula> by dynamically modeling nonlinear X-ray interactions. A Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is employed to construct dynamic compensation functions dependent on energy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for the photoelectric effect exponent and Compton scattering model in the X-ray interaction model. The decomposition network adopts Swin-Unet as its backbone and utilizes a hybrid loss function, which consists of L1 loss and SSIM loss for <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rho$</tex-math></inline-formula>, as well as a physics-informed loss derived from the L1 loss between the predicted <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rho$</tex-math></inline-formula> and the monoenergetic linear attenuation coefficient images generated by the optimized X-ray model. This design allows the network to simultaneously learn data-driven features and physical principles. Comparative experiments were conducted on a PCD-CT system between the proposed method and four methods (Lan, U-Net, Butterfly-net, and Swin-Unet without physical constraints). The results demonstrate that: for standard materials, the proposed method achieves a Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) below 5% for both <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rho$</tex-math></inline-formula> decomposition, with superior Noise Power Spectrum (NPS) performance; for biological samples including freshwater crayfish and mouse, the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> images generated by the proposed method exhibit higher Multi-Exposure Fusion Structural Similarity Index (MEF-SSIM), reaching 0.9559 for crayfish and 0.8950 for mouse. The method also demonstrates superior detail recovery capability in the restoration of the speckled tissue structure of crayfish and the tissue regions of mouse. By incorporating constraints from monoenergetic images generated based on the dynamic X-ray interaction model, the network effectively learns the nonlinear decomposition process of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{eff}$</tex-math></inline-formula> within a data-driven framework. The proposed method improves decomposition accuracy while reducing decomposition noise and enhancing the quality of decomposed images.

title: Weak-Mamba-UNet: Visual Mamba Makes CNN and ViT Work Better for Scribble-based Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Ziyang Wang, Tianli Tao, Yiyuan Ge, Zhihao Chen, Tianxiang Chen, Tianxiang Chen, Zi Ye, Yongxiang Lei
abstract: Medical image segmentation is increasingly reliant on deep learning techniques, yet the promising performance often come with high annotation costs. This paper introduces Weak-Mamba-UNet, an innovative weakly-supervised learning (WSL) framework that leverages the capabilities of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Vision Transformer (ViT), and the cutting-edge Visual Mamba (VMamba) architecture for medical image segmentation, especially when dealing with scribble-based annotations. The proposed WSL strategy incorporates three distinct architecture but same symmetrical encoder-decoder networks: a CNN-based U-Net for detailed local feature extraction, a Swin Transformer-based Swin-UNet for comprehensive global context understanding, and a VMamba-based Mamba-UNet for efficient long-range dependency modeling. The key concept of this framework is a collaborative and cross-supervisory mechanism that employs pseudo labels to facilitate iterative learning and refinement across the networks. The effectiveness of Weak-Mamba-UNet is validated on two publicly available datasets with processed scribble annotations, where it surpasses the performance of a similar WSL framework utilizing only U-Net or Swin-UNet, as well as other baseline methods. This paper highlights the potential of Mamba for medical image segmentation in scenarios with sparse or imprecise annotations. The source code, dataset, and all baseline methods are made publicly accessible <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ziyangwang007/Mamba-UNet</uri>.

title: Telemetric Occlusion and Flow Detection in Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt
authors: Farzad Ahmadi, Caleb E. Stewart
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: This paper introduces an implantable sensor system with telemetric capabilities, designed to detect and communicate malfunctions in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) shunts. Using Ansys Electronics Desktop, the system was simulated on a commercially available ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt for detecting catheter occlusions. The proposed system utilizes a flexible C-shaped capacitive sensor wrapped around the catheter to detect the presence and absence of CSF by monitoring capacitance variations. These changes alter the resonance frequency of the system, which can be measured using a wireless reader coil located outside the body. The results demonstrated the sensor's sensitivity to CSF variations, with measurable shifts in resonance frequencies under different conditions. This approach offers a telemetric solution for real-time monitoring of CSF shunt function.

title: Comparative Analysis of Time-Frequency Representations for Pediatric Respiratory Sound Classification Using Deep Learning
authors: Ghazaleh Shiri, Hanieh Bahrami, Alireza Fallahi
publish: 2025 32nd National and 10th International Iranian Conference on Biomedical Engineering (ICBME)
abstract: Respiratory sound classification has emerged as a promising non-invasive and scalable tool for the early detection of respiratory disorders. While most previous studies have relied on a single feature extraction method-such as Melfrequency cepstral coefficients (MFCC), Mel-spectrograms, or Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)-this study provides a comprehensive comparative analysis of these three approaches, evaluated individually, in pairwise combinations, and in a combined three-method configuration. Experiments are conducted on the SPRSound dataset, a pediatric respiratory sound database comprising 6,656 annotated events from seven unbalanced classes. Three architectures are assessed under identical preprocessing and augmentation strategies: a custom convolutional neural network (CNN), and two pre-trained models (VGG16 and InceptionV3) fine-tuned via transfer learning. Results show that STFT consistently delivers the highest performance for CNN and VGG16 models, while MFCC achieves the best accuracy with InceptionV3. Specifically, VGG16 with STFT attained 93.43% accuracy (score: 0.9545), whereas InceptionV3 with MFCC achieved the top performance within its architecture. These findings highlight the importance of aligning feature extraction techniques with model architecture and provide a systematic benchmark for SPRSound-based respiratory sound classification.

title: A 19dBm 110–142 GHz Power Amplifier Employing an Enhanced-Power Cascode and 4-Way Dual-Coupled Combiner in 65-nm CMOS
authors: Md Hedayatullah Maktoomi, Xuyang Liu, Hamidreza Aghasi
publish: 2026 IEEE Topical Conference on RF/Microwave Power Amplifiers for Radio and Wireless Applications (PAWR)
abstract: This paper presents a high-output broadband power amplifier (PA) for the D-band (110–170 GHz) frequency range. To enhance the output power from the PA stage, a current-reuse cascode configuration based on a novel device-centric power maximization is proposed. To facilitate a larger current in the common-gate transistor, an additional third transistor is added to the cascode stage, which enables simultaneous parasitic neutralization, impedance matching, and gain enhancement. To further enhance the output power, a novel slotline-based dual-coupled 4-way differential combiner is proposed. The PA is fabricated in 65-nm CMOS process. The measured peak saturated output power is 19 dBm, and the peak small signal gain is 15.3 dB with 32.8 GHz of bandwidth. Various amplitude modulations are evaluated with this PA with an average output power <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 7.5$</tex> dBm and an error vector magnitude (EVM) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq-21$</tex> dB.

title: Improved Alzheimer’s Disease Classification from MRI Scans Using Deep Learning
authors: Wildan Arif Maulana, Zainul Abidin, Rahmadwati
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: The global rise in life expectancy has increased the elderly population, leading to a higher prevalence of Alzheimer’s disease. Early detection is crucial, as timely intervention can slow disease progression and improve patients’ quality of life. Deep learning provides an effective solution for automating brain MRI classification by identifying complex patterns within medical images. This study investigates the implementation of the VGG-16 deep learning architecture in conjunction with two data augmentation techniques: Albumentations and CutMix for the classification of Alzheimer’s disease using MRI images. The classification task encompasses four categories: Mild Demented, Moderate Demented, Very Mild Demented, and Non-Demented. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score as assessment metrics. The VGG-16 model achieved an accuracy of $87.89 \%$ when employing a combination of Albumentations and CutMix, compared to $78.12 \%$ with CutMix as a single technique, $73.44 \%$ with Albumentations as a single technique, and $35.16 \%$ without augmentation. These results demonstrate that combining Albumentations and CutMix enhances data diversity and model generalization, thereby improving classification accuracy.

title: Analysis of Indonesian Public Sentiment Towards Government Free Meal Program Using Artificial Intelligence
authors: Dwi Kurniawan Aliputra, Ervan Fernando Wijaya, Yosua Samuelpin, Yulia Ery Kurniawati
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: The Free Nutritious Meal Program implemented by the Indonesian government is one of the strategic steps that aims to reduce malnutrition and increase awareness of the importance of nutrition. However, as with any government policy, the role and perception of the public towards the program can affect whether the program can be successfully implemented. Now, with the increasing popularity of social media among the public for sharing opinions, sentiment analysis has proven its importance. This study aims to compare three machine learning algorithms, namely Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest Tree (RFT), and Multinomial Naive Bayes (MNB), in analyzing public sentiment towards the free nutritious meal program in Indonesia. Using data gathered from 40 YouTube videos and using YouTube Data API v3, a total of 19,723 comments were gathered. The preprocessing processes for this research are separated into 5 steps, which include; Cleaning, Normalization, Tokenization, Stopword Removal, and Data Stemming. After preprocessing, the data sample will need to go through feature extraction using TF-IDF weighting scheme. In addition, this study also applied the SMOTE algorithm for data imbalance handling and compared the result with the dataset without imbalance handling. The results show that SVM performs best when not using SMOTE, with an accuracy of $\mathbf{8 4 . 1 4 \%}$ and an F1-Score of $\mathbf{8 2 . 6 2 \%}$. Followed by RFT for overall quality with its evaluation score of $\mathbf{8 0 . 3 2 \%}$ accuracy. Lastly, the MNB algorithm got a significant increase in performance after implementing SMOTE with an accuracy of $\mathbf{8 0 . 2 3 \%}$ and an F1-Score of 77.71%.

title: Singular Vector Decomposition In Reconstructing Data Electricity Consumption with Clustering Analysis
authors: Dine Tiara Kusuma, Gusti Ayu Aulia Anandha Jyothi, Yozika Arvio, Iriansyah Bm Sangadji
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Electricity is the primary energy source most widely used in various business sectors. Electricity consumption data plays a crucial role for corporate clients in monitoring operational performance, optimizing resource utilization, and identifying energy efficiency potential. However, this data often has very large dimensions, so an effective dimensionality reduction technique is needed to support further analysis. This study aims to evaluate the effectiveness of the Singular Vector Decomposition (SVD) algorithm in reducing the dimensionality of business customers’ electricity consumption data and integrating it into the clustering process. The K-means algorithm was used as the clustering method, while validation was performed using the Silhouette Score metric. The results showed that the reduced data from $\mathbf{7 2 0} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{1 2}$ to $\mathbf{7 2 0} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{4}$ variables resulted in a Silhouette Score of 0.58397. This finding indicates that the application of the SVD algorithm can maintain relevant information structures and support the formation of quality clusters, making it an effective approach in large-scale energy consumption data analysis.

title: Adversarial Model Poisoning in CNNs for Brain Tumor Detection: Risks to Smart Healthcare Integrity
authors: Prashant Singh, Kusum Lata, Sandeep Saini
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: The broad employment of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in radiology images has remarkably enhanced automated radiologists; yet, their security flaws have long been a great concern. This work discusses the impact of a trojaned or poisoned pooling layer's presence on CNN-based brain tumor classifier from MR images. Three state-of-the-art CNN structures, DenseNet121, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3, were evaluated under poisoning and clean conditions using 5-fold cross-validation for robust performance estimation. The poisoned models showed drastic loss of classification accuracy by up to 49 % when the trojan is enabled. We also created a Flaskbased front end for mimicry of real-world deployment where normal functioning takes place under regular input while the pre-designed input patterns invoke the trojaned behavior. We also evaluated the inference performance and utilized the resources running the models across several hardware devices such as the Xilinx Kria KV260, Raspberry Pi 5, and the regular CPU, thereby showing the real-world impact of running such models in resource-limited environments. This work highlights the prime security concerns in the deployment of AI models for healthcare diagnostic purposes and the importance of developing sound detection and disabling mechanisms for model poisoning attacks in safety-oriented applications.

title: Optimization of Ensemble Deep Learning Models for Freshwater Fish Disease Classification Using Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Mochammad Rafli Samsul Maarif, Guruh Fajar Shidik, Purwanto
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Disease outbreaks in aquaculture pose serious threats to food security and economic stability, particularly in freshwater fish farming. Traditional diagnostic methods are time-consuming and lack efficiency, highlighting the need for intelligent, automated solutions. This study presents an optimized ensemble deep learning approach for classifying freshwater fish diseases, enhanced using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). The proposed model integrates four pre-trained architectures—ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetB3—whose weights are dynamically adjusted using PSO based on performance metrics. To improve generalization and address dataset imbalance, a comprehensive data augmentation pipeline was applied to expand a small initial dataset into a balanced set of 2,100 images across seven disease classes. Explainable AI techniques, such as Grad-CAM, were employed to enhance interpretability. Experimental results show that the optimized ensemble achieved $98 \%$ accuracy and balanced F1-scores, outperforming both single models and unoptimized ensembles. The results were statistically validated using paired t-tests. This approach offers a robust, interpretable, and scalable solution for automated fish disease diagnosis, contributing to smarter and more sustainable aquaculture practices.

title: Precision Image Segmentation-Enhanced CNN for Apple Leaf Disease Detection
authors: Anusha Sirugumalle, Pramoda Patro
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: This paper proposes a Precision Image Segmentation-enhanced CNN to detect apple diseases on the leaves. Combining the U-Net-based methodology of image segmentation to produce improved, upgraded four-channel images (RGB + mask), the model emphasizes the location of the disease. This improvement makes the CNN perform at an extended accuracy of 91.74% which is high. This paper shows the efficiency of integrating segmentation with a deep learning approach to accurate and specific classification of plant diseases in full recognition of five categories of Alternaria leaf spot, Brown spot, Gray spot, Healthy leaf, and Rust.

title: Hybrid Sampling with Mixed Shape and Seed Representations for Point Cloud Recovery
authors: Kaiyue Zhou, Zhenyu Wang, Ya-Li Li, Shengjin Wang
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: Point cloud recovery is essential for data processing in remote anonymous systems, aiming to sample a coarse version of original points and then restore it for downstreams like understanding or reconstruction. Non-differentiable sampling methods (FPS) prohibit gradients from flowing back during optimization, and existing differentiable methods simply construct point-wise features without considering the shape information. Here, we propose Hybrid Shape-Seed Sampling that combines shape and seed representations to refine non-differentiable sampled points in a differentiable manner, enhancing geometric continuity and shape coverage. We propose a seed mixer to mix the shape code and point-wise features via a k-nearest-neighbors (kNN) based transformer, enabling the construction of point-shape correspondences. The sampled points by seed mixer serve as critical seeds for local or remote processing. Our method achieves superior recovery results on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrated through comparative analyses against state-of-the-art models by at least 13%.

title: MDP-Based Distributed Relay Selection Policy for Cooperative Networks with Energy Harvesting
authors: Kangyi Weng, Wei Li, Wenzheng Yu, Baoxi Sun
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: To address the low efficiency and high complexity of transmission policy optimization algorithms in large-scale energy harvesting (EH) multi-relay cooperative networks, a Markov decision process (MDP)-based distributed optimization method is proposed for joint relay selection and power allocation. First, an MDP model is established of the EH multi-relay cooperative networks. To reduce energy consumption and system complexity, only one EH relay is selected to forward the information of source node within a transmission period, thereby avoiding inter-relay interference. Then, a distributed framework of EH communication networks is designed, which exploits the value iteration to distributedly compute the state-value function of each relay. Based on the distributed value iterations, the relay selection and power allocation policy is derived to optimize the outage probability of information transmission. Moreover, the theoretical analysis shows that the transmission policy obtained by the proposed method converges to the optimal policy under high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Finally, the simulation experiments are conducted to analyze the impact of network parameters on outage probability performance. The simulation results show that, compared with DQN, the proposed method can obtains at least 2 dB of SNR gains for dual-relay when the outage probability is below 0.01. As the number of relays increases, the proposed method can obtains higher SNR gains under the same outage probability, and obtains at least 5 dB of SNR gains when the outage probability is less than 0.001. Besides, the proposed method has fast convergence and low complexity, and it can provide high applicability in practice.

title: Hybrid Beam Steerable & Low RCS UWB Vivaldi Antenna Array for Ground Surveillance Radar Systems
authors: M L S N S Lakshmi, Manoj Kumar Goel, Mekala Naga Vineela, KVV RajKumar, Kosaraju Lalithendra, Gogunta Bhanu
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Antennas with ultra-wideband (UWB) capabilities are essential components for radar and surveillance systems of the future, particularly for defense and security applications. Vivaldi antennas, in particular, have established themselves as a strong contender for the combination of broadband impedance bandwidth, and easy to manufacture end-fire radiation patterns when constructed as an array. This article attempts to provide a structured review of the recent advancements of Vivaldi antenna designs and reduction of RCS, broken down into C/X band related techniques. By reviewing the strengths, weaknesses, and trade-offs of all the various methods, our review found the potential for compact, fabricable antennas based on the existing UWB designs tailored for the C and X bands. The review is expected to help lead to low-RCS wideband antenna arrays that address the challenges of performance and low-observation in real-world radar systems.

title: Analysis and Simulation of DC-DC Converters for Solar Microgrids
authors: Aaditi Rammohan, Sunil Thomas
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: With the global shift toward sustainable energy, microgrids powered by renewable sources such as solar photovoltaic (PV) systems have gained significant attention. These systems offer flexibility, reliability, and scalability, particularly in remote and distributed energy environments. One of the key challenges in such microgrids is managing the variable DC output from solar panels and ensuring stable voltage levels for effective energy storage and load supply. To address this, DC-DC converters are essential for regulating power flow across the system. This work aims to design and simulate using MATLAB/Simulink a DC microgrid comprising a solar PV panel with a Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) mechanism, a lithium-ion battery-based Energy Storage System (ESS), and a DC load. A buck-boost converter is employed to manage voltage regulation between the ESS and the DC grid under various charging and discharging conditions. Additionally, a boost converter with a Proportional-Integral (PI) controller is implemented to control voltage between the PV panel and the DC grid. The study also analyzes the converter and system performance under varying irradiance conditions thereby evaluating the effectiveness and adaptability of the design for real-time microgrid applications.

title: Comparison of Linear Regression and Long Short-Term Memory in Predicting the Electricity Demand of PLN in Jambi City
authors: Herman Bedi Agtriadi, Abdurrasyid, Lidya Meltiana, Dine Tiara Kusuma, Rima Rizqi Wijayanti
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
abstract: Electrical energy is a fundamental need that supports economic activities and daily life. PT PLN (Persero), as the national energy provider, has the responsibility to ensure adequate and reliable electricity supply, particularly in urban areas such as Jambi City. As electricity demand increases, the ability to accurately predict energy consumption becomes crucial to avoid supply-demand imbalances. This study aims to compare the performance of two prediction models Linear Regression and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) in forecasting the electricity demand of R1 1300 VA customers. Using historical data from 2013 to 2023 obtained from PT PLN UP3 Jambi, the dataset was split into $80 \%$ for training and $20 \%$ for testing.
