title: Accurate Threshold Voltage Prediction Control for Charge-Controlled LLC Converters in Two-Stage Single-Phase Rectifiers
authors: Wenzhe Chen, Hongqi Ding, Yichao Sun, Cheng Jin, Pengyu Wang, Kangli Liu, Jianfeng Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This paper proposes an accurate threshold voltage prediction control (ATVPC) strategy for charge-controlled LLC converters in two-stage single-phase rectifiers, addressing the challenges of second-harmonic output voltage ripple and limited dynamic response. The proposed method solves precise control signal instead of relying on a slow PI controller for error compensation, which are commonly required in existing state-plane feedforward control schemes. Consequently, the proposed strategy overcomes the bandwidth limitation imposed by slow linear compensators. Simulation and experimental results verify its dynamic enhancement and robust ripple rejection capability.

title: High-Gain Dual-Polarized Anisotropic Flat Luneburg Lens Antenna with Asymmetric Apertures
authors: Yang Gao, Zhong-Xun Liu, Haoyu Lei, Yue-Nian Chen, Yongtao Jia, Qiao Sun, Lei Zhu, Ying Liu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A dual-polarized anisotropic flat Luneburg lens (DAFLL) antenna is proposed to achieve high-gain performance with asymmetric apertures. It enables independent design of orthogonal polarizations over asymmetric apertures using anisotropic materials, overcoming the limitations of conventional isotropic flat Luneburg lenses (LLs). The proposed asymmetric flat LL is realized through multi-dimensional transformation mapping of a spherical Luneburg lens (sLL). By retaining and adjusting only the dominant diagonal components (ϵx, ϵy) of the permittivity tensor, the lens effectively controls x- and y-polarizations independently, thus ensuring high gain for both polarizations. Simulated results demonstrate that the proposed DAFLL antenna achieves comparable dual-polarized high gain to the ideal sLL antenna with the identical aperture area. A prototype was fabricated and measured using 18-layer stacked substrates. The measured results show good agreement with simulations, confirming the validity of the proposed work.

title: Critical Success Factors of Agile Software Development Projects in Hybrid Settings: A Mixed-method Approach
authors: Saagarika Gosu, Akshy Sripad Raghavendra Prasad, Xiaofeng Wang
abstract: Context: The rapid shift to hybrid work challenges Agile software development teams, because agile methodologies were originally designed for co-located environments. It is unclear whether the critical success factors (CSFs) identified in the existing agile literature are still relevant and how their interdependencies manifest in hybrid working environments. Objectives: This study re-examines CSFs for agile projects in hybrid settings and models their hierarchical interdependencies. Methods: We used a mixed-method approach, first surveying industry practitioners to re-examine 36 established CSFs. Subsequently, Interpretive Structural Modeling (ISM) was used with an expert panel to construct a hierarchical model of the interdependencies of these factors. The model was then analyzed using MICMAC to classify the factors by importance. Results: The survey confirmed the relevance of the 36 traditional CSFs, with no new factors suggested for the hybrid settings. The ISM analysis produced a seventeen-level hierarchy, showing that success is built progressively from foundational project and technical factors to team and organizational factors. MICMAC analysis identified a small set of foundational ’driver’ factors critical for agile success in hybrid settings. Conclusions: The findings from this study demonstrate that agile success in hybrid settings is an interconnected system rather than a simple checklist. By establishing a hierarchical structure, this research highlights the necessity of prioritizing foundational ’driver’ factors, which provide the leverage required for systemic improvement rather than focusing on outcome-level factors in isolation. Our framework supports organizations in making more informed, data-driven decisions when adapting agile methodologies to modern hybrid workplaces.

title: Energy-Efficient Velocity Control with State Augmentation for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Yuyang Xia, Shuncheng Liu, Xu Chen, Yan Zhao, Han Su, Xiaofang Zhou, Kai Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Velocity control in autonomous driving is an emerging technology that has achieved rapid progress over the last decade. However, existing velocity control studies ignore cascading disturbances in multi-lane scenarios and usually ignore the negative impact caused by harsh velocity decisions. To address these issues, we propose a reinforcement learning-based framework, called RISE (cont<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>ol veloc<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">I</u>ty for autonomou<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">S</u> v<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">E</u>hicle) to make velocity decisions for an autonomous vehicle in multi-lane traffic scenarios. To detect latent disturbances in the traffic flow, we propose a novel state encoder to learn the spatio-temporal correlation between different vehicles based on a well-designed impact graph. Afterward, we introduce an actor-critic paradigm to make velocity decisions with the aid of a hybrid reward function considering four optimization objectives: safety, efficiency, comfort, and impact. In particular, the impact term can penalize the harsh decisions of the autonomous vehicle, thus encouraging it to reduce the negative impacts on traffic flow. Further, we propose an improved RISE (RISE++) framework that incorporates a motion prediction model to augment state features for reasonable decisions, a modification of the reward function for energy efficiency, and a multi-worker paradigm for training efficiency. Extensive experiments offer evidence that the proposed framework can advance the state of the art in terms of effectiveness and efficiency.

title: High-Degree-of-Freedom Range-Doppler Modulation via Optimized Pseudo-Random Coding Metasurface
authors: Liwei Chen, Junjie Wang, Yan Ma, Jiong Wu, Dejun Feng, Xiaobin Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Time-coding metasurfaces (TCM) have emerged as a promising approach for radar target feature modulation (RTFM), attracting significant attention in recent years. However, existing methods often encounter a trade-off between system complexity and modulation flexibility, with limited multidimensional control. Based on the linear discrete harmonic generation characteristics of periodic modulation TCMs, this paper establishes a grid distribution model of false targets in the range-Doppler (RD) domain. A phase-modulated reflector (PMR) system is further proposed to achieve high-degree-of-freedom joint modulation of target range and Doppler features, while maintaining system complexity comparable to that of 1-bit TCMs. The core of the system lies in periodic pseudo-random coding, which supports flexible RD feature modulation. By integrating a genetic algorithm with a suitable fitness function, the system enables customized harmonic generation, thereby redistributing real target energy over the RD grid to synthesize false target peaks at specified locations. Leveraging the multi-reflection and retro-reflection properties of corner reflectors, the system can be configured in either 1-bit or 2-bit phase modulation mode to generate symmetrically or asymmetrically distributed false targets in the RD domain, while preserving low system complexity. The proposed RTFM method is validated through both simulation and practical radar experiments, demonstrating its potential for diverse application scenarios.

title: Constructions of Combinatorial Neural Codes with Asymmetric Discrepancy
authors: Zhonghua Sun, Xinxin Song, Yang Li
abstract: The recent work by Cotardo and Ravagnani (IEEE Trans. Inf. Theory, vol. 68, no. 5, pp. 2941-2950, May 2022) introduced a class of binary codes endowed with asymmetric discrepancy, which are referred to as Combinatorial Neural codes (CN codes), and are motivated by theoretical neuroscience. The applications in binary asymmetric memoryless channel and neuroscience have spurred interest in constructing binary codes and analyzing the error-correction capabilities under asymmetric discrepancy. In this paper, we first characterize equidistant CN codes and propose several constructions of (equidistant) CN codes based on the Hadamard codes and punctured Hadamard codes. For a binary linear code C ⊆ GF(2)n, we then analyze the minimum asymmetric discrepancy of the nonzero subset C\{0}, the coset u+C (u ∈ GF(2)n), and the union C ∪(1+C), where 0 denotes the all-zero vector and 1 the all-one vector. Based on these results, we completely determine the exact parameters for several classes of CN codes by combining simplex codes or projective 2-weight codes.

title:
Simulated VO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Neuron with Embedded HfO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Memristive Synapse
authors: Juan Núñez, Rafaella Fiorelli
abstract: We present a neuromorphic circuit cell that integrates a volatile VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> neuron with a non-volatile HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> memristive synapse, enabling autonomous local plasticity in a compact, CMOS-compatible building block. Unlike conventional neuromorphic implementations where neurons and synapses are separated and coordinated by peripheral circuitry, the HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> memristor is embedded directly in the leak path of a VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> integrate-and-fire neuron. As a result, the neuron’s spike dynamics directly modulate the synaptic conductance, which in turn reshapes neuronal excitability. Using experimentally calibrated Verilog-A models and electrical simulations, we demonstrate stable firing-rate adaptation, long-term conductance evolution driven by neuronal activity, and compatibility with standard 1T1R programming schemes. This unified neuron–synapse cell forms a device–circuit primitive where volatile and non-volatile dynamics are co-designed at the cell level, enabling scalable local plasticity without peripheral control circuitry.

title: Automatic Formation Generation based on Scene Awareness for Guided Group Navigation in VR
authors: Jian Wu, Lili Wang, Zhikai Wen, Yanzhou Chen, Qianwen Wang, Bin Hu, Xuehuai Shi, Xiaolong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Group navigation is a virtual reality (VR) technology designed to replace personal navigation, enhancing efficiency and aiding guided tours in path planning for other users. Group navigation techniques require users to have a clear understanding of navigation and travel modalities. Tour guides must ensure that users do not intersect with the environment or other users' models and must provide reasonable tour formations. To address these needs, we propose a scene-aware automatic formation generation method for fast and easy-to-use guided tours. First, we generate a limited number of candidate points for the exhibition and initialize the visiting group formation. Next, we optimize the formation to maximize view quality using our proposed viewpoint observation score. Finally, we match the visitors to the optimized formation to ensure a minimal view deflection angle. Moreover, we conducted a user study to evaluate the performance of our approach. Compared to the current method, our approach significantly increased navigation efficiency and view quality. Additionally, it substantially decreased the task load and improved the system usability for both tour guides and visitors.

title: 3D NMC-HBM: Design of 3D Near Memory Computing Architecture in High Bandwidth Memory considering Signal/Power Integrity and Computing Performance
authors: Jiwon Yoon, Seongguk Kim, Keeyoung Son, Seonguk Choi, Junghyun Lee, Haeyeon Kim, Taein Shin, Joonsang Park, Haeseok Suh, Hyunjun An, Youngwoo Kim, Joungho Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: In this paper, we propose a design framework for a 3D near-memory computing architecture in high bandwidth memory (3D NMC-HBM) considering signal/power integrity (SI/PI) and computing performance. The design framework enables the physical co-design of the 3D interconnect and power distribution network (PDN) based on an electrical performance evaluation, while integrating the extracted interconnect-level parameters into a cycle-accurate simulator for a system-level performance evaluation. Each component of the 3D NMC-HBM architecture including the NMC processor die, interconnect channels, and a hierarchical 3D PDN is modeled considering the physical dimensions of the conventional graphics processing unit (GPU)-HBM module. The electrical performance of the 3D NMC-HBM architecture is analyzed using a detailed methodology considering SI for providing the extended bandwidth, and PI to ensure stable power delivery for high performance operation of the NMC processing units. Furthermore, the efficacy of the proposed framework is verified through a system-level evaluation. Integrated with a cycle-accurate GPU simulator, the computing performance and power consumption of the 3D NMC-HBM architecture is evaluated, considering the effects of reduced interconnect length, bandwidth extension, and higher core utilization. The results show that the 3D NMC-HBM architecture achieves higher computing performance and lower power consumption when computing memory-bound workloads, compared to the GPU-HBM.

title: Performance Analysis of Reflecting RIS-Assisted VLCP System: From Asymptotic Capacity to Cramer-Rao Bound
authors: Qi Wu, Jian Zhang, Ya-qi Chen, Yan-yu Zhang, Gang Xin, Er-feng Zhang, Dun Li
abstract: The reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted integrated visible light communication and positioning (VLCP) system has emerged as a promising technology, capable of mitigating the inherent vulnerability to line-of-sight (LoS) blockage while enabling simultaneous communication, positioning, and illumination. Among existing technologies, three main types of reflecting optical RIS have been proposed to create non-negligible non-line-of-sight (NLoS) links. This paper investigates the reflecting RIS-assisted VLCP system by optimizing RIS parameters to balance communication and positioning performance. Specifically, we adopt the asymptotic capacity as the communication metric and derive the Cramer-Rao Bound (CRB) as the positioning metric. For comparison, we consider three RIS architectures: the meta-surface-based RIS (MS-RIS), the mirror-array-based RIS (MA-RIS), and the liquid crystal-based digital RIS (LC-DRIS). For each, we formulate a weighted optimization problem that jointly maximizes the asymptotic capacity and minimizes the CRB through the optimization of RIS parameters. To solve the resulting non-convex problems, we develop efficient algorithms with reduced computational complexity. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed optimization methods against benchmarks, and a comparative analysis highlights the respective strengths and limitations of the three optical RIS architectures.

title: A Novel Eigen-Volume-based Co-Activation Pattern Framework for Dynamic Functional Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis
authors: Fatemeh Valipour, Maryam Mohebbi, Mani Garousi, Maryam S. Mirian, Anthony L. Traboulsee, Shannon Kolind, Martin J. McKeown
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Imaging biomarkers are essential for monitoring multiple sclerosis (MS), wand resting-state functional MRI (rs-fMRI) offers functional insights that complement structural imaging. This study investigates whether a novel co-activation pattern (CAP) approach for dynamic rs-fMRI can function as a dual-purpose biomarker in MS, aiding diagnosis and tracking disease severity. RS-fMRI scans from 25 relapsing-remitting MS patients and 41 healthy controls (HCs) were analyzed using a novel CAP-based approach. CAPs derived from individual time frames to capture dynamic brain activity patterns incorporated a bivariate similarity assessment, eigen volume-based dimensionality reduction, and consensus clustering. We evaluated the framework in two analyses: (1) a diagnostic evaluation, using dynamic CAP features—dwell time, persistence, and transition probabilities—for group comparisons and classification; and (2) a severity-prediction analysis, relating these CAP-derived measures to clinical disability (EDSS) in MS using LASSO regression. Method performance was benchmarked against standard CAP and sliding-window (SW) approaches. It revealed significant differences in brain activity between MS and HCs, within the default mode, sensorimotor, and language networks (p < 0.05), highlighting alterations relevant to motor, cognitive, and sensory functions affected in MS. Transition probabilities showed strong correlations with EDSS (r > 0.75) and yielded better classification performance than standard CAP and SW approaches in classifying MS from HCs. These results suggest that dynamic brain activity patterns are altered in MS and linked to clinical disability. The proposed CAP provided improved performance in distinguishing MS patients, offering enhanced clinical monitoring. Transition probabilities emerged as a potential biomarker for tracking MS progression, with network shifts reflecting disease severity. As MS advances, increased transitions toward sensory, motor, and executive networks suggest compensatory recruitment. Conversely, reduced transitions from default mode and salience networks to sensorimotor and frontoparietal systems were associated with greater disability and diminished adaptive reorganization.

title: Multi-expert Trajectory Prediction for Highway weaving sections Using Conflict Potential Energy and GAN
authors: Lei Wang, Heng Zhang, Xiuying Wang, Zhiwei Guan, Mei Xiao, Jian Liu, Mingjiang Wei, Yong Pan
abstract: To address the challenge of lane-changing prediction for autonomous vehicles (AVs) within highway weaving sections characterized by dynamic interactions in human-machine mixed traffic flows, this paper proposes a multi-expert collaborative prediction model based on the Conflict Potential Field and Generative Adversarial Networks, termed G-MoE-WGAN. This model quantifies the dynamic game intensity among interacting vehicles via a Conflict Potential Field (CPF). The proposed model constructs a Mixture of Experts (MoE) system to facilitate intention decision-making and trajectory generation, employing adversarial training to optimize the physical plausibility and interaction adaptability of the prediction results. Furthermore, a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) is introduced for the reconstruction and smoothing of raw naturalistic driving trajectories to validate the model. Experimental results demonstrate that the G-MoE-WGAN achieves a lane-changing intention classification accuracy of 94.35%, representing an improvement of 3.89% to 16.55% compared to baseline models. Within a 3-second prediction horizon, the Final Displacement Error (FDE) and Average Displacement Error (ADE) of the proposed model are significantly reduced by 8.61%–40.55% and 3.21%–40.27%, respectively. In the 5-second long-term prediction, benefiting from the dynamic expert fusion mechanism, the ADE and FDE metrics exhibit a reduction in error of 3.19%–39.29% relative to comparative methods.The study indicates that the proposed conflict potential representation and the multi-expert adversarial training mechanism effectively capture the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of high-dynamic interactions in weaving sections significantly enhancing the robustness and interpretability of lane-changing predictions in complex scenarios.

title: A Review of Bandwidth Enhancement Techniques for Die-to-Die Single-Ended Interfaces in Chiplets
authors: Haitao You, Yixiang Cheng, Li Zhang, Xiaofang Gao, Xinyu Wu, Tianyi Zhang, Shizeng Zhang, Dimitar Nikolov, Qiang Cui, Juin J. Liou
abstract: With the rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) and high-performance computing (HPC), the demand for computational power is increasing exponentially. Traditional single-chip architectures can no longer meet the growing computational power requirements. The Chiplet architecture has emerged as one effective solution. However, this architecture faces several critical challenges. In particular, die-to-die (D2D) interface bandwidth is a critical bottleneck impacting the performance of the entire Chiplet architecture. This paper systematically summarizes and compares existing technologies aimed at enhancing the bandwidth of D2D single-ended interfaces, including signal modulation, and interface circuits for functional blocks such as drivers, continuous-time linear equalizers (CTLE), and decision feedback equalizers (DFE). This paper also provides valuable insights and guidance for selecting optimal signal modulation schemes and interface circuit architectures. Finally, forward-looking directions are proposed for bandwidth enhancement, such as co-design and the development of new electronic design automation (EDA) tools, to meet the rapidly growing demand for computational power.

title: Physics-Informed Transformer for Rolling Bearing RUL Prediction: A Dual-Stream Trend-Residual Decomposition Architecture
authors: Zehua Zhang, Ning Shen, Xingfen Wang
abstract: Bearing operating conditions are harsh and complex, with limited data available to effectively characterize bearing degradation processes during full-lifecycle condition monitoring. This makes it difficult to extract discriminative representations, severely impacting bearing remaining life prediction. Addressing the challenges of long lifecycles, subtle degradation features, and the physically inconsistent predictions in data-driven models for rolling bearings, this paper proposes a Physics-Informed Transformer (PIT) prediction framework. First, based on the physical properties of bearing degradation, a physics-informed feature selection criterion SC combining stationarity and correlation is constructed to screen high-confidence features. Second, a physics-data dual-stream residual architecture is designed. It explicitly models the macro-physical trends of degradation using a grey model GM(1,1) and replaces traditional recurrent networks with a Transformer encoder. Through a multi-head self-attention mechanism, it concurrently captures global long-term dependencies within complex data residuals. Most critically, a monotonicity-aware loss function was introduced, embedding the monotonicity regularization of degradation as soft constraints within the Transformer training process. Experiments demonstrate that this approach not only overcomes the vanishing gradient problem inherent in RNN-based models but also effectively eliminates non-physical fluctuations in prediction results, achieving an improved trade-off of accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability.

title: NPSVC++: A Representation Learning Framework for Nonparallel Classifiers_supp1-3661343.pdf
authors: Zhihui Lai
abstract: This article focuses on a specific family of classifiers called nonparallel support vector classifiers (NPSVCs). Different from typical classifiers, the training of an NPSVC involves the minimization of multiple objectives, resulting in the potential concerns of feature suboptimality and class dependence. Consequently, no effective learning scheme has been established to improve NPSVCs’ performance through representation learning, especially deep learning. To break this bottleneck, we develop NPSVC++ based on multiobjective optimization, enabling the seamless learning of NPSVC and its features. By pursuing Pareto optimality, NPSVC++ theoretically ensures feature optimality across different classes, thus effectively overcoming these two issues mentioned above. A general learning procedure via duality optimization is proposed, based on which we provide two applicable instances, K-NPSVC++ and D-NPSVC++. Moreover, the convergence analysis of the proposed algorithm is provided. The experiments show their superiority over the existing methods and verify the efficacy of NPSVC++.

title: PSA-1DCNN: Multimodal Biomarker and Text Integration for Lung Cancer Diagnosis
authors: Cyrille Yetuyetu Kesiku, Begonya Garcia-Zapirain
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Integrating multimodal data, such as unstructured clinical narratives and quantitative blood biomarkers, remains a major challenge in modern healthcare due to heterogeneous formats, complex semantics, and limited inter-modal correlations. Despite significant advances in deep learning, effective fusion of clinical text and biomarkers is still unresolved, restricting the full potential of precision medicine. We propose PSA-1DCNN, a novel Parallel Self-Attention 1D Convolutional Neural Network designed for multimodal integration in lung cancer detection. By combining self-attention mechanisms with 1D convolutional layers, PSA-1DCNN captures global semantic relationships from clinical text while learning local discriminative patterns from biomarker data. We further investigate four fusion strategies to optimize cross-modal information integration. Experiments conducted on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV demonstrate that PSA-1DCNN outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including ClinicalBERT, LSTM, and 1D-CNN. Our best-performing configuration achieves an F1-score of 98.4% on MIMIC-IV, with strong cross-version generalization to MIMIC-III. SHAP-based interpretability further highlights the clinical relevance of key biomarkers such as WBC and RBC, alongside critical textual features. This study presents a scalable and interpretable framework that bridges heterogeneous modalities, advancing precision oncology and offering promising opportunities for personalized diagnostics.

title: Modeling User Perception for Multi-Quality Tile-Based 360° Video Streaming
authors: Moatasim Mahmoud, Stamatia Rizou, Andreas S. Panayides, Nikolaos V. Kantartzis, George K. Karagiannidis, Pavlos I. Lazaridis, Zaharias D. Zaharis
abstract: As virtual reality (VR) and 360° video applications continue to gain traction, multi-quality tile-based 360° video streaming has emerged as a flexible and adaptive solution for delivering high-quality immersive content while optimizing bandwidth usage. However, the presence of mixed quality levels within a single 360° video frame can adversely affect users' perceived quality. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial quality variations across tiles and within users' viewports, and provide improved perceptual quality modeling for tiled 360° videos. To realize this goal, we introduce the Subjective Tile based Assessment for 360° Videos (STAV360) dataset, which includes six source 360° videos, each encoded using twelve tile based configurations, subjective ratings from 27 participants, and corresponding viewing trajectories. Based on this dataset, we propose novel tile-based and viewport-aware 360° video quality assessment (360° VQA) models that incorporate tile-level quality information and user interaction data. Experimental results show that our models outperform conventional full-frame methods by more accurately predicting perceived quality. This work provides key insights for designing perceptually-driven quality metrics and lays the foundation for more efficient and adaptive tile-based 360° video streaming strategies.

title: Design and Implementation of an Integrated Big Data Governance and Development Platform Based on DataOps Methodology
authors: Haixu Miao, Lei Zhang, Yang Li, Xinlin Li, Yanfang Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: This study addresses critical challenges in enterprise data management, including metadata silos requiring 3-5 days for asset inventory with <70% accuracy, severe lineage disconnection with 35-40% breakage rate, and governance-development separation characterized by 50% rule coverage and 7-10 days development cycles, by designing an integrated big data governance and development platform grounded in DataOps principles. The platform implements a four-layer architecture integrating data ingestion, metadata governance, development collaboration, and service application, featuring event-driven automation, a three-tier model-lineage bidirectional linkage algorithm achieving sub-500ms impact analysis, and an Abstract Syntax Tree-based field-level lineage tracking algorithm with 94.1% accuracy. Validation employs the TPC-H 100GB dataset comprising 358 lineage relationships across 110 tables in a Docker environment. Experimental results demonstrate 61% development cycle reduction, 87.5% manual configuration decrease, 85% automation rate, and 94.1% field-level lineage precision. The platform outperforms existing solutions in metadata query response, concurrent processing capacity, and functional completeness. This research provides enterprises with a validated technical solution for integrated data governance and development, substantially enhancing data engineering efficiency through DataOps methodology implementation.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Fog Resource Allocation for Dynamic Task Execution in Fog Environment
authors: Chanchal Ahlawat, Shivani Tufchi, Abhay Kumar Agrahari, Neha Yadav
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The efficient resource allocation is essential to minimizing latency and maximizing task success in fog environments, the rapid expansion of IoT applications has created significant challenges. This study investigates reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods for dynamic fog resource allocation. A custom fog environment was developed in OpenAI Gym, modeled as a 5×5 grid with fog nodes characterized by varying bandwidth (2–12 units) and processing speeds (2–12 units). Given both algorithms are trained for 5000 episodes using the learning rate of 0.2, discount factor 0.99 and e-greedy exploration from 1.0-0.05, the results show Q-Learning has a faster convergence that provides peak rewards close to 1500 by episode 5000. However, Q-Learning has unstable with accumulating rewards close to 3100-4100, whereas SARSAs learning is more stable. Initially, Q-Learning has faster improvements but SARSA has better task completion rates in critical applications. The heatmaps verify that Q-Learning tends to make inclusive allocations on high-capacity nodes compared to SARSA, which has more balanced policies on resources for better steady-state allocations. Therefore, Q- Learning is essential to maximize efficiency but is also extremely unstable. Meanwhile, SARSA is less optimal for maximizing efficiency but more stable hence more suitable for critical fog applications.

title: Dynamic Allocation Mechanism and Optimization of Resources for High-Orbit Mobile Communication Satellite Systems
authors: Jun Zheng, Ligang Fei, Shuming Yin, Qiang Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: High-orbit mobile communication satellite systems, characterized by wide-area coverage, have become a core component of China’s "space-air-ground integrated" communication network. Currently, such systems universally face three critical contradictions: the rigidity of power supply versus the dynamics of service demands, the fixity of beam coverage versus the sparsity of user distribution, and the limitation of frequency bandwidth versus the growth of service data rates. Consequently, resource allocation efficiency has emerged as a key bottleneck constraining system performance. Taking Tiantong-1 Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellites as the research platform, this paper proposes a universal dynamic allocation framework featuring "three-dimensional resource linkage, satellite-ground collaborative decision-making, and priority-oriented adaptation". Centered on three core technical approaches—power pooling allocation, dynamic beam aggregation control, and intelligent frequency reuse—this framework integrates a multi-dimensional priority system encompassing service urgency, regional importance, and channel quality, and establishes a closed-loop mechanism of "demand perception - resource optimization - execution feedback". Verification based on actual operational data of Tiantong-1 satellites demonstrates that the proposed framework reduces the blocking rate of high-priority services by 38.2%, improves the comprehensive resource utilization rate by 25.7%, and enhances the communication quality qualification rate in edge areas by 42.3%. This work provides theoretical support and engineering references for the resource management design of China’s subsequent high-orbit satellite systems.

title: A Hybrid Deep Reinforcement Learning and Graph Neural Network Approach for Multi-objective Waste Transportation Optimization
authors: Like Gao, Wendong Xing
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: With the increasing amount of urban waste generated, optimizing and scheduling waste transportation routes has become a major challenge in the development of smart cities. This paper proposes a method based on combining deep reinforcement learning (PPO) with graph neural networks (GNNs) to address the path planning and scheduling problem in urban waste transportation. By combining PPO with GNNs, this study is able to optimize the transportation routes and vehicle scheduling strategies for multiple types of waste within a multi-objective optimization framework that considers transportation costs, carbon emissions, and computational efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms traditional integer programming (IP), genetic algorithm (GA), and particle swarm optimization (PSO) methods in terms of transportation cost and carbon emissions control, while maintaining a stable computational time growth and exhibiting strong scalability and robustness. This study provides an innovative solution to the waste transportation scheduling problem in smart cities and has important practical implications for future green logistics and sustainable urban development.

title: Towards Personalized News Recommendation Using Collaborative Filtering and Multi-Armed Bandits (MABs)
authors: J. Alamelu Mangai, Zafar Ali Khan, Sterlin Minish T. N, Alvin Nishant
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: In the digital landscape of news consumption, recommendation systems are pivotal in guiding users through the vast array of available stories. These systems utilize complex algorithms to analyze user preferences, offering personalized news suggestions and simplifying the process of discovering relevant information. This study consists of two phases. The first phase involves evaluating traditional recommendation systems using User-Based Collaborative Filtering with a provided dataset. Subsequently, the top 10 recommended articles from phase 1 are analyzed using multi-armed bandit (MAB) algorithms, including Epsilon-Greedy, Softmax, Thompson Sampling, and UCB. The objective is to personalize the recommendation system and determine optimal display times for each news article. Through this approach, leveraging advanced algorithms and balancing exploration and exploitation, the study aims to optimize user engagement and satisfaction in the dynamic digital news consumption landscape. The integration of user-based collab-orative filtering with Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) algorithms proved highly effective. Among MAB algorithms, softmax and epsilon-greedy consistently delivered low regret and high rewards for users. Their success lies in efficiently ex-ploiting articles with high click-through rates (CTR), enhancing user engagement. Moreover, MAB algorithms allowed personalized display times for articles, optimizing rewards and minimizing regrets.

title: Integrating Machine Learning and XAI for Interpretable Diagnosis of Cancer, Pneumonia, and Diabetes
authors: Ritika Salimath, Diana Olivia
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: Machine Learning algorithms play a massive role in healthcare as their integration enables early diagnosis and informed decision-making. But people are skeptical about its adoption due to its black box nature leading to a lack of interpretability. This study addresses this issue by combining traditional ML algorithms with Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to help predict three medical conditionscancer, diabetes and pneumonia. This is done using a publicly available Kaggle dataset. Various classification models were trained and evaluated, namely Logistic Regression, SVM, KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, LightGBM and stacked model. The stacked model was found to give the best performance among them and it was further optimized using Optuna, achieving an accuracy of 82%. Several XAI techniques such as SHAP, LIME, ELI5 and Interpret-Community were implemented to help in interpretability. SHAP delivered global feature importance, and LIME provided local explanations for single predictions. ELI5 and Interpret-Community added information through permutation-based scoring and model introspection. The research illustrates how the incorporation of XAI with predictive modeling improves interpretability without impacting performance. This not only enhances model reliability within clinical environments but also builds trust, providing an important step towards explainable and responsible AI in healthcare.

title: Vayupragna (Air Intelligence)- High-risk predictive health outcomes of air pollution exposure
authors: Aman Verma, Lavanya Dhiman, Tarun Yadav, Akshat Tyagi, Vikas Chauhan
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: A predictive modeling strategy of health risks caused by exposure to air pollution in Indian metropolitan population taking the form of two components is used to mitigate some of the traditional air quality management failures in the application of the latest artificial intelligence frameworks, e.g. LSTM and RF, so that high-resolution spatio-temporal/data forecasts on the key pollutants like PM 2.5, PM 10, and NO 2 can be obtained. The model relies on an elaborated ground, satellite, and meteorological data. The approach is expanded to estimate pollutant concentration using epidemiological exposure-response functions to quantify excess morbidity and mortality risk, enabling the building of a health risk-based Air Quality Index. This metric helps public health and policymakers make decisions by addressing the communication gap in standard AQI assessments. Stringent testing improves Indian city prediction and contextual applicability. The multidisciplinary framework emphasizes strength of machine learning in environmental health to enable real-time pollution management and public protection decisions.

title: Automatic Term Extraction in Low-Resource Languages and Technical Domains Using Multilingual Models
authors: Yermek Alimzhanov, Aigerim Mansurova
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: Automatic term extraction using deep learning models has shown promising results when sufficient training data is available. Unfortunately, in certain emerging domains and low-resource languages such data may be lacking, leading to reduced performance due to insufficient fine-tuning of these models. To address this issue, in this study we propose a multilingual transformer-based sequence labeling model for term extraction, trained on an original dataset in English and Kazakh. Our evaluation results demonstrate that the multilingual XLM-RoBERTa model achieves improvements in automatic term extraction compared to a model with BERT-BiLSTM-CRF architecture trained on limited training data. Although the degree of improvement varies across domains, languages and dataset sizes, our evaluation shows that in Kazakh subsets of the dataset the highest F1-score improvement reaches 20 % in the Blockchain domain, while in the domain of Materials Science the improvement amounts to 12.9 %. Furthermore, our multilingual model attains competitive results when compared to a model with popular and highly effective architecture trained on a small amount of training data.

title: X-Band Single-Layer Reflectarray Antenna for Harmonic Radar
authors: Huanjian Luo, Guofu Wang, Mimi Qin, Xiaobo Jia, Yecheng Li, Yansheng Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: The paper introduces a X-band single-layer reflectarray antenna for harmonic radar based on 0.581 × 0.581 unit cells. A novel Malta patch element achieves reflection phase is tuned about 360 ° by varying the radius. A 15 × 15 elements prototype yields 26 dB gain, 13 % 1 dB gain bandwidths, sidelobe below -16 dB and cross-polarization below -40 dB.

title: UniKD: Unified Cross-Architecture Knowledge Distillation with Warm-Start Scheduling for Real-Time DETR Models
authors: Hongye Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning, Computational Intelligence and Pattern Recognition (MLCIPR)
abstract: Real-time object detection often relies on compact Transformer detectors, yet compressing DETR-style models remains challenging. We present UniKD, a unified cross-architecture distillation framework for real-time DETR that employs a two-stage schedule. In the Warm-Start phase, the student is trained solely on detection losses to form stable self-features. In the Ramp-Start phase, distillation is introduced progressively—from higher-level encoder features to lower-level ones—while linearly increasing the distillation weight, mitigating feature mismatch and instability. A modular distill pipeline enables encoder-level FGD together with decoder-level supervision: teacher decoder predictions (logits/boxes/aux) are cached into the student’s outputs for loss computation under a frozen teacher; an optional teacher-forward mode supports decoder-only distillation; and two lightweight losses, LogitKD (temperature-scaled KL) and BoxKD (query-aligned L1 with optional GIoU), handle query-length mismatches. Experiments on COCO show consistent gains: +2.4 AP for a DEIM-DFINE Nano student distilled from DEIM-DFINE Small, +1.7 AP for DEIMv2-Atto from DEIMv2-Pico, and +3.0 AP using DINOv3-S as teacher for DEIMv2-Atto. These results indicate that UniKD improves both accuracy and convergence of compact DETR models with minimal overhead.Our code are available at https://github.com/Ou7r3/UniKD.

title: A Stacked Transfer Learning Approach for Accurate Classification of Soil-Transmitted Helminth Eggs Using YOLOv8 and ResNet50
authors: Ivan Miguel Leopoldo, Jerson Paul Leones
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Communication Technology and Information Technology (ICCTIT)
abstract: Soil-transmitted helminthiasis (STH) is a significant public health issue in the Philippines, with prevalence rates up to 94.4% for certain parasites. Current diagnostics rely on manual microscopy, often leading to misdiagnosis due to similarities between helminth eggs. This thesis proposes a deep learning approach for accurate classification of parasitic ova. A stacked transfer learning model is developed to identify three species: Ascaris lumbricoides, Trichuris trichiura, and hookworm. Microscope images undergo specialized preprocessing before being analyzed by a hybrid architecture combining YOLOv8 for detection and ResNet50 for classification, both adapted for parasite recognition. The model is trained and evaluated using a publicly available dataset from IEEE DataPort and compared with conventional convolutional neural networks and one-versus-all logistic regression classifiers. Performance is measured using precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score for each class. This work aims to improve diagnostic accuracy for STH infections, offering an automated tool that can enhance detection capabilities, especially in resource-limited tropical settings.

title: Comparison of Parallel and Iterative CORDIC Architectures for Sine and Cosine Computation on FPGA
authors: Salim Hamouda, Abderrahim Hamli, Samir Hamdani, Hamid Khelfi
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference of Advanced Technology in Electronic and Electrical Engineering (ICATEEE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive exploration of the iterative and parallel architectures of the CORDIC (Coordinate Rotation Digital Computer) algorithm implemented on an FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array) platform. The study focuses on the adaptability of CORDIC for the computation of cosine and sine values, offering versatile solutions for trigonometric calculations. In the iterative approach, the algorithm performs accurate angle calculations over multiple iterations, while the parallel architecture operates in real-time, eliminating the need for clock synchronization. The research demonstrates the efficacy of CORDIC in FPGA-based applications, providing high-precision sine and cosine computation. This work delivers valuable insights to engineers and researchers seeking efficient and accurate solutions for trigonometric calculations in various domains, showcasing the potential of CORDIC in practical applications.

title: Real-Time Battery SOC Estimation via a Quantized Neural Network on a RISC-V SoC
authors: Fathima Sana P, Raghu C. V.
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: This paper introduces a complete, end-to-end system for estimating battery State of Charge (SOC) in real-time on a resource-constrained embedded platform. Traditional methods for SOC estimation often struggle with accumulating errors, while modern model-based approaches can be too computationally demanding for small devices. To address this, we developed a lightweight feedforward neural network trained on a rich dataset of Li-ion battery behavior across various operating conditions. For deployment on our target hardware, the model was quantized to 8-bit integers and executed using a custom, bare-metal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C$</tex> inference engine. The system is hosted on a VexRiscv softcore processor, synthesized on an Arty Z7 FPGA, showcasing a flexible, open-source pathway for creating application-specific systems. Our experimental results, under dynamic load conditions, demonstrate a responsive and accurate real-time SOC estimator with a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 1.2. The entire firmware, including the model and drivers, has a minimal memory footprint of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 6 ~ K i B}$</tex>. This work offers a validated blueprint for deploying custom machine learning models on RISC-V based Systems-on-Chip (SoC), presenting a practical approach for building efficient and scalable Battery Management Systems (BMS).

title: Wolverine Optimization Algorithm: a New Naturederived Algorithm for Network Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Ananta Chattpadhyay, Srijita Baksi, Partha Ghosh
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: As cyber threats rise in today's connected world, securing computer networks has become more crucial than ever. Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) act as early warning mechanisms by monitoring activity, analyzing traffic, and detecting unauthorized access. IDS can be Host-based (HIDS), tracking system-level changes, or Network-based (NIDS), observing network traffic. NIDS commonly uses signaturebased detection for known threats and anomaly-based detection for unusual patterns, though the latter may trigger false alarms. A major challenge lies in processing massive traffic data: too many features slow the system, while irrelevant ones reduce accuracy. Feature Selection(FS) addresses this by identifying the most relevant features, improving efficiency and accuracy. Many FS techniques have been developed, including PCA, GA, PSO, ACO, and MCSS, often combined with machine learning. However, these methods face limitations such as slow processing and poor adaptability to evolving attacks. To overcome this, the authors propose the Wolverine Optimization Algorithm (WOA), a bio-inspired FS method that strategically selects essential features. Tested on benchmark datasets like NSLKDD, WOA outperforms many traditional FS techniques by enhancing detection accuracy, reducing false alarms, and optimizing resource usage, making it effective for real-time NIDS in complex networks.

title: Semantic Segmentation of Aerial Imagery Using Transfer Learning and Blockchain
authors: Satabdee Mahapatra, Pranati Mishra, Ranjan Kumar Dash, Saswati Soumya Tripathy, Bikash Chandra Naik, Jyotirmayee Rautaray
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Semantic aerial imagery segmentation is of great importance in land use classification, urban planning and disaster estimation. Its capability is compromised by limitations such as a lack of enough labeled data and data authenticity and security issues. In this paper, we present a novel framework that combines transfer learning with blockchain technology to break such limitations. Transfer learning enables the usage of pretrained deep neural networks that reduce the necessity for large sets of labeled data without sacrificing segmentation performance. For integrity, traceability, and secure sharing of imagery information and segmentation outputs, a blockchain infrastructure is implemented in the framework. The distributed database securely retains model output and metadata, enabling multiple stakeholders to collaborate without jeopardizing data privacy. Experimental validation verifies that the proposed method performs segmentation best with enhanced training efficiency and secure data management. Through the combination of the latest machine learning and distributed technology, the framework offers a flexible and reliable solution for geospatial analysis, particularly in reliable data-critical applications like disaster management and smart city planning.

title: Integrating Physiological Signals into Music Machine Learning: An HRV-Based Framework
authors: Uhma Ponnusamy, B Geethanjali, Suhana Thanseen A F
publish: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference for Women in Computing (InCoWoCo)
abstract: Music is widely being recognized as a non-pharmacological intervention for anxiety, yet its physiological mechanisms remain unexplored. This study examines the effect of Kaapi raga (a south Indian classical music) and weightless ambient music on cardiovascular regulation and anxiety reduction using heart rate variability (HRV) and mean arterial pressure (MAP). Twenty participants with high anxiety (HAM-A >25) completed a protocol involving rest, music listening, and attention tasks while ECG and MAP were continuously recorded.HRV features were extracted across time, frequency, and nonlinear domains and analysed using machine learning classifiers. Random Forest and XGBoost achieved the highest performance (70.8% accuracy; AUC up to 0.89), outperforming linear models. Feature ablation revealed non-linear SD2, pNN50, HR mean, and high-frequency power percentage as key predictors of music-induced physiological changes.The findings here demonstrate that ensemble machine learning methods can robustly model music’s impact on anxiety, while domain-specific HRV features provide actionable biomarkers for developing personalized, evidence-based music therapy interventions.

title: An Automatic Annotation Method for Defective Cigarette Package Appearance Images Based on the Improved YOLOv8n Model
authors: Bo Zhang, Tao Liu, Xin An Yang, Cui Zhu Qian, Fang Fang Niu, Wen Kui Zhu
publish: 2025 8th International Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Artificial Intelligence (ACAI)
abstract: The quality of cigarette outer packaging is a critical determinant influencing consumers' purchasing decisions; consequently, efficient and accurate detection of packaging defects on the production line is of paramount importance. Deep learning methods have demonstrated considerable promise in this domain; however, their reliance on large quantities of manually annotated data leads to substantial labor costs and limited processing efficiency. To address this limitation, this paper proposes an automatic annotation framework based on an enhanced YOLOv8n (You Only Look Once v8 Nano) architecture. The core innovation centers on the integration of the ASC module, which significantly strengthens the model s capacity to capture discriminative features of complex defects through the synergistic combination of multi-scale dilated convolutions and an attention mechanism. Experimental evaluations conducted on a self-constructed dataset of cigarette small-pack defects reveal that the improved model achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 95.9%, representing a 1.5 percentage point improvement over the baseline YOLOv8n. Moreover, the proposed method enables direct generation of high-quality bounding boxes and corresponding label files for unannotated images, thereby facilitating end-to-end automatic annotation. This study demonstrates that the proposed approach not only enhances recognition accuracy but also substantially improves annotation efficiency, offering a robust and scalable solution for industrial surface quality inspection systems.

title: An Intelligent IoT-Enabled Entry Gateway for Contactless Health and Safety Screening
authors: Vijay M, G.Balaji, P.Pavani Durga, Dasa Lahari, Anabala Geetha, N Nandhini
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic stressed that there is an urgent necessity to implement automated screening solutions with a low human contact level that allow the preservation of accuracy and efficiency. This paper introduces a Smart Gateway Entry System which is a combination of Internet of Things (IoT) devices and computer vision models built on deep learning to conduct full health and safety inspections. The system is a composite of a variety of subsystems, such as face mask and face shield detection, QR-code-based vaccination verification, temperature sensor, and automated sanitization. Mask and shield were detected using a hybrid deep learning model that was constructed by combining MobileNetV2 and VGG19 with detection accuracies of 97 and 99, respectively. The MLX90614 infrared sensor ( ±0.5 o C accuracy) was used to monitor the temperature and it was logged into a live Firebase database. The graphical interface of the system created with the help of Tkinter allows the system to interact and keep records with ease. The findings of the experiments support the hypothesis that the suggested gateway can screen people in real-time, without contacts, and with high accuracy and is a valuable solution to safer entry management in the social and work setting.

title: Examining the Factors Influencing Generative AI's Impact on Quantum Cybersecurity: A Thematic Analysis
authors: Hussein A. Al-Hashimi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Modeling, Simulation &amp; Intelligent Computing (MoSICom)
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative AI and its integration with quantum cybersecurity present significant opportunities and new problems. While generative AI can improve security in quantum computing, it also brings new risks and uncertainties. Discovering the driving forces or constraints of using generative <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> in this application domain is essential to devising effective mechanisms for harnessing its possibilities while minimizing the risks. The main objectives of this paper are to perform a thematic analysis on several primary motivators and demotivators driving the adoption and impact of generative AI in quantum cybersecurity. The study found several key motivators, such as the potential for new predictive capabilities, the automation of threat detection, and the strengthening of quantum encryption protocols. The sources of demotivation included suspicion about the dependability of AI models in a super complex quantum world, ethical problems of the autonomous decision-making AI, and node computational power to integrate AI into quantum. These actors are identified to play an essential role in the feasibility and acceptability of generative AI for quantum cybersecurity.

title: Research on Constructing Recommendation Algorithms Based on Graph Neural Networks
authors: Shuang He, Liang Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Machine Learning, Computational Intelligence and Pattern Recognition (MLCIPR)
abstract: The rapid advancement of intelligent algorithms has opened new avenues for optimizing recommendation systems. Addressing the limitations of traditional models in capturing higher-order associations and sparsity issues, this study proposes a recommendation algorithm model based on graph neural networks. Centered on the user-item interaction graph, the model employs multi-layer feature aggregation and attention-weighted mechanisms to achieve high-dimensional node embeddings. Building upon the LightGCN framework, it incorporates a residual propagation structure to enhance stability. Experimental results demonstrate that on the MovieLens-1M dataset, Precision@10 improves to 0.348, representing a 20.4% increase over the NCF model. On the Amazon-Books dataset, Recall@20 reaches 0.226, validating the model's superior performance in sparse scenarios.

title: Sustainable Mobile Apps Development to Optimize Performance through Power Management and Battery Consumption
authors: B.P. Joshi, Venkata Suresh Babu Chilluri, Shweta Goyal
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: As they continue to develop and add new features, modern smartphones have become essential for a large number of individuals worldwide. Yet, the speed at which smartphones have developed in recent years has outpaced battery capacity, resulting in rapid battery drain and the necessity for customers substitute and dispose them regularly. The main cause of these incredibly low rates is programmers' unfamiliarity with the tools, processes, software, and systems needed to analyse this important performance parameter. The necessity for increased understanding and integration of energy-efficient practices in application development is underscored by this knowledge gap. By using AI algorithms to forecast battery usage and optimise smartphone energy use, this work tackles this difficulty. An integrated tool in Android Studio, the Android profiler is intended to track application performance, including power consumption monitoring. More precise measurements at the system or code level are frequently made possible by software-based approaches. Metrics that gauge the software's energy usage are necessary for the creation of energy-efficient software. Within five minutes of doing the initial test, Apptim provides the performance findings. In summary, this study examined how much energy an Android application uses. Measurements of CPU, memory, and energy consumption were collected and examined from the test runs. To learn more about Android developers' perspectives on power consumption control, we intend to survey them in future work. Additionally, we intend to provide automated methods to assist developers in identifying, locating, and resolving issues related to power use. In addition, we wish to integrate issue tracker data to analyse power consumption commitments and contrast energy commits to other commit classes.

title: Automated Mock Interview Assessment Through Speech Emotion Detection and Semantic Analysis
authors: Dipali Dakhole, Prabhat Shankar, Kasturi Sangale, Mayank Saha, Vanisha Agrawal
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: Rapid advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) are accelerating the shift toward sustainable automation within Industry 4.0 and smart digital ecosystems. In this context, efficient, low-resource AI systems play a crucial role in enabling scalable and environmentally responsible technological solutions. This study introduces Intervalyze, an AI-powered mock interview evaluation system designed as a lightweight and energy-efficient web application built using Flask. Integrating speech analysis, emotion detection, semantic similarity, and automated feedback generation, the system contributes to green computing practices by optimizing computational workloads while maintaining high performance. Intervalyze incorporates an enhanced Wav2Vec2 model trained on the TESS dataset for emotion recognition across seven categories fear, anger, disgust, neutral, sad, happy, and pleasant surprise alongside a BERT-based semantic similarity module that evaluates response relevance using cosine similarity. Audio inputs are transcribed via a pre-trained Wav2Vec2ForCTC model, enabling fully automated, low-power processing suitable for scalable Industry 4.0 and robotics process applications. The system generates transcriptions, emotional states, similarity scores, and personalized feedback, supporting sustainable automation in digital training environments. Experimental results demonstrate strong model performance, achieving a 93.94% F1 score, 95.46% precision, and 94.11% accuracy and recall. The proposed solution performed better over baseline K-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) model, and highlights the potential of energy-efficient AI-driven platforms to support smart, automated, and resource-conscious digital systems aligned with the goals of sustainable engineering and automation.

title: Optimizing Disaster Response in 5G Cellular Systems Through Data Compression
authors: Poulomi Mukherjee, Aishwarya Mandal, Babul P. Tewari
publish: 2025 OITS International Conference on Information Technology (OCIT)
abstract: Energy-efficient network access service is one of the major concerns of disaster-response facilities. The main constraint of such a service requirement is the limitation of power backup for the battery-operated devices. The present manuscript addresses a framework for optimized disaster response network access service using Device-to-Device (D2D) multicasting, considering strategic application of DBSCAN algorithm and data compression technique. We have incorporated the DBSCAN algorithm for appropriate cluster head (CH) selection together with cluster members receiving content from the transmitting CH. Additionally, the usage of data compression has been integrated, which suitably saves the energy consumption of the devices, particularly when BS failure occurs. The proposed methodology leads to considerable energy saving with appropriate QoS, leading to seamless connectivity under the control of battery-operated devices. Simulation results with a comparative study establish that our approach results in a considerable energy saving and reduction in data transmission time.

title: IntuitIQ - AI Driven Calculator
authors: Himesh Bhattacharjee, Debolina Saha, Arun Roy, Priyanka Bal, Koushik Sarkar, Sudipta Banerjee, Kanhaiya Sharma
publish: 2025 Modern Electronics Devices and Intelligent Communication Systems (MEDCOM)
abstract: This work proposes an AI Enabled Platform that uses Generative AI Models which is expected to solve mathematics, abstract question & diagrams. The platform guarantees that sophisticated natural language processing gives the user the right response to a query correctly timed. It also features a dynamic interface where users can describe the mathematical and logical problems through drawings or in words, and detailed step-by-step solutions will be generated accordingly. The platform employs an Optical Character Recognition, handwritten text, rough drawings and images parsing together with the textual judging. This makes it useful for the general public, independent of domains such as engineering and education. We can bring on educative problem-solving activities within the reach of a learner through this project, enhancing technology and user-level education. The goal of the project aims at bringing a solution to users who have difficulty accessing complex mathematical structures and who are willing to build Artificial Intelligence education software alongside this project.

title: A SiGe BiCMOS K-Band LNA with 1.5 dB Noise Figure for LEO SATCOM Terminals
authors: B. Martineau, B. Blampey, G. Yaakoubi, A. Siligaris, P. Garcia, D. Belot
publish: 2026 IEEE Space Hardware and Radio Conference (SHaRC)
abstract: This paper presents a K-band Low Noise Amplifier (LNA) targeting user terminals for low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite communication systems. The circuit, fabricated in STMicroelectronics' BiCMOS55X SiGe technology, achieves a measured peak gain of 15 dB over a 3-dB bandwidth ranging from 17.7 to 23.1 GHz. A minimum noise figure of 1.5 dB is recorded, with an input 1-dB compression point of −11 dBm. The amplifier operates from a 2.4 V supply and draws only 10 mA of current, demonstrating an effective trade-off between noise performance and power efficiency. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this work achieves the lowest reported noise figure among published K-band SiGe LNAs. The core circuit occupies a compact area of just 0.11 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, excluding pads, making it well-suited for dense front-end integration in next-generation SATCOM user terminals.
